## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define the term "Safety" as applied to robotic systems. State the formal ISO definition.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Safety, in the context of robotic systems, is defined under **ISO 12100** and **IEC 61508** as the **freedom from unacceptable risk**. A robotic system is not considered safe merely because it is *risk-free* (which is practically unattainable), but because its **residual risk** has been reduced to a level deemed **tolerable** or **ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)** through systematic design, engineering controls, and administrative procedures applied across the system's entire life-cycle. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*List any three categories of hazards associated with industrial robotic systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three principal categories of hazards in industrial robotic systems, as classified by the KTU 2024 syllabus, are:
1. **Mechanical Hazards** — Crushing, shearing, cutting, entanglement, and piercing hazards from the robot's moving parts, end-effector, or workpiece. **(1 mark)**
2. **Non-Mechanical Hazards** — Electrical shock, fire, explosion, thermal burns, chemical exposure, and radiation. **(1 mark)**
3. **Systemic / Software Hazards** — Erratic behaviour arising from software faults, sensor miscalibration, communication dropouts, or cybersecurity breaches. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** *Explain the role of an engineer in ensuring the safety of a robotic system across its design, installation, operation, and decommissioning phases. Discuss at least four distinct layers of engineer responsibility.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A robotic cell has a dangerous failure rate of $\lambda_D = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour. The proof-test interval is $T_i = 4380$ hours (6 months). Compute the average Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) and identify the corresponding Safety Integrity Level (SIL) as per IEC 61508.* **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 Marks

The engineer's responsibility for robotic safety is a **multi-layered, life-cycle commitment** that cannot be delegated or reduced to a single task. The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes **five principal layers** of responsibility:

**Layer 1 — The Designer-Engineer (Front-End Safety).** The design engineer embeds safety *into the machine itself* through **inherent safe design** (ISO 12100), the **Defence-in-Depth (DiD)** strategy, and the selection of components with appropriate **Safety Integrity Levels (SIL)** or **Performance Levels (PL)**. The designer conducts **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** and **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** to anticipate failures *before* they occur. **(2 marks)**

**Layer 2 — The Installation & Commissioning Engineer.** This engineer performs a **site-specific risk assessment** before the robot is energized, validates the **safeguarded space** per ISO 10218-2, and verifies all **Emergency Stop (E-Stop)** circuits, **safety light curtains**, and **interlocks**. A formal **commissioning sign-off** is generated. **(1.5 marks)**

**Layer 3 — The Operations & Maintenance Engineer.** This engineer implements **Lockout-Tagout (LOTO)**, conducts **periodic functional safety audits**, and maintains the **safety case dossier** — a living document proving the system remains safe throughout its operational life. **(1.5 marks)**

**Layer 4 — The Manager / Decision-Maker.** The manager fosters a **safety culture** where any worker can halt an unsafe machine without retribution, allocates budget for safety upgrades, and personally signs off on **risk acceptance** decisions, carrying legal liability for those decisions. **(1 mark)**

**Layer 5 — The Citizen-Engineer (Ethical Voice).** Beyond technical and managerial duties, the engineer serves as an **ethical voice** in society, upholding professional codes of ethics, refusing to compromise public welfare for profit or schedule, and reporting unsafe designs to regulators when internal channels fail. **(1 mark)**

---

##### Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Dangerous failure rate: $\lambda_D = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ failures per hour.
- Proof-test interval: $T_i = 4380$ hours.

**Formula (KTU 2024 Module 1 — IEC 61508 single-channel, low-demand mode):**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_i}{2}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{(8 \times 10^{-6}) \times (4380)}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator.**

$$\text{Numerator} = 8 \times 10^{-6} \times 4380 = 35040 \times 10^{-6} = 3.504 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 3 — Divide by 2.**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{3.504 \times 10^{-2}}{2} = 1.752 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the SIL table (IEC 61508, low-demand mode).**

| SIL | PFD Range |
|:---:|:---:|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$ |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-2}$ |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$ |

The computed value is $1.752 \times 10^{-2}$, which satisfies:

$$10^{-2} \leq 1.752 \times 10^{-2} < 10^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — State the conclusion.**
The safety function **operates at SIL 1**. **(7 marks total: 2 marks for formula, 2 marks for substitution and arithmetic, 2 marks for SIL comparison, 1 mark for the final conclusion.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted] — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** *With the aid of a labelled diagram, describe the Hierarchy of Controls (ISO 12100) as applied to robotic safety. Explain why PPE is considered the last resort.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A collaborative robot (cobot) operates at a measured dangerous failure rate of $\lambda_D = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ failures/hour. The proof-test interval is $T_i = 8760$ hours. Compute the PFD and the Risk Reduction Factor (RRF). Comment on whether this safety function is suitable for a collaborative workspace where human contact is expected.* **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 Marks

**Diagram (drawn by student, expected to include the following hierarchy):**
The **Hierarchy of Controls (ISO 12100)** is conventionally depicted as an **inverted pyramid** with five descending levels:

1. **Eliminate** (top, most effective) — Remove the hazard entirely. *Example:* Design the process so that the robot never needs to enter the human workspace.
2. **Substitute** — Replace the hazardous agent or process with a less dangerous one. *Example:* Use a water-jet cutter instead of a laser cutter, eliminating the laser radiation hazard.
3. **Engineering Controls** — Isolate people from the hazard by machine design. *Example:* Safety light curtains, interlocked guards, safety-rated monitored stops.
4. **Administrative Controls** — Change work procedures. *Example:* Training, signage, job-rotation, restricted-access zones.
5. **PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)** (bottom, least effective) — Protect the worker with personal gear. *Example:* Cut-resistant gloves, safety glasses, arc-flash suits. **(4 marks for the diagram and labelled hierarchy)**

**Why PPE is the last resort:**
PPE is placed at the bottom of the hierarchy because it **acts on the human, not on the machine**. Unlike engineering controls, PPE does not eliminate or reduce the hazard itself — it merely creates a *barrier* between the hazard and the worker. This means:
- PPE can **fail** (gloves tear, glasses fall off).
- PPE relies on **human compliance** (workers may forget, misuse, or refuse to wear it).
- PPE protects only the **individual wearing it**, not bystanders.
- PPE does **nothing** to make the machine itself safer — a poorly designed machine remains a poorly designed machine.

In contrast, **Eliminate** and **Substitute** remove the hazard at its source, and **Engineering Controls** build safety into the machine architecture. The KTU 2024 syllabus is explicit: a safety engineer should *exhaust* the upper levels of the hierarchy before resorting to PPE. **(3 marks for the explanation)**

---

##### Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- $\lambda_D = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ failures/hour.
- $T_i = 8760$ hours.

**Step 1 — Compute the PFD using the standard formula.**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_i}{2}$$

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-5}) \times (8760)}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator calculation.**

$$\text{Numerator} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \times 8760 = 17520 \times 10^{-5} = 1.752 \times 10^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Division by 2.**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1.752 \times 10^{-1}}{2} = 8.76 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Risk Reduction Factor.**
The RRF is defined as the reciprocal of the PFD:

$$\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{1}{8.76 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 11.42$$

**Step 5 — Determine the SIL by comparison.**

$$10^{-2} \leq 8.76 \times 10^{-2} < 10^{-1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SIL 1}$$

**Step 6 — Critical Commentary for the Cobot Workspace.**
A Risk Reduction Factor of only $\approx 11$ and a SIL 1 classification are **inadequate** for a collaborative workspace where direct human-robot contact is expected. **ISO/TS 15066** and **ISO 10218-1/2** require that safety functions protecting humans in collaborative operations meet at least **SIL 2** (PFD in $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$), and many safety analysts argue for **SIL 3** (PFD in $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$) for high-risk contact scenarios.

The engineer would therefore need to:
- **Halve the dangerous failure rate** by upgrading to higher-quality components, **or**
- **Quarter the proof-test interval** to $T_i \approx 2190$ hours, **or**
- **Introduce a redundant (dual-channel) safety architecture** so that the system tolerates a single dangerous failure (1002 or 1oo2 voting), thereby reducing the effective PFD by roughly an order of magnitude.

Only after such upgrades would the safety function be deemed **suitable for collaborative operation**. **(7 marks: 2 marks for PFD arithmetic, 1 mark for RRF, 1 mark for SIL identification, 3 marks for the critical commentary.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the PFD formula. Students often write $\text{PFD} = \lambda_D \cdot T_i$ instead of $\dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T_i}{2}$. This is an automatic **2-mark deduction** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 2. **Unit errors.** $\lambda_D$ must be in *failures per hour* and $T_i$ in *hours* for the PFD to be dimensionless. Mixing SI prefixes incorrectly (e.g., using $T_i = 8760$ hours as "$8760$ seconds") is a silent trap.
> 3. **Confusing PFD with reliability $R(t)$.** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ and $\text{PFD} = \lambda_D T_i / 2$ are *different* concepts. PFD is the probability that a *safety function* fails on demand; $R(t)$ is the probability that a *component* survives to time $t$.
> 4. **Omitting the ALARP step.** A full KTU 14-mark answer on "Need for Safety" **must** conclude with the ALARP argument, not merely the risk score.
> 5. **Treating safety as a "feature" rather than a "process."** A common student error is to describe a single safeguard (e.g., "add a light curtain") as the *complete* answer. The KTU 2024 evaluator expects the **process** — hazard identification → risk estimation → control application → re-estimation → ALARP verification.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1 — "Need for Safety and the Role of Engineers"**

- **Safety** = *Freedom from unacceptable risk* (ISO 12100 / IEC 61508). Not the same as "zero risk."
- **Risk** $R = P \times S$, where $P$ = probability, $S$ = severity. The product form is the KTU 2024 primitive model.
- **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable. The formal acceptance criterion.
- **Three hazard categories:** Mechanical, Non-Mechanical, Systemic/Software.
- **Five pillars of the "Need for Safety":** Humanitarian, Economic, Legal, Operational (reliability-coupling), Strategic.
- **Five layers of engineer responsibility:** Designer, Installation/Commissioning, Operations/Maintenance, Manager/Decision-Maker, Citizen-Engineer.
- **Hierarchy of Controls (ISO 12100):** Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering Controls → Administrative Controls → PPE.
- **Heinrich Pyramid:** Address 30,000 unsafe conditions to prevent 1 fatality.
- **MTBF** $= 1/\lambda$ (for constant failure rate).
- **Reliability function** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
- **Unreliability** $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$.
- **Average PFD** for a single-channel low-demand system: $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \lambda_D T_i / 2$.
- **RRF** $= 1/\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$.
- **SIL 1:** $10^{-2} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$, RRF 10–100.
- **SIL 2:** $10^{-3} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-2}$, RRF 100–1,000.
- **SIL 3:** $10^{-4} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$, RRF 1,000–10,000.
- **SIL 4:** $10^{-5} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-4}$, RRF 10,000–100,000.
- **Key Indian regulations:** Factories Act 1948, OSH Code 2020.
- **Key international standards:** ISO 10218-1/-2 (industrial robots), ISO 13849-1 (mechanical safety, PL a–e), ISO/TS 15066 (cobots), IEC 61508 (functional safety).
- **Engineer's code phrase to memorize:** *"Public welfare shall come before profit, and safety shall be designed in, not bolted on."*
- **V-Model** is the canonical safety life-cycle: Concept → Hazard Analysis → Requirements → Allocation → Realisation → Installation → Validation → Operation → Decommissioning.
- **Defence-in-Depth (DiD):** Multiple independent safety layers, no single point of failure.
- **The engineer's true leverage point is the base of the Heinrich Pyramid**, not the apex.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Safety: Problems and Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT831 - SAFETY AND RELIABILITY IN ROBOTIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robot Safety: Problems and Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robot Safety** is the systematic discipline of identifying, evaluating, and mitigating the physical, environmental, and operational hazards arising from the deployment and operation of robotic systems in human-shared workspaces. According to the **ISO 12100** and **ISO 10218** standards (the globally recognized baseline for robot safety adopted in KTU 2024 syllabus), robot safety encompasses a structured **risk assessment process** that addresses mechanical, electrical, thermal, ergonomic, and psychological hazards introduced when robots operate alongside, in collaboration with, or in proximity to human workers.

A **hazard** in the robotic context is defined as any *potential source of harm* arising from:
- The robot's inherent energy (kinetic, electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic)
- The end-effector or tool's operating characteristics
- The unpredictability of the work environment
- The complexity of human-robot interaction (HRI)

**Risk**, by contrast, is the *combination of the probability of occurrence of harm* and the *severity of that harm*, mathematically expressed as:

$$R = f(S, P)$$

where $S$ is severity and $P$ is probability of occurrence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The distinction between **hazard** and **risk** is a frequently tested concept in Part A (3-mark) questions. A *hazard* is intrinsic to the system; a *risk* is a quantified measure of harm potential.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Industrial "Iron Co-Worker"

Imagine a powerful hydraulic press in a workshop — capable of exerting **hundreds of kilonewtons** of force, capable of moving at **2 m/s**, and able to repeat an action **10,000 times a day** without fatigue. That is essentially what an industrial robot is: a tireless, superhuman "iron co-worker."

Now, imagine this co-worker cannot speak, cannot see human emotion, has no common sense about a child wandering into the workspace, and cannot reliably predict a worker's reflex movement. **This is the core safety problem of robotics.**

A human worker would instinctively slow down when they see another person nearby. A robot, governed purely by **pre-programmed trajectories and sensor inputs**, will continue its motion unless *explicitly instructed* to stop or to slow down. Therefore, the safety problem is not whether the robot is "dangerous" — it is *unavoidably* powerful — but whether we have engineered **sufficiently reliable barriers, sensors, and software logic** to keep its power from causing harm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Reference:** According to global industrial accident databases, the most common robot-related fatalities occur during **maintenance, programming, and setup phases**, not during normal operation. This is because safety interlocks are often bypassed by trained personnel for troubleshooting — a hazard class known as **"unprotected maintenance access."**

## 1.3 The Three Foundational Categories of Robot Hazards

For KTU examination purposes, hazards in robotic systems are broadly classified into the following three categories, which form the backbone of Module 1:

1. **Mechanical Hazards** — arising from moving parts, end-effectors, stored energy
2. **Environmental & Operational Hazards** — arising from workspace, ambient conditions, and process materials
3. **Human-Related Hazards** — arising from human error, psychological factors, and collaboration

These are explored in detail in the sections that follow.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Robot Hazards — A Structured Breakdown

The KTU 2024 syllabus, in alignment with **ISO 10218-1/2** and **ISO/TS 15066**, identifies the following principal hazard categories that students must master for Module 1.

### A. Mechanical Hazards (Most Critical — ~50% of marks)
- **Pinch Points and Crushing Zones:** Locations where two machine parts move toward each other (e.g., robot joint articulation, gripper closure).
- **Shearing and Cutting Hazards:** Sharp end-effectors, rotating spindles, or cutting tools.
- **Impact Hazards:** Unexpected robot motion causing blunt-force trauma.
- **Trapping Hazards:** A worker's body part becomes caught in a fixed mechanical structure during robot motion.
- **Ejection of Workpieces:** Flying parts from gripper slippage or process failures.
- **Stored Energy Release:** Hydraulic accumulators, pneumatic reservoirs, counterweights, capacitor banks in controllers.

### B. Electrical and Control-System Hazards
- **Arc Flash and Shock:** From power cabinets, motor drives, and busbars.
- **Servo Drive Failures:** A runaway motor can drive the robot arm at full speed.
- **Software Bugs / Logic Errors:** A path-planning error can send the end-effector into an unintended trajectory.
- **Sensor Failures:** Loss of feedback from encoders, force-torque sensors, or vision systems.

### C. Environmental and Process Hazards
- **Welding Fumes, Laser Radiation, Chemical Exposure:** Process-specific emissions.
- **Extreme Temperatures:** Hot end-effectors in foundry applications, cryogenic grippers.
- **Slip, Trip, Fall Hazards:** From cable management issues or floor contamination.

### D. Ergonomic and Human Factors Hazards
- **Cognitive Overload:** Operators misreading HMIs (Human-Machine Interfaces).
- **Startle / Surprise Reflexes:** When a robot moves unexpectedly near a human.
- **Trust Calibration Issues:** Over-trust or under-trust in collaborative robots (cobots).

### E. System-Level Hazards
- **Cybersecurity Vulnerabilities:** Networked robots are vulnerable to malicious command injection.
- **Mode Confusion:** Operators not knowing whether the robot is in AUTO, MANUAL, or TEACH mode.

## 2.2 The Hierarchy of Robot Hazard Causation

Following **Reason's Swiss Cheese Model** (adapted to robotics by KTU 2024 syllabus), hazards manifest when multiple *defense layers* fail simultaneously:

1. **Engineering Design Layer** (intrinsic safety) — fails when robot geometry creates trapping zones.
2. **Safeguarding Layer** (fences, light curtains) — fails when guards are bypassed.
3. **Sensing Layer** (E-stops, proximity sensors) — fails when sensors are miscalibrated.
4. **Procedural Layer** (lockout-tagout) — fails when operators skip steps.
5. **Operator Behavior Layer** — fails due to fatigue, distraction, or training gaps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** "No single safeguard is sufficient." A robot cell must have **at least two independent safety layers** for any given hazard (the principle of **redundancy**).

## 2.3 The Robot Hazard Severity Matrix

In KTU-aligned risk assessment, the severity $S$ of a robot hazard is typically classified into four discrete levels:

| Severity Level | Description | Typical Robot Example |
|---|---|---|
| **S1 — Negligible** | Bruise, minor scrape | Light contact with padded end-effector |
| **S2 — Marginal** | Reversible injury, fracture | Pinch in low-speed joint |
| **S3 — Critical** | Serious irreversible injury, amputation | Crushing in robotic press |
| **S4 — Catastrophic** | Fatality | High-speed impact, full payload swing |

Similarly, **probability of occurrence** $P$ is classified as:

| Probability Level | Description | Frequency Range |
|---|---|---|
| **P1 — Improbable** | Near-zero likelihood | $< 10^{-6}$ per mission |
| **P2 — Remote** | Unlikely under normal operation | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ |
| **P3 — Occasional** | Possible during lifetime | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ |
| **P4 — Frequent** | Expected to occur | $> 10^{-2}$ |

The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** for a robot hazard is then computed as:

$$\boxed{RPN = S \times P \times D}$$

where $D$ is **Detectability** (1 = easily detected, 10 = nearly undetectable). A high RPN indicates a hazard that demands immediate mitigation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Formatting Note:** In the KTU formula sheet, the severity-probability product $\vert S \cdot P \vert$ uses `\vert` rather than the pipe character to avoid breaking markdown table syntax in the rendered KTU digital answer sheet.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 1)

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Range | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Risk Function | $R = f(S, P)$ | Dimensionless index | Quantifying hazard severity |
| 2 | Risk Priority Number | $RPN = S \times P \times D$ | Index 1 – 1000 | FMEA prioritization |
| 3 | Robot Kinetic Energy | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Joules (J) | Stopping distance / impact energy |
| 4 | Safe Stopping Distance | $d_s = v^2 / (2 a)$ | Meters (m) | Light curtain placement |
| 5 | Minimum Safe Gap | $d_{min} = v \cdot t_{react} + d_s$ | Meters (m) | ISO 13855 safety distance |
| 6 | Stopping Time (ISO 10218) | $T_{stop} = T_{detect} + T_{react} + T_{brake}$ | Seconds (s) | Emergency stop analysis |
| 7 | Hazard Rate | $\lambda(t) = f(t) / R(t)$ | Failures / hour | Reliability engineering |
| 8 | Mean Time To Failure | $MTTF = 1 / \lambda$ | Hours | Safety integrity calculation |
| 9 | Pelizzari Formula (Severity) | $S = 10^{(T/1000)} \cdot (V/10) \cdot 0.1$ | Severity score | Historical industrial metric |
| 10 | Collaborative Speed Limit | $v_{max} = \sqrt{2 a_{limit} \cdot d_{safe}}$ | m/s | ISO/TS 15066 cobot limit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where these equations appear in the KTU paper:** Equation 3 ($E_k$) and Equation 4 ($d_s$) almost always appear in Part B 14-mark questions related to *robot impact analysis* and *safety distance calculation*. Equation 6 is the most frequently tested multi-component formula in ESE questions on **emergency stop performance**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The hazard analysis framework discussed here is directly applied in:
- **Automotive welding lines** (Fanuc / KUKA cells with light curtain zoning)
- **Collaborative assembly stations** (Universal Robots UR-series with force-limited joints)
- **Surgical robotics** (da Vinci system — hazard class: unintended instrument motion)
- **Autonomous warehouse robots** (Amazon Kiva / Amazon Proteus — hazard class: human-shared floor navigation)
- **Space robotics** (Canadarm on ISS — hazard class: high-inertia motion in microgravity)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Safe Stopping Distance for a Robot Arm

### Context
A KTU 2024 Part B question often asks: *"Determine the minimum safe distance for a safety light curtain such that the robot stops before a human reaches the hazard zone."*

### Step-by-Step Derivation

We start from Newton's second law of motion applied to the robot end-effector during emergency braking. The robot's braking motion is modeled as uniformly decelerated motion (constant braking deceleration $a$).

**Step 1: Kinematic Starting Equation**

For uniformly decelerated motion, the relationship between initial velocity $v_0$, final velocity $v_f$, deceleration $a$, and distance $s$ is given by the standard kinematic equation:

$$v_f^{\,2} = v_0^{\,2} - 2 a s$$

**Step 2: Define the Stopping Condition**

The robot comes to a complete halt when the final velocity reaches zero:

$$v_f = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0 = v_0^{\,2} - 2 a s$$

**Step 3: Solve for Stopping Distance**

Rearranging the equation from Step 2 to isolate the stopping distance $s$:

$$2 a s = v_0^{\,2}$$

$$s = \frac{v_0^{\,2}}{2 a}$$

**Step 4: Assigning Standard Industrial Symbols**

Following the KTU 2024 / ISO 13855 notation:
- $v_0 \rightarrow v$ (the robot's nominal operating speed at the moment of intrusion)
- $s \rightarrow d_s$ (the stopping distance)
- $a \rightarrow a_{brake}$ (the worst-case robot braking deceleration)

Therefore, the **robot stopping distance** becomes:

$$\boxed{d_s = \frac{v^{\,2}}{2 \, a_{brake}}}$$

**Step 5: Adding the Operator Reaction Time Component**

A real safety-distance calculation must also account for the time $T_{stop}$ between the moment the safety sensor detects a human intrusion and the moment the robot is mechanically at rest. This includes sensor delay, controller reaction time, and brake engagement time. The total distance traveled by the robot during this $T_{stop}$ interval is:

$$d_{lag} = v \cdot T_{stop}$$

**Step 6: Final Safe Distance Formula**

Adding the lag distance to the stopping distance yields the **minimum safety distance** the protective device (light curtain, safety mat) must be placed from the hazard:

$$d_{safe} = d_{lag} + d_s + d_{margin}$$

Substituting the expressions from Step 4 and Step 5:

$$d_{safe} = v \cdot T_{stop} + \frac{v^{\,2}}{2 \, a_{brake}} + d_{margin}$$

where $d_{margin}$ is a small additional safety margin (typically 0.5 m to 1.0 m) per ISO 13855.

**Step 7: The Fully Expanded KTU Form**

The final KTU-board-ready equation, with all terms explicitly defined, is:

$$d_{safe} = v \cdot (T_{sensor} + T_{controller} + T_{brake}) + \frac{v^{\,2}}{2 \, a_{brake}} + d_{margin}$$

**Step 8: Physical Interpretation**

- The first term $v \cdot T_{stop}$ accounts for the **perception-reaction lag** — the robot is *not yet aware* that braking is needed.
- The second term $v^{\,2} / (2 a_{brake})$ accounts for the **physical braking distance** — the time needed for the mechanical brakes to dissipate the kinetic energy $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$.
- The third term $d_{margin}$ is a **structural safety buffer**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When a question provides a *stopping time* rather than individual $T_{sensor}, T_{controller}, T_{brake}$ values, students must use the combined $T_{stop}$ directly. Partial credit is awarded for correctly identifying each term in the expansion.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Question:** A robotic arm moves at $v = 2.0$ m/s. The emergency stop system has a total response time of $T_{stop} = 0.25$ s, and the worst-case braking deceleration is $a_{brake} = 5.0$ m/s². Calculate the minimum safe distance for a light curtain, with a margin $d_{margin} = 0.5$ m.

**Step 1 — Compute Lag Distance**

$$d_{lag} = v \cdot T_{stop} = (2.0)(0.25) = 0.50 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Braking Distance**

$$d_s = \frac{v^{\,2}}{2 a_{brake}} = \frac{(2.0)^2}{2 \times 5.0} = \frac{4.0}{10.0} = 0.40 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Sum All Distances**

$$d_{safe} = d_{lag} + d_s + d_{margin} = 0.50 + 0.40 + 0.50 = 1.40 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** The light curtain must be installed at a minimum of **1.40 m** from the hazard zone.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Hazard Class vs. Mitigation Strategy

| Hazard Class | Energy Source | Severity (S) | Probability (P) | Typical RPN | Primary Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pinch point at joint | Servo motor torque | 3 | 3 | 27 (High) | Speed & separation monitoring |
| Crushing under payload | Gravity + payload mass | 4 | 2 | 32 (Very High) | Hard guarding + light curtain |
| Ejection of workpiece | Pneumatic gripper | 3 | 2 | 18 (Medium) | Enclosure + pressure relief |
| Electrical shock | 480 V drive cabinet | 4 | 1 | 16 (Medium) | Lockout-tagout, insulation |
| Welding fume inhalation | Arc welding process | 2 | 4 | 16 (Medium) | Local exhaust ventilation |
| Software path error | Control algorithm | 4 | 2 | 32 (Very High) | PLC safety logic, watchdogs |
| Sensor occlusion | Dust / obstruction | 3 | 3 | 27 (High) | Redundant sensing |
| Cybersecurity breach | Network | 4 | 1 | 16 (Medium) | Air-gapped control, encryption |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that the *highest* RPN values come from **dynamic, software-mediated hazards** (path errors, sensor failure) rather than static mechanical ones. This is a recurring KTU 2024 theme: **modern robot hazards are increasingly cyber-physical, not purely mechanical.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Robot Safety System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **layered safety architecture** of an industrial robotic cell, mapped to the KTU 2024 Module 1 hazard framework. It is rendered as a block-level functional architecture (per protocol) rather than a physical drawing, since the concept is fundamentally a logical process flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Hazard Source in Robot Cell] --> A1{Mechanical}
    A0 --> A2{Electrical}
    A0 --> A3{Process / Environmental}
    A0 --> A4{Human Factor}

    A1 --> B1[Intrinsic Design Safeguards]
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    A4 --> B1

    B1 --> C1{Passive Guards}
    B1 --> C2{Active Sensors}
    B1 --> C3{Control Logic}

    C1 --> D1[Fences, Enclosures, Shields]
    C2 --> D2[Light Curtains, Safety Mats, Scanners]
    C3 --> D3[PLC Safety Relay, E-Stop, Watchdog]

    D1 --> E1{Risk Reduction Layer 1}
    D2 --> E2{Risk Reduction Layer 2}
    D3 --> E3{Risk Reduction Layer 3}

    E1 --> F1[Residual Risk to Human]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1

    F1 --> G1{Risk Acceptable?}
    G1 -- No --> H1[Iterate Design / Add Safeguards]
    H1 --> A0
    G1 -- Yes --> I1[Approved Robot Cell Operation]
```

### Diagram Interpretation (for the KTU Examiner)

- **Layer 1** identifies the four fundamental hazard sources.
- **Layer 2** introduces the **hierarchical control principle** (ISO 12100): *first design intrinsically safe, then add safeguards.*
- **Layer 3** classifies safeguards into passive, active-sensor, and control-logic categories.
- **Layer 4** enforces the **redundancy requirement** — three independent protection layers.
- **Layer 5** evaluates whether residual risk is within tolerable limits; if not, the loop iterates.

## 4.2 Process Flow: Robot Risk Assessment Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Define Robot Cell Boundaries] --> S2[Step 2: Identify Hazards ISO 12100]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Estimate Severity S]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Estimate Probability P]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute RPN]
    S5 --> S6{RPN Above Threshold?}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Step 6: Apply Risk Reduction Measures]
    S7 --> S8[Step 7: Recalculate RPN]
    S8 --> S6
    S6 -- No --> S9[Step 8: Document and Validate]
    S9 --> S10[Approved Safety Case]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram for Exams:** Each step in this flow corresponds to a paragraph-length answer in a KTU 14-mark question on *risk assessment methodology*. Memorize the iteration loop — examiners award bonus marks for noting that *risk reduction is iterative, not one-shot.*

## 4.3 Hazard Cause-Effect Diagram (Ishikawa / Fishbone Format)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Robot Accident / Injury] --> M1[Mechanical Causes]
    P0 --> M2[Electrical Causes]
    P0 --> M3[Software Causes]
    P0 --> M4[Human Causes]
    P0 --> M5[Environmental Causes]

    M1 --> M1a[Pinch Points]
    M1 --> M1b[Stored Energy]
    M1 --> M1c[End Effector]

    M2 --> M2a[Servo Drive Failure]
    M2 --> M2b[Arc Flash]

    M3 --> M3a[Path Planning Bug]
    M3 --> M3b[Sensor Fusion Error]
    M3 --> M3c[Mode Confusion]

    M4 --> M4a[Operator Error]
    M4 --> M4b[Bypassed Interlock]
    M4 --> M4c[Inadequate Training]

    M5 --> M5a[Poor Lighting]
    M5 --> M5b[Floor Contamination]
    M5 --> M5c[Ambient Noise]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** Differentiate between a *hazard* and a *risk* in the context of robotic systems. Give one example of each from an industrial robot cell.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- A **hazard** is a *potential source of harm* inherent in the robotic system. It is a property of the system itself and exists regardless of whether the harm actually occurs. *Example:* The high-speed motion of a robot arm is a hazard; the pinch point at a robotic gripper is a hazard.
- A **risk** is the *quantified combination* of the severity of harm and the probability that the harm will occur as a result of that hazard. *Example:* The risk that a worker entering the cell will be crushed by the arm (severity S = 4, probability P = 3, yielding a high RPN).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU Standard):** 1 mark for hazard definition, 1 mark for risk definition, 1 mark for the example pair. Students often lose the third mark by giving two hazards instead of one hazard and one risk.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** What is a *pinch point* in a robotic system? Why is it considered a high-severity hazard?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- A **pinch point** is any location in a robotic system where two machine surfaces move toward each other and can trap, crush, or shear a human body part (typically a hand or finger).
- Examples include the gap between a robot gripper and a workpiece, between two articulated links, and between a robot arm and a fixed structure.
- It is considered **high-severity** because the robot's servomotor can exert continuous force of **hundreds of newtons** without operator awareness, and a trapped limb cannot be withdrawn due to the machine's unrelenting motion. A worker cannot physically out-pull a servo-driven axis.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing that "the robot is fast" rather than that "the robot has *unrelenting high torque*." Pinch-point severity arises from **force, not velocity**. Examiners deduct marks if students confuse the two.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Classify the various categories of hazards associated with industrial robotic systems. Explain *mechanical, electrical, and human-factor* hazards in detail, with at least **two examples** for each.

**(b) [7 marks]** A robotic welding arm moves along a linear track at a maximum speed of $v = 1.5$ m/s. The total emergency-stop response time of the system is $T_{stop} = 0.30$ s, and the worst-case braking deceleration is $a_{brake} = 4.0$ m/s². Calculate the **minimum safe distance** at which a safety light curtain must be installed from the hazard zone, assuming a safety margin of $d_{margin} = 0.75$ m. Also compute the kinetic energy of the arm–payload system if the total moving mass is $m = 250$ kg.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Hazard Classification (7 marks)

| # | Hazard Category | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mechanical** | Hazards from moving parts, energy storage, sharp tools | (i) Pinch point between gripper jaws; (ii) Crushing under a 250 kg payload during transfer; (iii) Shearing by a high-speed spindle |
| 2 | **Electrical** | Hazards from power systems and control electronics | (i) Arc flash from 480 V drive cabinet during maintenance; (ii) Servo drive runaway causing uncontrolled motion |
| 3 | **Human-Factor** | Hazards from operator behavior, training, ergonomics | (i) Operator bypassing a safety interlock during teaching; (ii) Cognitive overload from poorly designed HMI; (iii) Mode confusion (operator unsure if robot is in AUTO vs. MANUAL) |
| 4 | **Environmental / Process** | Hazards from the process and surroundings | (i) Welding fume inhalation; (ii) Laser radiation from cutting tools |
| 5 | **Software / Cyber** | Hazards from control logic and networked vulnerabilities | (i) Path-planning algorithm bug; (ii) Network injection attack |

*Mark allocation:* 1 mark per well-explained category, 2 marks for at least two examples with relevance to industrial robotics.

#### Part (b) — Safe Distance Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data**

$$v = 1.5 \text{ m/s}, \quad T_{stop} = 0.30 \text{ s}, \quad a_{brake} = 4.0 \text{ m/s}^{2}, \quad d_{margin} = 0.75 \text{ m}, \quad m = 250 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Lag Distance**

$$d_{lag} = v \cdot T_{stop} = (1.5)(0.30) = 0.45 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Braking Distance**

$$d_s = \frac{v^{2}}{2 \, a_{brake}} = \frac{(1.5)^{2}}{2 \times 4.0} = \frac{2.25}{8.0} = 0.281 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Sum to Obtain Minimum Safe Distance**

$$d_{safe} = d_{lag} + d_s + d_{margin} = 0.45 + 0.281 + 0.75 = 1.481 \text{ m}$$

Rounded to engineering precision: $\boxed{d_{safe} \approx 1.48 \text{ m}}$

**Step 5 — Compute the Kinetic Energy of the Moving Mass**

$$E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} (250)(1.5)^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} (250)(2.25) = 281.25 \text{ J}$$

**Step 6 — Physical Interpretation**

A kinetic energy of **281.25 J** is comparable to a **0.5 kg object dropped from 57 m** or a **2 kg object dropped from 14 m**. This is well above the **100 J threshold** typically considered the upper limit for survivable blunt impact. Therefore, the safety light curtain placement at **1.48 m** is a *mandatory* engineering control.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Question A):**
> 1. Students frequently **omit the safety margin** $d_{margin}$ and arrive at $0.731$ m — losing 1 mark.
> 2. Students sometimes use $a_{brake}$ in units of mm/s² without converting — losing 1 mark.
> 3. Students who write $E_k = m v$ (forgetting the square) lose all 1.5 marks allocated to the energy step.
> 4. Always show **units in every line** of the calculation; KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the **hierarchical robot safety architecture** as per ISO 12100. Discuss the roles of *intrinsic design*, *safeguarding*, *sensing*, and *procedural controls*.

**(b) [7 marks]** A collaborative robot (cobot) is operating in a shared workspace with a human. The cobot's maximum allowable speed in the collaborative zone is constrained by the **biomechanical limit** of human pain threshold, given by the formula:

$$v_{max} = \sqrt{\, 2 \, a_{limit} \cdot d_{safe} \,}$$

If the cobot's deceleration on contact is $a_{limit} = 150 \text{ m/s}^{2}$ and the safety buffer distance is $d_{safe} = 0.20$ m, calculate the **maximum permissible cobot speed** in the collaborative zone. State the ISO standard that governs this scenario.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Hierarchical Safety Architecture (7 marks)

The **ISO 12100 hierarchy of risk reduction** applied to robotic cells proceeds in the following four levels, in strict order of priority:

1. **Level 1 — Intrinsic Safe Design (Most Preferred):** The robot's mechanical design is modified to eliminate the hazard at its source. *Examples:* rounding sharp edges, reducing maximum reach envelope, limiting payload to prevent high-energy motion, using rounded end-effectors with compliant padding.

2. **Level 2 — Safeguarding (Engineering Controls):** Physical barriers and interlocks that prevent human access. *Examples:* perimeter fencing with interlocked gates, fixed enclosures around the robot's maximum reach, mechanical stops limiting axis travel.

3. **Level 3 — Sensing (Active Protective Devices):** Devices that detect human presence and command the robot to stop or slow. *Examples:* safety light curtains, laser area scanners, safety mats, vision-based human detection, capacitive proximity sensors.

4. **Level 4 — Procedural Controls (Information for Use):** Training, signage, lockout-tagout procedures, and operating instructions. *Examples:* operator manuals, warning labels, prescribed maintenance procedures, ISO 10218-2 installation guidelines.

*Block diagram:* Refer to the **Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes.

*Mark distribution:* 3 marks for correct ordering, 2 marks for examples, 2 marks for explaining that *higher levels are preferred over lower levels*.

#### Part (b) — Collaborative Speed Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data**

$$a_{limit} = 150 \text{ m/s}^{2}, \quad d_{safe} = 0.20 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Speed Constraint Formula**

$$v_{max} = \sqrt{ 2 \, a_{limit} \cdot d_{safe} } = \sqrt{ 2 \times 150 \times 0.20 } = \sqrt{ 60.0 } = 7.746 \text{ m/s}$$

Wait — this value is **unrealistically high** for a collaborative robot. The model formulation here is an *upper-bound* energetic estimate. Applying a more conservative interpretation: in **ISO/TS 15066**, the actual biomechanical limit for transient contact on the hand is approximately **$v_{max} \leq 250$ mm/s = 0.25 m/s**. Therefore, the practical collaborative speed limit is governed by the **lower** of the two values.

$$\boxed{v_{max,\,practical} = 0.25 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Governing Standard**

The applicable standard is **ISO/TS 15066:2016 — Collaborative Robots** (specifically, the speed-and-separation monitoring regime and the power-and-force-limiting regime). This standard supplements **ISO 10218-1/2** by specifying biomechanical thresholds for human-robot contact in collaborative applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Question B):**
> 1. Students who skip the *ordering* in Part (a) and write the four levels in random order lose 1.5 marks.
> 2. Students who compute $v_{max} = 7.75$ m/s and stop, without noting the **biomechanical ISO/TS 15066 ceiling** of 0.25 m/s, lose 1 mark for failing to apply the standard's practical limit.
> 3. Writing "ISO 15066" instead of the correct "**ISO/TS 15066**" loses 0.5 mark (the "TS" denotes a Technical Specification, distinct from a full ISO standard — KTU examiners are strict on this).
> 4. Students frequently confuse *power-and-force-limiting* with *speed-and-separation monitoring*; they are two different collaborative operating modes per ISO/TS 15066.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Hazard vs. Risk:** Hazard = intrinsic source of harm; Risk = quantified severity × probability.
- **RPN Formula:** $RPN = S \times P \times D$, where $S \in \{1,2,3,4\}$, $P \in \{1,2,3,4\}$, $D \in \{1, \ldots, 10\}$.
- **Five Hazard Categories:** Mechanical, Electrical, Environmental, Human-Factor, Software/Cyber.
- **Most Common Robot Hazard:** Pinch point — high severity due to **unrelenting servo torque**, not speed.
- **Most Common Accident Phase:** *Maintenance, setup, programming* — not normal operation.
- **Reason's Swiss Cheese Model:** Hazards manifest only when multiple defense layers fail simultaneously.
- **ISO 12100:** Hierarchy of risk reduction — intrinsic → safeguarding → sensing → procedural.
- **ISO 10218-1/2:** Industrial robot safety standard (Part 1 = robot; Part 2 = cell integration).
- **ISO/TS 15066:** Collaborative robots; specifies the 0.25 m/s practical speed limit and biomechanical contact thresholds.
- **ISO 13855:** Defines minimum safety distance for protective devices (light curtains, mats).
- **Safe Stopping Distance Formula:** $d_{safe} = v \cdot T_{stop} + \frac{v^2}{2 a_{brake}} + d_{margin}$.
- **Kinetic Energy:** $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ — values **above 100 J** are typically considered non-survivable for blunt impact.
- **Redundancy Principle:** *No single safeguard is sufficient* — at least two independent safety layers are required for any given hazard.
- **Stop Time Composition:** $T_{stop} = T_{sensor} + T_{controller} + T_{brake}$.
- **Four Robot Operating Modes:** AUTO, MANUAL, TEACH, SERVICE — confusion among these is a major human-factor hazard.
- **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Modern robot hazards are *cyber-physical* (software, networking, sensor fusion) — not purely mechanical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examination Tip:** When asked to *"list the hazards,"* always organize your answer using the **five-category taxonomy** (Mechanical, Electrical, Environmental, Human, Software). Examiners award full marks only for systematic classification. Bullet-point dumps of random hazards lose 1–2 marks for lack of structure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Roles of Robot Manufacturers and Users in Robot Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT831 - SAFETY AND RELIABILITY IN ROBOTIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Roles of Robot Manufacturers and Users in Robot Safety

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of industrial robotic safety, **robot safety is a shared bi-directional responsibility** distributed between two principal entities: the **Robot Manufacturer (Supplier/Integrator)** and the **Robot User (End-User/Employer)**. This division of responsibility is codified in international consensus standards, most notably:

- **ANSI/RIA R15.06-2012** (American National Standard for Industrial Robots and Robot Systems — Safety Requirements)
- **ISO 10218-1:2011** (Robots and robotic devices — Safety requirements for industrial robots — Part 1: Robots)
- **ISO 10218-2:2011** (Robots and robotic devices — Safety requirements for industrial robots — Part 2: Robot systems and integration)
- **ISO/TS 15066:2016** (Collaborative Robots — Technical Specification)
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147** (U.S. Occupational Safety and Health Administration standards)

The **Robot Manufacturer** is the entity that designs, fabricates, assembles, and markets a robot or robot component. The **Robot User** (or system integrator acting on behalf of the user) is the entity that commissions the robotic workcell, integrates the robot into a complete operational system, and operates it within a specific industrial environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The fundamental principle in robot safety is that **the manufacturer's responsibility ends at the cell boundary**, while the **user's responsibility begins at the cell boundary**. This is a critical demarcation point that is frequently tested in KTU examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine purchasing a **new car** from a manufacturer and then driving it daily on highways:

| Role | Car Analogy | Robot Analogy |
|------|-------------|---------------|
| **Manufacturer** | Builds the car with **airbags, ABS brakes, crumple zones, seatbelts, owner's manual**, and ensures it passes crash-test safety standards. | Designs a robot with **built-in safety-rated hardware, emergency stop circuits, speed/force limiting, software limits, and documentation (User Manual, Risk Assessment File)**. |
| **User** | Drives the car responsibly, **follows traffic rules, maintains the vehicle, wears the seatbelt, trains other drivers**, and does not modify safety features. | Installs the robot safely, conducts a **site-specific risk assessment, adds guarding/perimeter, trains operators, performs regular maintenance**, and never bypasses safety interlocks. |

Just as a car manufacturer **cannot be held liable** for a driver who disables the airbags and crashes, a robot manufacturer **cannot be held liable** for an end-user who removes the light curtain and ignores the safety manual. Conversely, just as a driver cannot blame the manufacturer for a poorly-maintained car, the user cannot blame the manufacturer for a poorly-designed workcell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The manufacturer delivers a **safe product** (a "safe robot"), but the user is responsible for creating a **safe system** (a "safe robot cell" or "robot system"). The two are fundamentally different deliverables, and each party has a distinct legal, ethical, and technical responsibility.

## 1.3 Governing Safety Metrics & Standardized Constants

The following standardized metrics and constants are used throughout the KTU syllabus and are essential for numerical problems:

- **ISO 13855 Approach Speed (K):** $\mathbf{K = 1600 \text{ mm/s}}$ for upper limb reach, $\mathbf{K = 2000 \text{ mm/s}}$ for lower limb reach, $\mathbf{K = 2500 \text{ mm/s}}$ for the whole body.
- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL):** Discrete levels **1, 2, 3, 4** as per IEC 61508, with **SIL 4** being the highest.
- **Performance Level (PL):** Discrete levels **a, b, c, d, e** as per ISO 13849-1, with **PL e** being the highest.
- **Collaborative Robot Quasi-Static Limit:** $\mathbf{250 \text{ mm/s}}$ (quasi-static contact speed threshold per ISO/TS 15066).
- **Collaborative Robot Power and Force Limiting (PFL) Threshold:** $\mathbf{N = 150 \text{ N}$ (maximum permissible quasi-static contact force on the hand/finger region per ISO/TS 15066).**
- **Stopping Time (T):** Total time from the activation of a safeguard to the cessation of all hazardous motion, measured in **seconds (s)**.
- **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF):** A dimensionless multiplier representing the reduction in risk achieved by a protective measure.

## 1.4 Distinction: "Robot" vs. "Robot System"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Frequently Tests This:**
> - A **"Robot"** = The manipulator alone (manufacturer's deliverable).
> - A **"Robot System"** = Robot + End-Effector + Workpiece + Peripherals + Safeguarding + Workspace = **The complete integrated workcell** (user's deliverable).
>
> Standards like ISO 10218-1 apply to the **robot**, and ISO 10218-2 applies to the **robot system**. Students must clearly state which standard is being referenced in their answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Manufacturer's Role — Detailed Breakdown

The robot manufacturer carries the following technical and legal obligations, as specified in **ISO 10218-1, Clause 5** and **ANSI/RIA R15.06**:

### A. Inherent Safe Design (Hazard Elimination at Source)
- Eliminate hazards or reduce risks by design **before** relying on safeguarding or protective equipment.
- Apply the **Hierarchy of Controls**: **Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering Controls → Administrative Controls → PPE**.
- Examples: Limiting robot reach through mechanical stops, using low-inertia actuators, designing collision-tolerant structures, applying current-limiting motor drives.

### B. Built-in Safety Functions
- Provide **Safety-Rated Soft Axis and Space Limiting (SASL)** — software-based axis limits.
- Provide **Safety-Rated Soft Stop (SS1, SS2)** — controlled stop functions per IEC 61800-5-2.
- Provide **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** capability.
- Provide **Emergency Stop (E-Stop)** circuits with **Category 0, 1, or 2** stop functionality per IEC 60204-1.
- Provide **Safety-rated monitored stop (SMS)**.
- Provide **Hand-guiding** functionality for collaborative robots.
- Provide **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** via current sensing, collision detection, or compliant joints.

### C. Risk Assessment (at the Robot Level)
- Conduct a **manufacturer's risk assessment** of the robot as a standalone product, considering all foreseeable hazards (mechanical, electrical, thermal, ergonomic, radiation, noise, vibration).
- Document findings in the **Risk Assessment File** as part of the technical construction file.

### D. Documentation and Information Provision
- Provide an **Installation, Operation, and Maintenance Manual** with specific safety information.
- Provide **declarations and markings** (e.g., CE marking, nameplate, payload rating, reach envelope).
- Provide **Safety Function Specifications** (PFHd, PL, SIL, response time for each safety function).
- Provide **Cyber-physical security guidance** for connected/IoT-enabled robots.

### E. Verification, Validation, and Testing
- Conduct **type-testing** of safety functions.
- Provide **test reports** demonstrating compliance.
- Maintain **traceability** between hazards, risk estimates, and protective measures.

## 2.2 User's Role — Detailed Breakdown

The robot user (or system integrator acting on the user's behalf) carries the following obligations, as specified in **ISO 10218-2, Clause 5** and **OSHA guidelines**:

### A. System-Level Risk Assessment
- Conduct a **site-specific, application-specific risk assessment** of the complete robot system.
- Consider the **actual task** (welding, painting, material handling, assembly, etc.), the **workpiece**, the **end-effector**, the **layout**, the **operator interaction profile**, and the **environmental conditions**.
- This is the user's **primary and most critical obligation**.

### B. Application of Safeguarding Measures
- Install **physical guards** (fixed, interlocked, adjustable per ISO 12100).
- Install **presence-sensing safeguarding devices (PSSD)** — light curtains, safety mats, laser scanners, 3D area scanners.
- Establish **safety-rated zones**: Restricted Space, Operating Space, Safeguarded Space, and Collaborative Workspace.
- Apply **Two-Hand Control** devices if required.
- Install **Emergency Stop** devices at all operator stations.

### C. System Integration and Layout
- Ensure **minimum safety distances** between hazards and personnel using the formula $S = (K \times T) + C$.
- Validate **workspace clearances** for the **maximum reach** of the robot plus end-effector plus workpiece.
- Ensure **cable management** does not create new hazards (tripping, entanglement).
- Ensure **environmental** considerations (lighting, noise, temperature, EMI/EMC) do not degrade safety.

### D. Training and Competency
- Provide **task-specific training** to all personnel who operate, program, teach, maintain, or supervise the robot system.
- Training must cover: Normal operation, Programming, Teaching, Maintenance, Troubleshooting, and Emergency procedures.
- Maintain **training records** as evidence of compliance.

### E. Maintenance, Inspection, and Continuing Compliance
- Establish a **Preventive Maintenance Schedule** for all safety components.
- Conduct **periodic inspection and testing** of safety-rated functions (typically every 12 months or per manufacturer recommendation).
- Maintain a **log of all changes, modifications, and incidents**.
- Re-evaluate the risk assessment whenever a change is made to the system (the "**Change Management**" obligation).

### F. Compliance with Local Regulations
- Comply with national and regional laws (e.g., OSHA in the USA, CE Machinery Directive in EU, Factories Act in India).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas matter in KTU exams:** The minimum safety distance formula and the risk reduction formula are tested in **both Part A (3 marks)** as short derivation questions and in **Part B (14 marks)** as full numerical problems.

### Table 2.1 — Safety Distance Formulas (ISO 13855 / EN ISO 13857)

| Formula | Application | Description |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| $S = (K \times T) + C$ | General safeguarding (light curtains, two-hand control) | Standard safety distance for safeguards perpendicular to the approach direction |
| $S = (K \times T) + (C - d_{pf})$ | Light curtains with reach-through | Where $d_{pf}$ is the depth of the field of the detection zone |
| $S_{SSM} = S_h + S_r + S_s + C$ | Speed and Separation Monitoring (collaborative) | $S_h$ = hand speed, $S_r$ = robot reaction, $S_s$ = stopping distance, $C$ = intrusion |

### Table 2.2 — Risk Reduction and Severity Metrics

| Symbol | Parameter | Unit / Value | Source Standard |
|--------|-----------|--------------|-----------------|
| $R$ | Risk | Dimensionless (qualitative scale) | ISO 12100 |
| $S$ | Severity of harm (1-4) | S1=Negligible, S2=Marginal, S3=Critical, S4=Catastrophic | ANSI B11.0 / ISO 13849-1 |
| $F$ | Frequency of exposure (1-5) | F1=Rare to F5=Frequent | ISO 12100 |
| $P$ | Possibility of avoidance (1-5) | P1=Possible to P5=Impossible | ISO 12100 |
| $N$ | Max PFL contact force (hand/finger) | **150 N** | ISO/TS 15066 |
| $v_{max,QS}$ | Max quasi-static contact speed | **250 mm/s** | ISO/TS 15066 |
| $K$ | Approach speed (upper limb) | **1600 mm/s** | ISO 13855 |
| $T$ | Total stopping time | seconds | IEC 60204-1 |
| $C$ | Intrusion distance | mm | ISO 13855 |

### Table 2.3 — Bi-Directional Responsibility Matrix

| Responsibility | Manufacturer | User / Integrator |
|----------------|:------------:|:-----------------:|
| Conducting risk assessment on standalone robot | ✅ | ❌ |
| Conducting risk assessment on complete system | ❌ | ✅ |
| Providing safety-rated hardware functions | ✅ | ❌ |
| Installing perimeter safeguarding (guards, light curtains) | ❌ | ✅ |
| Providing user manual and installation instructions | ✅ | ❌ |
| Conducting site-specific training | ❌ | ✅ |
| Validating minimum safety distance | ❌ | ✅ |
| Performing periodic safety function tests | ❌ | ✅ |
| CE marking / regulatory certification | ✅ | ❌ |
| Maintaining log of operational incidents | ❌ | ✅ |
| Re-evaluating risk after system modification | ❌ | ✅ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The bi-directional responsibility model is not merely an academic construct. It is the foundation of:
- **Product Liability Law** — determines legal accountability in case of injury or death.
- **Insurance Underwriting** — premiums for robotic cells depend on which party's safety obligations were fulfilled.
- **Cross-Border Trade** — mutual recognition of standards (e.g., ANSI/RIA R15.06 being harmonized with ISO 10218) enables global robot trade.
- **Forensic Engineering** — in the event of a robot-related accident, the responsibility matrix is the first document an investigator consults.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always structure your answer into **two clear sub-sections: (i) Manufacturer's Role, (ii) User's Role**, and conclude with a brief paragraph on the **coordinated nature** of the responsibility. This earns the **"synthesis" cognitive mark** under the KTU 2024 OBE evaluation rubric.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Minimum Safety Distance Formula

The minimum safety distance $S$ ensures that a person approaching a hazardous zone is detected, and the hazard is neutralized, **before the person can reach the hazard**. This derivation follows the logic of **ISO 13855:2010** (Safety of machinery — Positioning of safeguards with respect to the approach speeds of parts of the human body).

### Step 1: Define the Physical Scenario

A person is standing at a distance $S$ (in mm) from a hazard. A light curtain (or similar electro-sensitive protective device) is positioned at distance $S$ from the hazard, oriented **perpendicular** to the direction of approach.

### Step 2: Identify the Time Components

From the instant the person interrupts the light curtain, two events must occur in sequence:

1. **Detection and Reaction Time ($T_1$)**: The time for the safeguard to detect the interruption and trigger the stop signal.
2. **Braking/Halt Time ($T_2$)**: The time for the machine to physically come to a complete stop after receiving the stop signal.

Therefore, the **Total Stopping Time** is:

$$
T = T_1 + T_2
$$

where $T$ is measured in **seconds (s)**.

### Step 3: Define the Approach Speed

A person approaching the hazard will move at a characteristic speed $K$, measured in **mm/s**. According to ISO 13855, this is conservatively set to:

$$
K = 1600 \text{ mm/s} \quad \text{(upper limbs, perpendicular approach)}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Distance Travelled by the Person

While the machine is stopping, the person continues to approach the hazard. The distance covered by the person in time $T$ is:

$$
D_{\text{approach}} = K \times T
$$

### Step 5: Account for Intrusion Beyond the Detection Plane

Even after the machine has stopped, the person's hand or fingers may have already intruded a distance $C$ into the hazard zone before the safeguard is reached. This is the **intrusion distance**, measured in **mm**.

### Step 6: Combine All Terms

The total minimum safety distance must satisfy the condition:

$$
S \geq D_{\text{approach}} + C
$$

Substituting Step 4:

$$
S = (K \times T) + C
$$

$$
\boxed{S = (K \times T) + C}
$$

This is the foundational safety distance equation. Each term has a clear physical meaning:

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| $K$ | Approach speed of human body part | $K = 1600$ mm/s |
| $T$ | Total stopping time of the robot | $0.1 \text{ to } 2.0$ s (application dependent) |
| $C$ | Intrusion distance beyond the safeguard | $0$ to $1200$ mm (per ISO 13855 Table 1) |

### Step 3.2 — Extended Formula: Light Curtains with Reach-Through

When a person can reach **through** the light curtain plane (e.g., a horizontal light curtain with openings), the effective reach into the hazard is reduced. ISO 13855 modifies the formula to:

$$
S = (K \times T) + (C - d_{pf})
$$

where $d_{pf}$ is the **depth of the protective field** measured from the light curtain plane to the far edge of the hazard.

### Step 3.3 — Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM) for Collaborative Robots

In collaborative operation per **ISO/TS 15066**, the protective separation distance is computed as:

$$
S_{SSM}(t_0) = v_{h}(t_0) \times T_{r} + v_{robot} \times T_{r} + \int_{t_0}^{t_0 + T_s} v_{robot}(t)\,dt + C + Z_{s} + Z_{r}
$$

where:

- $v_{h}(t_0)$ = operator hand speed at time $t_0$
- $T_{r}$ = robot's reaction time
- $T_{s}$ = robot's stopping time
- $C$ = intrusion distance
- $Z_{s}$ = position uncertainty of the operator (sensing system)
- $Z_{r}$ = position uncertainty of the robot

For steady-state operation, this simplifies to:

$$
S = S_h + S_r + S_s + C
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A 6-DOF industrial robot is used for spot welding in an automotive assembly line. A vertical light curtain protects the perimeter of the cell. The robot's controller has a reaction time of 30 ms, and the mechanical braking time is 200 ms. The intrusion distance per ISO 13855 Table 1 for a reach above the light curtain is 850 mm. The operator is assumed to be approaching with the upper limbs.
>
> **Compute the minimum safety distance S.**

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Compute the total stopping time $T$**

$$
T = T_1 + T_2
$$

$$
T = 0.030 \text{ s} + 0.200 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T = 0.230 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2: Identify the approach speed $K$ for upper limbs (perpendicular approach per ISO 13855)**

$$
K = 1600 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the distance travelled by the operator during the stop**

$$
D_{\text{approach}} = K \times T
$$

$$
D_{\text{approach}} = 1600 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.230 \text{ s}
$$

$$
D_{\text{approach}} = 368 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Add the intrusion distance**

$$
S = D_{\text{approach}} + C
$$

$$
S = 368 \text{ mm} + 850 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
S = 1218 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5: Express the result in metres and state the engineering interpretation**

$$
\boxed{S = 1218 \text{ mm} \approx 1.22 \text{ m}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The light curtain must be installed **at least 1.22 metres away from any point where the operator's hand could reach a hazardous robot motion**. In practice, engineers would round **up** to the nearest practical mounting position (e.g., 1.25 m or 1.30 m) for additional safety margin and to comply with the **"ALARP" (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)** principle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Convention:** Always carry **2-3 extra significant figures** in intermediate steps and only round the final answer. Always include the **unit** in every line. Marks are deducted for "naked numbers" without units.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Risk Assessment Decision Logic

The user's risk assessment follows a structured, iterative process. The analytical matrix below summarizes the decision logic of **ISO 12100:2010** as it is applied in robot cell safety:

| Step | Action | Decision / Output | Responsible Party |
|------|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Define the robot system's limits and intended use | System description document | User |
| 2 | Identify hazards (mechanical, electrical, ergonomic, etc.) | Hazard list | User |
| 3 | Estimate risk for each identified hazard | Risk score $R = S \times F \times P$ | User |
| 4 | Evaluate whether risk is tolerable | Compare $R$ to tolerable risk criteria | User |
| 5 | If not tolerable → apply protective measures (hierarchy) | Risk reduction measure installed | User |
| 6 | Verify that the new residual risk is tolerable | Re-calculate $R$ | User |
| 7 | Document the entire process | Risk Assessment File | User |
| 8 | Review whenever a change is made | Updated Risk Assessment File | User |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Bi-Directional Responsibility Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["STANDARDS BODY<br/>ISO 10218 / ANSI RIA R15.06<br/>ISO TS 15066 / IEC 61508"]
    node2["ROBOT MANUFACTURER<br/>Designs Safe Robot<br/>ISO 10218-1 Compliance"]
    node3["ROBOT USER or INTEGRATOR<br/>Builds Safe Robot System<br/>ISO 10218-2 Compliance"]
    node4["SAFE ROBOT<br/>Hardware Functions<br/>Software Limits<br/>Documentation"]
    node5["SAFE ROBOT SYSTEM<br/>Cell Layout<br/>Perimeter Guards<br/>Trained Operators"]
    node6["INDUSTRIAL WORKCELL<br/>Operational State<br/>Ongoing Maintenance"]
    node7["WORKPLACE SAFETY<br/>Zero Incidents Goal<br/>Regulatory Compliance"]

    node1 -->|Specifies Requirements| node2
    node1 -->|Specifies Requirements| node3
    node2 -->|Delivers| node4
    node3 -->|Integrates and Operates| node5
    node4 -->|Shipped to| node3
    node5 -->|Deploys| node6
    node6 -->|With Maintenance| node7
    node2 -.->|Shared Boundary: Cell Perimeter| node3
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Step 1: Define Robot System<br/>Limits and Intended Use"]
    startB["Step 2: Identify All Hazards<br/>Mechanical Electrical Ergonomic"]
    startC["Step 3: Estimate Risk<br/>Severity x Frequency x Possibility"]
    startD{"Step 4: Is Risk<br/>Tolerable?"}

    startA --> startB
    startB --> startC
    startC --> startD

    startE --> startF
    startF --> startD

    startG --> startH
    startH --> finish
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Manufacturer vs. User Responsibility Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph manuBlock["MANUFACTURER SCOPE - ISO 10218-1"]
        m1["Inherent Safe Design<br/>Eliminate Hazards"]

    boundary --> u2
    boundary --> u3
    boundary --> u4
    boundary --> u5
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    h1["Level 1: ELIMINATE<br/>Remove the hazard entirely<br/>Example: Replace hazard with non-hazardous process"]
    h2["Level 2: SUBSTITUTE<br/>Use less hazardous material or process<br/>Example: Use collaborative robot instead of high-speed robot"]
    h3["Level 3: ENGINEERING CONTROLS<br/>Guards, interlocks, light curtains, two-hand control"]
```
