# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any THREE key responsibilities of a robot manufacturer in ensuring robot safety, as per ISO 10218-1.**

**Model Answer:**

The three key responsibilities of a robot manufacturer per ISO 10218-1 are:

1. **Inherent Safe Design:** The manufacturer must design the robot to eliminate or reduce hazards at source before relying on safeguarding. For example, using low-inertia actuators and mechanical stops to limit reach.

2. **Built-in Safety Functions:** The manufacturer must provide safety-rated hardware and software functions such as **Emergency Stop (E-Stop)**, **Safety-Rated Soft Axis and Space Limiting (SASL)**, **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SMS)**, and **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** for collaborative robots.

3. **Documentation and Information Provision:** The manufacturer must provide a complete **Installation, Operation, and Maintenance Manual**, the **Risk Assessment File** for the standalone robot, safety function specifications (PFHd, PL, SIL), and proper **markings/labeling** (e.g., nameplate, CE marking).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: State the formula for the minimum safety distance as per ISO 13855 and explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer:**

The minimum safety distance formula as per ISO 13855 is:

$$
S = (K \times T) + C
$$

Where:
- **S** = Minimum safety distance, measured in **mm**.
- **K** = Approach speed of the human body part toward the hazard (e.g., **1600 mm/s** for upper limbs in perpendicular approach).
- **T** = Total stopping time of the machine, which is the sum of the safeguard's reaction time $T_1$ and the machine's mechanical braking time $T_2$, measured in **seconds**.
- **C** = Intrusion distance, which is the additional distance a person's hand or fingers can travel beyond the detection plane of the safeguard before the hazard is reached, measured in **mm**.

This formula ensures that the safeguard (e.g., light curtain) is mounted at a sufficient distance so that the hazard is neutralized before the operator can physically reach it.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: With neat diagrams, explain the roles and responsibilities of (a) the robot manufacturer and (b) the robot user in ensuring safety in an industrial robotic cell. Also, mention the relevant international standards governing these roles.**

#### (a) Robot Manufacturer's Roles and Responsibilities (7 Marks)

**Definition:** The robot manufacturer is the entity that designs, fabricates, and supplies the robot manipulator. Their safety responsibility is governed by **ISO 10218-1:2011** and the relevant portions of **ANSI/RIA R15.06-2012**.

**Key Responsibilities:**

1. **Inherent Safe Design:** Apply the **hierarchy of controls** to eliminate hazards at the design stage. This includes using low-inertia materials, designing energy-isolating mechanisms, providing mechanical stops, and limiting the robot's reach envelope.

2. **Built-in Safety-Rated Functions:** Provide Safety Integrity Level (SIL) or Performance Level (PL) rated functions including:
   - Emergency Stop (Category 0, 1, or 2)
   - Safety-Rated Soft Axis and Space Limiting (SASL)
   - Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SMS)
   - Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)
   - Power and Force Limiting (PFL) for collaborative operation
   - Hand-Guiding functionality

3. **Risk Assessment (Robot Level):** Conduct a risk assessment of the standalone robot considering all foreseeable hazards (mechanical, electrical, thermal, ergonomic, noise, vibration).

4. **Documentation:** Provide the **User Manual**, **Installation Manual**, **Maintenance Manual**, **Risk Assessment File**, **Declaration of Conformity**, and proper **markings/nameplate**.

5. **Verification and Type Testing:** Conduct type tests on all safety functions and provide test reports demonstrating compliance with ISO 10218-1.

**Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.3** Mermaid diagram (Manufacturer Scope block).

---

#### (b) Robot User's Roles and Responsibilities (7 Marks)

**Definition:** The robot user (or system integrator acting on behalf of the user) is the entity that commissions, integrates, and operates the robotic workcell. Their safety responsibility is governed by **ISO 10218-2:2011** and **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147** (in the U.S.).

**Key Responsibilities:**

1. **Site-Specific Risk Assessment:** Conduct a comprehensive risk assessment of the **complete robot system** — considering the actual task, workpiece, end-effector, layout, operator profile, and environmental conditions.

2. **Application of Safeguarding:** Install **physical guards** (fixed, interlocked), **electro-sensitive protective equipment** (light curtains, safety mats, laser scanners), and establish safety-rated zones (Restricted Space, Operating Space, Safeguarded Space, Collaborative Workspace).

3. **Validation of Safety Distances:** Calculate and validate the **minimum safety distance** using $S = (K \times T) + C$.

4. **Training and Supervision:** Provide task-specific training to operators, programmers, maintenance staff, and supervisors. Maintain training records.

5. **Periodic Inspection and Maintenance:** Implement a preventive maintenance schedule, perform periodic safety function tests, and maintain a log of all incidents and modifications.

6. **Re-evaluation After Changes:** Re-conduct the risk assessment whenever any modification is made to the robot system (Change Management).

**Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.3** Mermaid diagram (User/Integrator Scope block).

---

#### Relevant International Standards

| Standard | Title | Applicability |
|----------|-------|---------------|
| ISO 10218-1:2011 | Robots and robotic devices — Safety requirements for industrial robots — Part 1: Robots | Manufacturer |
| ISO 10218-2:2011 | Robots and robotic devices — Safety requirements for industrial robots — Part 2: Robot systems and integration | User / Integrator |
| ISO/TS 15066:2016 | Collaborative Robots — Technical Specification | Both (for cobots) |
| ANSI/RIA R15.06-2012 | American National Standard for Industrial Robots | Both |
| ISO 12100:2010 | Safety of machinery — General principles for design | Both |
| ISO 13849-1:2015 | Safety of machinery — Safety-related parts of control systems | Both |
| IEC 61508 | Functional Safety of E/E/PE Systems | Manufacturer |
| ISO 13855:2010 | Safety of machinery — Positioning of safeguards | User |

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) A palletizing robot cell is protected by a vertical light curtain. The operator approaches the cell with the upper limbs. The robot controller's reaction time is 40 ms, and the mechanical braking time is 250 ms. The intrusion distance per ISO 13855 Table 1 is 950 mm. Calculate the minimum safety distance.**

**(b) Discuss why the robot user's responsibility is considered more critical and more complex than the robot manufacturer's responsibility, with reference to the actual operating environment.**

#### Part (a) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Approach speed for upper limbs (perpendicular): $K = 1600$ mm/s
- Reaction time: $T_1 = 40$ ms $= 0.040$ s
- Mechanical braking time: $T_2 = 250$ ms $= 0.250$ s
- Intrusion distance: $C = 950$ mm

**Step 1: Compute the total stopping time $T$**

$$
T = T_1 + T_2
$$

$$
T = 0.040 + 0.250
$$

$$
T = 0.290 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the safety distance formula**

$$
S = (K \times T) + C
$$

$$
S = (1600 \times 0.290) + 950
$$

$$
S = 464 + 950
$$

$$
S = 1414 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: Express the final answer in metres**

$$
\boxed{S = 1414 \text{ mm} = 1.414 \text{ m} \approx 1.42 \text{ m}}
$$

**Engineering Recommendation:** The light curtain should be installed at a minimum of **1.42 m** from any reach point into the hazard. In practice, the engineer would round up to the next practical mounting position (e.g., **1.50 m**) to incorporate a **safety margin** and to comply with the ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) principle.

---

#### Part (b) — Discussion (7 Marks)

The robot user's responsibility is considered more critical and more complex than the manufacturer's for the following reasons:

**1. Variability of the Operating Environment:** The manufacturer designs the robot for a **generic, defined set of operating conditions**. The user, however, must deploy the robot in a **unique, real-world environment** that may include variable lighting, temperature, humidity, dust, electromagnetic interference, and floor conditions — all of which can affect safety function reliability.

**2. Application-Specific Hazards:** The manufacturer cannot foresee the specific task the user will assign. A robot designed for **cleanroom semiconductor handling** faces very different hazards when re-tasked to **metal cutting** or **foundry operations**. The user is responsible for identifying these **application-specific hazards** that emerge only after integration.

**3. Integration of Multiple Subsystems:** The user integrates the robot with end-effectors, conveyors, fixtures, vision systems, and safety devices from **multiple vendors**. Each interface introduces potential hazards (mechanical pinch points, electrical mismatches, communication latencies) that the manufacturer does not control.

**4. Human Interaction Variability:** The user must consider the **actual human operators** — their training level, fatigue, attention, and behaviour. Human variability is a major source of risk that the manufacturer does not control.

**5. Lifecycle Management:** The user's responsibility extends throughout the **operational life** of the cell (often 10–25 years), including **maintenance, modifications, and decommissioning**. The manufacturer's responsibility ends at the point of sale/installation.

**6. Site-Specific Risk Assessment:** The user must perform a **fresh, site-specific risk assessment** for **every** installation, even if the same robot model is used in multiple cells. This assessment is the foundation of legal compliance and is **not transferable** from the manufacturer.

**7. Regulatory and Legal Liability:** In the event of an accident, the user (employer) is the **primary legal defendant** under occupational safety laws, not the manufacturer. This makes the user's role the most legally consequential.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Students frequently write $T_1 = 40$ ms without converting to seconds, leading to an answer that is **1000 times too large**. Always convert ms → s before substituting.
> 2. **Confusing $T_1$ and $T_2$:** Some students add them incorrectly or omit one. The total stopping time $T = T_1 + T_2$ is **non-negotiable**.
> 3. **Forgetting the intrusion distance $C$:** Many students compute only $K \times T$ and stop. The $C$ term is essential and carries significant marks.
> 4. **Using the wrong approach speed $K$:** $K = 1600$ mm/s is for upper limbs. $K = 2000$ mm/s is for lower limbs, and $K = 2500$ mm/s is for whole-body approach. Misreading the question leads to a wrong answer.
> 5. **Not stating the standard:** KTU examiners award 1 mark for **citing the standard** (e.g., "as per ISO 13855"). Omitting this is a mark loss.
> 6. **Confusing "robot" vs. "robot system":** Stating that the manufacturer is responsible for the "robot cell" loses marks. The manufacturer is responsible only for the "robot" per ISO 10218-1.
> 7. **In 14-mark questions, omitting a diagram:** Part (a) of long-answer questions **must** include a diagram (e.g., a light curtain + operator + hazard schematic). Missing diagrams can cost 2–3 marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Roles of Robot Manufacturers and Users in Robot Safety**

- ✅ **Two parties, two scopes:** Manufacturer (ISO 10218-1) = safe **robot**; User (ISO 10218-2) = safe **robot system**.
- ✅ **Cell boundary is the handoff point:** Manufacturer's responsibility ends and user's responsibility begins at the cell perimeter.
- ✅ **Manufacturer must provide:** Inherent safe design, safety-rated functions (E-Stop, SASL, SMS, PFL), documentation, risk assessment of standalone robot, and type testing.
- ✅ **User must provide:** Site-specific risk assessment, perimeter safeguarding, safety distance validation, training, maintenance, change management.
- ✅ **Safety Distance Formula:** $S = (K \times T) + C$. Know each term cold.
- ✅ **Approach speeds to memorize:** $K = 1600$ mm/s (upper limbs), $K = 2000$ mm/s (lower limbs), $K = 2500$ mm/s (whole body).
- ✅ **Collaborative robot thresholds:** $v_{max,QS} = 250$ mm/s, $N_{max,PFL} = 150$ N (hand/finger).
- ✅ **Standards to cite in answers:** ISO 10218-1, ISO 10218-2, ISO/TS 15066, ISO 13855, ISO 12100, ANSI/RIA R15.06.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls:** Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering → Administrative → PPE. This sequence is **non-negotiable** in any risk-reduction discussion.
- ✅ **Total stopping time:** $T = T_1 + T_2$ (always convert ms to s).
- ✅ **SSM formula:** $S = S_h + S_r + S_s + C$ for collaborative robots.
- ✅ **Re-evaluation obligation:** The user must re-evaluate the risk assessment **after every modification** — this is a KTU favourite.
- ✅ **ALARP principle:** As Low As Reasonably Practicable — always mention this when justifying safety margins in numerical problems.
- ✅ **Common examiner traps:** Unit conversion errors, missing $C$ term, wrong $K$ value, missing diagram, and missing standard citation.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme 14-mark questions, structure your answer as: **(1) Definition → (2) List of responsibilities with explanation → (3) Relevant standards in a table → (4) Engineering interpretation or numerical solution → (5) A closing synthesis paragraph on the bi-directional nature of responsibility.** This structure covers all 6 cognitive levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy and maximizes your score.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Considerations in Robot Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT831 - SAFETY AND RELIABILITY IN ROBOTIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Safety Considerations in Robot Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛡️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Safety Considerations in Robot Design** refers to the systematic integration of hazard identification, risk assessment, and engineered protective measures into every phase of a robotic system's lifecycle — from conceptual design and mechanical fabrication to electrical integration, software control, deployment, and end-of-life decommissioning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Strictly Aligned):**
> Safety in robot design is the proactive engineering discipline that ensures robotic systems operate without causing unacceptable physical, electrical, ergonomic, or environmental harm to human operators, co-workers, bystanders, property, or the system itself — even under foreseeable misuse, component failure, or abnormal operating conditions.

The governing international standards for industrial and collaborative robot safety include:

| Standard | Full Title | Scope |
|----------|------------|-------|
| **ISO 10218-1:2011** | Robots and robotic devices — Safety requirements for industrial robots — Part 1: Robots | Robot manufacturer design requirements |
| **ISO 10218-2:2011** | Robots and robotic devices — Safety requirements for industrial robots — Part 2: Robot systems and integration | System integrator requirements |
| **ISO/TS 15066:2016** | Collaborative Robots — Technical Specification | Power/force limiting, speed/separation monitoring |
| **ANSI/RIA R15.06-2012** | Industrial Robots and Robot Systems — Safety Requirements | U.S. adoption of ISO 10218 |
| **ISO 13849-1:2015** | Safety of machinery — Safety-related parts of control systems | Performance Levels (PL a–e) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Knife" Intuition

Imagine designing a **professional kitchen knife** for a busy restaurant:

- The knife is the **robot** — it is *designed to be sharp and powerful* to do its job.
- The chef's hand is the **human operator** working in close proximity.
- The handle, the bolster (thick junction), and the sheath are the **safety features**.
- A cut-resistant glove is the **collaborative safeguard** — it doesn't stop the knife from being sharp, but limits harm.
- The training certificate is the **procedural safeguard**.

Similarly, a robot is built to be **powerful, fast, and precise** — these are the qualities that make it useful. Safety design does **not** weaken the robot; it ensures that this inherent power can be co-existed with humans through engineered barriers, controlled energy release, and intelligent monitoring.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight for KTU Students:** Safety is **NOT an add-on feature** bolted at the end. It is a **design parameter** as fundamental as payload capacity, reach, or repeatability. A robot designed without safety considerations is, by definition, a **non-engineered weapon**.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Robot Safety

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU question on robot safety design will be anchored to these three pillars:

1. **Inherent Safety by Design** — Eliminating hazards at the source (e.g., rounded edges, low-voltage buses, energy-stored mechanical locks).
2. **Safeguarding (Engineering Controls)** — Physical barriers, interlocks, light curtains, emergency stops.
3. **Procedural & Administrative Controls** — Training, lockout-tagout, warning signs, restricted access zones.

## 1.4 Why Robot Safety is Uniquely Challenging

Robotic systems are not "just machines." They exhibit specific characteristics that amplify hazard potential:

- **High kinetic energy**: Industrial robots can move at speeds up to **$\mathbf{v = 10\ m/s}$** with payloads exceeding **$\mathbf{m = 1000\ kg}$**, yielding kinetic energy $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ potentially exceeding **$\mathbf{50\ kJ}$**.
- **Non-intuitive motion**: Multi-axis articulated arms trace **unpredictable 3D trajectories** that humans cannot anticipate.
- **Repetitive high-speed cycles**: Operator attention fatigue is a major risk.
- **Multiple energy domains**: Electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic, gravitational (suspended loads), and stored mechanical energy.
- **Programmable behavior**: A single software bug can cause catastrophic motion at any moment.

## 1.5 Key Terminology (KTU High-Frequency Vocabulary)

> [!NOTE]
> These terms appear in nearly every KTU Part-A question. Memorize verbatim:

- **Hazard** — A potential source of harm.
- **Risk** — The combination of the *probability* of occurrence of harm and the *severity* of that harm.
- **Safeguard** — A protective measure used to reduce risk.
- **Stop Category 0** — Uncontrolled stop by immediate removal of power (hardest, fastest).
- **Stop Category 1** — Controlled stop with power maintained until stop is achieved, then power removed.
- **Stop Category 2** — Controlled stop with power retained (e.g., servo hold).
- **Performance Level (PL)** — Discrete level (a, b, c, d, e) specifying the reliability of safety functions per **ISO 13849-1**.
- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** — Analogous metric (1–4) per **IEC 61508**, used in process industries.
- **Collaborative Operation** — Operation where a robot and a human share the same workspace.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Risk Assessment Process (ISO 12100 Framework)

Robot safety design is anchored to the **risk assessment loop** defined in **ISO 12100:2010** — the umbrella standard for machinery safety. Every KTU design question will require you to articulate this loop.

### 2.1.1 The Four-Step Logic Chain

1. **Hazard Identification** — Systematically enumerate *all* potential sources of harm. For a robot, this includes mechanical, electrical, thermal, ergonomic, and software-induced hazards.
2. **Risk Estimation** — For each hazard, estimate the **Severity (S)** of potential harm, the **Frequency of Exposure (F)** to the hazard, and the **Possibility of Avoidance (P)**.
3. **Risk Evaluation** — Compare the estimated risk against the **Acceptable Risk Threshold**. If above threshold, mitigation is mandatory.
4. **Risk Reduction** — Apply the **hierarchy of controls** (see §2.2 below) iteratively until residual risk is acceptable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The loop is **iterative**. After applying a safeguard, the residual risk must be re-evaluated — the safeguard itself may introduce new hazards (e.g., a light curtain creates an optical nuisance, a fence creates a confined-space entry hazard).

## 2.2 Hierarchy of Controls (KTU Favourite Diagram Topic)

> [!NOTE]
> This hierarchy is **strictly ordered** by effectiveness. KTU questions often ask: *"What is the FIRST control measure you should apply?"* — The answer is always **Inherent Safe Design**, never PPE.

From **most effective to least effective**:

1. **Inherent Safe Design** — Eliminate the hazard entirely (e.g., reduce maximum speed, use rounded profiles, lower operating voltage).
2. **Safeguarding & Complementary Protective Measures** — Engineering controls (guards, interlocks, two-hand controls, light curtains).
3. **Information for Use** — Training, manuals, warning labels, signage.
4. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — Gloves, safety glasses — *last resort*.

## 2.3 Mechanical Safety Design Principles

### 2.3.1 Physical Hazard Mitigation

| Hazard Source | Design Countermeasure |
|---------------|----------------------|
| **Sharp edges / pinch points** | Rounded corners, compliant covers, minimum **$\mathbf{25\ mm}$** clearance on grippers |
| **Crush / shear points** | Limiting joint travel ranges, mechanical stops, compliance devices |
| **Suspended loads** | Secondary retention (safety chains on hoists), gravity-fail-safe brakes |
| **Stored mechanical energy** | Spring-loaded dump valves, slow-exhaust pneumatic circuits, brake release locks |
| **Rotating shafts** | Fixed guarding per **ISO 13857** (safety distances) |

### 2.3.2 Safety Distance Calculation (KTU 14-Mark Favourite)

The minimum safety distance $\mathbf{S}$ from a hazardous point to a safeguard (e.g., light curtain, safety mat) is calculated per **ISO 13855**:

$$
S = K \cdot T + C
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{S}$ = minimum safety distance (mm)
- $\mathbf{K}$ = approach speed parameter (typically **$\mathbf{1600\ mm/s}$** for upper limbs walking toward the hazard, **$\mathbf{2000\ mm/s}$** for the whole body)
- $\mathbf{T}$ = total stopping time (response time of safeguard + machine stop time, in seconds)
- $\mathbf{C}$ = intrusion distance constant (typically **$\mathbf{850\ mm}$** for vertical light curtains, or **$\mathbf{1200 - 0.4 \cdot H}$** for horizontal curtains with height H)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **$\mathbf{C}$ constant of 850 mm** is derived from the assumption of a human reach radius. KTU examiners explicitly test whether you remember which constant to use for *vertical* vs. *horizontal* light curtains.

## 2.4 Electrical Safety Design

> [!NOTE]
> Three non-negotiable electrical safety design rules for robots:

1. **Isolation** — All power circuits must have a lockable main disconnect per **IEC 60204-1**.
2. **Earthing (Grounding)** — Protective earth conductor with impedance **$\mathbf{< 0.1\ \Omega}$** to chassis.
3. **Emergency Stop (E-Stop)** — Hardwired, normally-closed, positive-opening contact per **IEC 60947-5-5**, wired directly into the safety relay chain — *not* through the PLC's standard I/O.

### 2.4.1 E-Stop Circuit Topology (Stop Category 0)

The E-stop must **bypass** all software and standard PLC logic. It operates by physically interrupting the power supply to the motor contactors via a **safety relay** (e.g., Pilz PNOZ, Sick FX3). When pressed:

- The **safety contactor (K1, K2)** drops out.
- Power to the servo drives is removed.
- Brakes engage (fail-safe spring-set brakes are *energize-to-release*).
- The system enters **Stop Category 0** (uncontrolled stop).

## 2.5 Collaborative Robot (Cobot) Safety

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Hot Topic:** ISO/TS 15066 defines **four collaborative operation methods**. Any Part-B design question on cobots will require naming all four.

| Method | Core Mechanism | Limitation |
|--------|----------------|------------|
| **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)** | Robot stops when human enters workspace; resumes when human leaves | No concurrent motion |
| **Hand Guiding** | Operator physically leads the robot via a validated handle | Limited to low-speed, low-force tasks |
| **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** | Robot speed dynamically adjusted to maintain protective distance | Requires reliable real-time tracking |
| **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** | Robot's biomechanical limits (force, pressure) capped to below injury thresholds | Inherently limited to low-payload, low-speed designs |

### 2.5.1 PFL — The Bio-Mechanical Limits

ISO/TS 15066 specifies **quasi-static and transient contact force limits** for different body regions:

$$
F_{max} = f(\text{body region}, \text{contact area})
$$

For example, the maximum permissible force on the **hand/finger** during a transient contact is approximately **$\mathbf{140\ N}$**, while for the **chest** it is approximately **$\mathbf{140\ N}$** (quasi-static) but the **transient limit** is **$\mathbf{210\ N}$**.

The pressure limit on the skin is:

$$
P_{max} \leq 50\ N/cm^2 \quad \text{(quasi-static, sustained contact)}
$$

## 2.6 Performance Levels (PL) per ISO 13849-1

> [!NOTE]
> Each safety function in a robot system must be assigned a **Performance Level (PL)** based on risk. KTU questions frequently ask: *"A robot's emergency stop must perform to what PL?"* — The answer is typically **PL d, Category 3** minimum for industrial robots.

| PL | Probability of Dangerous Failure per Hour (PFHd) | Typical Use in Robots |
|----|--------------------------------------------------|----------------------|
| **a** | $\geq 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$ | Simple warnings |
| **b** | $\geq 10^{-6}$ to $< 10^{-5}$ | Light curtain in non-critical zone |
| **c** | $\geq 10^{-7}$ to $< 10^{-6}$ | Standard guarding |
| **d** | $\geq 10^{-8}$ to $< 10^{-7}$ | E-stop, interlocked gate |
| **e** | $< 10^{-8}$ | Highest-integrity safety functions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Category 3 vs Category 4:** Category 3 requires that a *single fault* does not lead to loss of safety function, and the fault is *detected* where reasonably practicable. Category 4 requires that a *single fault* does not lead to loss of safety function, AND the fault is *always detected*. Use Category 4 for the highest-risk safety functions.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Strictly use `\vert` or `\mid` notation — never bare `|` — to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Safety Distance (Vertical Light Curtain) | $S = K \cdot T + 850$ | mm | Minimum curtain-to-hazard gap |
| 2 | Safety Distance (Horizontal Curtain) | $S = K \cdot T + (1200 - 0.4 \cdot H)$ | mm | Floor-level detection |
| 3 | Robot Kinetic Energy | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Joules (J) | Hazard severity quantification |
| 4 | Approach Speed (Upper Limb) | $K = 1600$ | mm/s | ISO 13855 standard |
| 5 | Approach Speed (Whole Body) | $K = 2000$ | mm/s | ISO 13855 standard |
| 6 | Stop Time (Worst Case) | $T_{total} = T_{response} + T_{brake}$ | seconds | Includes safeguard latency |
| 7 | Bio-Mechanical Pressure Limit | $P \leq 50$ | N/cm² | Cobot PFL threshold |
| 8 | PFHd Threshold (PL d) | $PFH_d \in [10^{-8}, 10^{-7})$ | failures/hour | Safety function reliability |
| 9 | Earthing Impedance Limit | $R \leq 0.1$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Electrical safety |
| 10 | Robot Reach Envelope (Volume) | $V = \tfrac{4}{3} \pi R^3$ | m³ | Restricted-space calculation |
| 11 | Risk Score (Simplified) | $R = S \times F \times P$ | dimensionless | Risk matrix cell value |
| 12 | Stopping Distance (Linear) | $d_s = v_0 \cdot t_r + \tfrac{v_0^2}{2 \cdot a}$ | metres | Worst-case overrun |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Pitfall:** Students often forget that $T$ in the safety distance equation must include **the response time of the safeguard device ITSELF** plus the **machine stopping time**, not just one or the other. If the light curtain takes 20 ms to respond and the robot takes 280 ms to brake, $T = 0.300$ s, not 0.280 s.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles covered here are not academic exercises. They are the **legal foundation** for any industrial deployment. In practice:

- A factory installing a 6-axis robot welding cell must conduct a documented risk assessment before commissioning.
- Insurance underwriters in the EU/CE marking regime **refuse coverage** if ISO 10218 is not demonstrably applied.
- A cobot designed without a **PFL biomechanical model** is non-compliant and cannot carry the **CE mark**.
- Robot designers use these formulas to determine physical cell dimensions — get the safety distance wrong by 200 mm and a worker can reach a pinch point before the robot stops.

# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Safety Distance for a Robotic Press Brake

> [!NOTE]
> This worked derivation is the **gold standard** for a KTU 14-mark design question. The total stop time, the approach speed, and the intrusion constant are all explicitly shown.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A robotic press brake has a vertical light curtain positioned in front of the punch. Given:
- Measured machine stopping time (from safety signal to full stop): **$T_m = 380\ ms$**
- Light curtain response time: **$T_r = 22\ ms$**
- Reach distance beyond the light curtain to the nearest hazard point: **$H_d = 600\ mm$**
- The operator's hand can approach horizontally and reach **$H_d$ beyond** the curtain plane.

Calculate the **minimum required safety distance $S$** between the light curtain and the hazard point, and verify whether a curtain mounted at **$S_{actual} = 1100\ mm$** is compliant.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the applicable formula.**

Since the light curtain is vertical and detects the upper limbs approaching the hazard:

$$
S = K \cdot T + C
$$

**Step 2: Determine $K$.**

The approach speed of the operator's hand toward the hazard, per **ISO 13855**, is the upper-limb approach speed:

$$
K = 1600\ \text{mm/s}
$$

**Step 3: Determine $T$ — the total stopping time.**

The total stopping time is the sum of the safeguard's response time and the machine's stop time:

$$
T = T_r + T_m
$$

Substituting values:

$$
T = 22\ \text{ms} + 380\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
T = 402\ \text{ms}
$$

Converting to seconds:

$$
T = 0.402\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 4: Determine $C$ — the intrusion constant.**

The operator's hand can reach **$H_d = 600\ mm$** beyond the light curtain plane into the hazard zone. The standard intrusion constant for upper limbs is **$C = 850\ mm$**.

> **Logic check:** The 850 mm constant assumes a full hand-plus-arm reach. If the *actual* reach is less (e.g., 600 mm here), the *smaller* value is used. Therefore:

$$
C = 600\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 5: Compute $S$.**

$$
S = (1600\ \text{mm/s}) \times (0.402\ \text{s}) + 600\ \text{mm}
$$

First, compute the kinematic term:

$$
1600 \times 0.402 = 643.2\ \text{mm}
$$

Then add the intrusion constant:

$$
S = 643.2 + 600
$$

$$
\boxed{S = 1243.2\ \text{mm}}
$$

**Step 6: Compliance verification.**

The actual installed safety distance is $S_{actual} = 1100\ \text{mm}$.

$$
S_{actual} = 1100\ \text{mm} \quad < \quad S_{required} = 1243.2\ \text{mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** The installation is **NON-COMPLIANT**. The light curtain must be repositioned outward by at least:

$$
\Delta S = 1243.2 - 1100 = 143.2\ \text{mm}
$$

The corrective action is to move the light curtain to **$S_{corrected} \geq 1244\ \text{mm}$** from the hazard point. Alternatively, the machine's stopping time must be reduced to meet the existing 1100 mm distance.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Allowable Speed for a Cobot Using PFL

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation tests the student's ability to relate *kinematic* (speed, mass) parameters to *biomechanical* (force, pressure) limits — a frequent KTU exam theme.

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A collaborative robot has an effective moving mass at the end-effector of **$m = 8\ kg$**. The robot must operate in PFL mode against a human operator. The transient contact force limit on the operator's hand per ISO/TS 15066 is **$F_{max} = 140\ N$**. Compute the **maximum allowable end-effector speed** $v_{max}$.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Energy balance at impact.**

Assuming a perfectly inelastic collision (worst case), the kinetic energy of the moving mass is fully converted into the deformation work on the human body. The transient force limit implicitly assumes a specific deceleration distance. For a conservative estimate, equate kinetic energy to the work done against the limit force over the human-tissue deformation distance **$d = 5\ mm = 0.005\ m$** (a typical ISO/TS 15066 assumption):

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} m v^2 = F_{max} \cdot d
$$

**Step 2: Solve for $v_{max}$.**

$$
v_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot F_{max} \cdot d}{m}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
v_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 140 \times 0.005}{8}}
$$

Compute the numerator:

$$
2 \times 140 \times 0.005 = 1.4\ \text{J}
$$

Compute the ratio:

$$
\dfrac{1.4}{8} = 0.175\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

Take the square root:

$$
v_{max} = \sqrt{0.175}
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{max} \approx 0.418\ \text{m/s}}
$$

**Step 3: Engineering interpretation.**

A speed of 0.42 m/s (≈ 1.5 km/h) is well below the typical 1 m/s cap on cobots. This shows that PFL is *not* a high-speed mode — it is the **slowest and safest** collaborative method, and designers must build the robot to be **mechanically capable of lower kinetic energy** at the same payload as a non-collaborative industrial robot.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Safety Methods

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 14-mark questions often ask for a *comparative analysis*. Use this matrix as your answer backbone.

| Dimension | Light Curtain | Safety Mat | Interlocked Gate | PFL (Cobot) |
|-----------|--------------|-----------|------------------|-------------|
| **Detection Principle** | Optical beam break | Pressure/contact | Mechanical switch | Force/pressure sensing |
| **Worst-Case Stop Cat.** | 1 (controlled) | 0 or 1 | 1 | 2 (servo hold) |
| **Reset Required** | No (auto-reset allowed) | No | Yes (manual ack.) | No |
| **PL Achievable** | Up to PL e | Up to PL d | Up to PL e | Up to PL d |
| **Maintenance Cost** | Medium (lens cleaning) | High (wear) | Low (mechanical) | Low (software) |
| **Susceptibility to Bypass** | High (reach over/under) | Medium (crawl-over) | Low (interlock) | Low (sensor-based) |
| **Cost (Relative)** | $$ | $ | $ | $$$ |

## 3.4 Sample Computation: Risk Score from a Risk Matrix

A robot cell has the following hazard profile:

- **Severity (S):** S2 (Serious — could cause permanent injury)
- **Frequency of Exposure (F):** F2 (Frequent — daily)
- **Possibility of Avoidance (P):** P1 (Possible under specific conditions)

The simplified risk score is:

$$
R = S \times F \times P
$$

Substituting:

$$
R = 2 \times 2 \times 1 = 4
$$

On a typical 1–25 risk matrix, a score of 4 falls in the **"Medium Risk — Risk Reduction Required"** zone, demanding the application of at least one safeguarding measure from the hierarchy of controls.

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The Risk Assessment Loop (ISO 12100)

This diagram maps the iterative risk-reduction workflow that anchors every robot safety design. Each node is a defined phase; arrows are the formal information flow mandated by ISO 12100.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Step 1: Determine Machine Limits and Intended Use"] --> node2["Step 2: Hazard Identification"]
    node2 --> node3["Step 3: Risk Estimation S F P"]
    node3 --> node4{"Step 4: Risk Above Acceptable Threshold?"}
    node4 -- YES --> node5["Step 5: Apply Hierarchy of Controls"]
    node5 --> node6["Step 6: Re-evaluate Residual Risk"]
    node6 --> node4
    node4 -- NO --> node7["Step 7: Document and Deploy"]
    node7 --> node8["Step 8: Periodic Review and Reassessment"]
    node8 --> node2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The loop is **not** unidirectional. Once deployed, periodic review (Step 8) feeds back into hazard identification. Real-world incidents, near-misses, or design changes (e.g., a new gripper) trigger a new pass through the loop.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Hierarchy of Controls (KTU Visual Favourite)

This diagram ranks protective measures from most to least effective — a 14-mark question almost always includes this as a sub-part.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ctrlA["TIER 1: INHERENT SAFE DESIGN - Eliminate hazard at source - e.g., lower speed, rounded geometry"] --> ctrlB
    ctrlB["TIER 2: SAFEGUARDING - Engineering controls - guards, interlocks, light curtains, two-hand control"] --> ctrlC
    ctrlC["TIER 3: INFORMATION FOR USE - Procedural controls - training, manuals, warning signs"] --> ctrlD
    ctrlD["TIER 4: PPE - Last resort - gloves, safety glasses, ear protection"]
    style ctrlA fill:#d4edda
    style ctrlD fill:#f8d7da
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The colour gradient (green → red) reinforces the inverse relationship between *priority* and *reliance on human behaviour*. Tier 4 is the least reliable because it depends on perfect human compliance 100% of the time.

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Emergency Stop Circuit Topology (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

For a 6-axis industrial robot, this is the **functional safety architecture** that physically implements a Stop Category 0 emergency stop. It bypasses the standard PLC, ensuring the E-stop is *independent* of any software fault.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pwr["3-Phase Mains Supply 415 V AC"] --> disc["Main Disconnect Switch Lockable per IEC 60204"]
    disc --> con1["Safety Contactor K1 Hardwired NC Contact"]
    disc --> con2["Safety Contactor K2 Redundant Path"]
    con1 --> drive["Servo Drive Bus"]
    con2 --> drive
    drive --> motor["Servo Motors on All 6 Axes"]
    motor --> brake["Spring-Set Fail-Safe Brakes Engage on Power Loss"]
    estop["E-Stop Button NC Positive Opening IEC 60947"] --> srelay["Safety Relay Pilz PNOZ s4 or Equivalent"]
    gate["Interlocked Gate Switch"] --> srelay
    lc["Light Curtain OSSD Output"] --> srelay
    srelay --> con1
    srelay --> con2
    srelay --> ind["Safety Status Indicator Lamp"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Note that the E-stop button, the gate interlock, and the light curtain all feed *directly* into the safety relay. The safety relay has *redundant* outputs (K1 and K2) that drive two *independent* contactors. This is the **Category 3 architecture**: a single contactor failure (e.g., K1 welded closed) does not disable the E-stop function, because K2 is still operational. For **Category 4**, the contactors would also be *monitored* for weld faults via auxiliary contacts feeding back to the safety relay.

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Collaborative Operation Decision Tree

This diagram helps a designer choose the appropriate collaborative mode for a given application.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{"Is human in shared workspace?"} -- NO --> ind1["Use Standard Industrial Safeguarding ISO 10218-1"]
    q1 -- YES --> q2{"Concurrent motion required?"}
    q2 -- NO --> srms["Use SRMS Safety Rated Monitored Stop"]
    q2 -- YES --> q3{"Operator must physically guide robot?"}
    q3 -- YES --> hg["Use Hand Guiding with Validated Enabling Device"]
    q3 -- NO --> q4{"Reliable real-time distance sensing available?"}
    q4 -- YES --> ssm["Use SSM Speed and Separation Monitoring"]
    q4 -- NO --> pfl["Use PFL Power and Force Limiting with Biomechanical Validation"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical design rule:** SSM and PFL are the *only* methods that permit **true concurrent robot-human motion** (both robot and human moving in the workspace simultaneously). SRMS and Hand Guiding require the robot to be *stationary* during human presence.
