# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q1]`

**Define the term "Performance Level (PL)" as applied to safety-related parts of robot control systems. List all five levels in increasing order of reliability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Performance Level (PL)** is a discrete level used in **ISO 13849-1** to specify the reliability of a safety function in terms of its **Probability of Dangerous Failure per Hour (PFH$_d$)**. It represents the ability of the safety-related control system to perform its required safety function under foreseeable conditions.

The five levels in increasing order of reliability are:

| PL | PFH$_d$ (failures/hour) |
|----|--------------------------|
| PL a | $\geq 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$ |
| PL b | $\geq 10^{-6}$ to $< 10^{-5}$ |
| PL c | $\geq 10^{-7}$ to $< 10^{-6}$ |
| PL d | $\geq 10^{-8}$ to $< 10^{-7}$ |
| PL e | $< 10^{-8}$ |

*(1 mark: definition; 2 marks: correct ordering of all five levels with PFH$_d$ range.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q2]`

**What is the difference between Stop Category 0 and Stop Category 1 as defined in IEC 60204-1?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Stop Category 0** is an **uncontrolled stop** achieved by the immediate removal of power to the machine actuators. Brakes (if any) are engaged by mechanical springs. It is the **fastest** and **hardest** stop. *(1.5 marks)*

- **Stop Category 1** is a **controlled stop** where power to the actuators is maintained during the braking phase to achieve a controlled deceleration, and the power is removed only **after the machine has come to a standstill**. The control system supervises the stop. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 📘 **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Q]`

**(a)** Explain the **four collaborative operation methods** defined in ISO/TS 15066, with one practical application example for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A vertical light curtain guards a robotic palletizing cell. The light curtain response time is **$T_r = 30\ ms$**, the robot stop time is **$T_m = 420\ ms$**, and the operator's hand can reach **$750\ mm$** beyond the curtain plane to the hazard. Calculate the **minimum required safety distance $S$** and state whether a curtain installed at **$S_{actual} = 1500\ mm$** meets the requirement. *(7 marks)*

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Four Collaborative Methods (7 marks)

**1. Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS) — 1.5 marks**
The robot performs its full-speed task outside the human's reach. When the human enters the collaborative workspace, sensors (e.g., safety scanner) trigger a **Stop Category 2** (servo-hold). The robot is stationary while the human is present. **Example:** A CNC machine tending robot that an operator must load/unload; the robot stops the moment the operator opens the cell door.

**2. Hand Guiding — 1.5 marks**
The operator physically grasps a validated handle on the robot's end-effector. A three-position **enabling device** must be continuously held in the middle position; releasing it or squeezing it to the third position triggers an emergency stop. **Example:** Teaching a complex welding path by physically guiding the welding torch along the joint.

**3. Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM) — 2 marks**
A safety-rated sensor (laser scanner or vision system) continuously measures the **separation distance** between the human and the robot. The robot's speed is **dynamically reduced** as the distance shrinks, maintaining a protective distance at all times. **Example:** A mobile robot in a warehouse that slows as a worker approaches and stops if the worker gets too close.

**4. Power and Force Limiting (PFL) — 2 marks**
The robot is intrinsically designed so that any contact with a human cannot exceed **biomechanical injury thresholds** (force, pressure) specified in ISO/TS 15066. This requires **rounded surfaces, compliant padding, and torque-sensing joints**. **Example:** A cobot assembling delicate electronics alongside a human operator on the same bench.

---

### Part (b) — Safety Distance Calculation (7 marks)

**Given data:**
- $T_r = 30\ \text{ms} = 0.030\ \text{s}$
- $T_m = 420\ \text{ms} = 0.420\ \text{s}$
- Reach beyond curtain: $H_d = 750\ \text{mm}$
- Approach speed: $K = 1600\ \text{mm/s}$ (upper limb, per ISO 13855)

**Step 1: Compute total stopping time $T$ (1 mark)**

$$
T = T_r + T_m = 0.030 + 0.420 = 0.450\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 2: Identify the intrusion constant $C$ (1 mark)**

Since the actual reach is $750\ \text{mm}$ (less than the standard $850\ \text{mm}$):

$$
C = 750\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the safety distance formula (2 marks)**

$$
S = K \cdot T + C
$$

$$
S = 1600 \times 0.450 + 750
$$

$$
S = 720 + 750
$$

$$
\boxed{S = 1470\ \text{mm}}
$$

**Step 4: Compliance check (2 marks)**

$$
S_{actual} = 1500\ \text{mm} \quad > \quad S_{required} = 1470\ \text{mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** The installation is **COMPLIANT** with a margin of **$30\ \text{mm}$**. The installer has satisfied ISO 13855 requirements.

**Note (1 mark):** A margin of only 30 mm is marginal; in good engineering practice, a safety factor of at least 10% is recommended. A preferred installation distance would be $S_{recommended} \approx 1617\ \text{mm}$.

---

### 📗 **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Q]`

**(a)** Describe the **hierarchy of controls** as defined in ISO 12100. Why is PPE considered the *last* resort? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 6-axis robot has a payload of **$m = 25\ kg$** moving at a peak end-effector speed of **$v = 2.5\ m/s$**. Compute the **kinetic energy** of the moving mass and comment on the safety implications. *(7 marks)*

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Hierarchy of Controls (7 marks)

**The hierarchy has four levels, applied in strict top-down order (1 mark for the list, 2 marks for explanation):**

1. **Inherent Safe Design** — Modify the design itself to remove the hazard. Examples: use lower voltage, reduce maximum speed, use rounded edges, eliminate stored energy.

2. **Safeguarding & Complementary Protective Measures** — Add engineering controls that intervene between the hazard and the human. Examples: fixed guards, interlocked gates, light curtains, two-hand control, safety mats.

3. **Information for Use** — Inform and warn the user. Examples: warning labels, operator manuals, training, signage.

4. **PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)** — Equip the human with protective gear. Examples: safety glasses, gloves, cut-resistant sleeves.

**Why PPE is the last resort (4 marks):**
- **Human Compliance Dependency:** PPE works *only if* the human wears it correctly *every single time*. Statistics show PPE compliance rates of 60–80% in industrial settings — this is unacceptable for life-critical applications.
- **No Hazard Elimination:** The hazard remains fully present; PPE merely mitigates the *consequence*, not the *cause*.
- **Comfort and Heat Stress:** PPE can cause operator fatigue, leading to other errors.
- **Single-Point-of-Failure:** A torn glove, a removed safety glasses for "just a second" — any lapse immediately removes the protection.
- **Engineering controls and inherent design are passive, automatic, and do not depend on the operator's state of mind, fatigue, or training level.**

---

### Part (b) — Kinetic Energy Computation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $m = 25\ \text{kg}$
- $v = 2.5\ \text{m/s}$

**Step 1: Apply the kinetic energy formula (2 marks)**

$$
E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2
$$

**Step 2: Compute $v^2$ (1 mark)**

$$
v^2 = (2.5)^2 = 6.25\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 3: Substitute and compute (2 marks)**

$$
E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 25 \times 6.25
$$

$$
E_k = 12.5 \times 6.25
$$

$$
\boxed{E_k = 78.125\ \text{J}}
$$

**Step 4: Safety commentary (2 marks)**

A kinetic energy of **$78.125\ \text{J}$** is **highly hazardous**. For reference:
- A **1 kg** object dropped from **8 metres** (impact speed ≈ 12.5 m/s) has $E_k \approx 78\ \text{J}$ — sufficient to cause severe skull injury.
- The robot's payload has nearly the same energy at only 2.5 m/s.

**Engineering implications:**
- A **light curtain or safety mat is mandatory** before commissioning.
- The robot must operate in a **fully enclosed cell** with interlocked access.
- **Emergency stop response time** must be minimized to limit the overrun.
- The mechanical design must include **fail-safe brakes** capable of dissipating this energy safely.
- An **energy-absorbing compliant covering** on the end-effector can reduce injury severity in the event of a contact fault.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Robot Safety Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $C$ constant's conditional use:** Many students blindly write $C = 850$ mm. If the *actual* reach is less (as in Q3.1 above), the smaller value applies. Examiners deduct **2 full marks** for this.
> 2. **Mixing Stop Categories:** Stop Category 0 (uncontrolled, power removed) is *not* the same as Stop Category 1 (controlled, power removed after stop). They are not interchangeable.
> 3. **Confusing PL with SIL:** PL is per **ISO 13849-1** (machinery). SIL is per **IEC 61508** (process). Citing the wrong standard for the wrong industry loses marks.
> 4. **PPE listed first:** In any "design a safety system" question, PPE must be **last**, not first. KTU examiners will deduct **at least 2 marks** if PPE is placed at the top of the hierarchy.
> 5. **Forgetting the safeguard's own response time in the $T$ term:** $T = T_{response} + T_{machine}$. Omitting $T_{response}$ is the single most common computational error.
> 6. **Calling SRMS, Hand Guiding, SSM, PFL "the same thing":** Each has a distinct ISO/TS 15066 definition. Vague answers score 1–2 marks out of 7.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Safety Considerations in Robot Design**

- ✅ **Safety is a design parameter**, not an add-on. This is the philosophical foundation of all robot safety engineering.
- ✅ **ISO 10218-1/-2** governs industrial robot safety; **ISO/TS 15066** governs collaborative robot safety.
- ✅ The **four collaborative methods** are: **SRMS, Hand Guiding, SSM, PFL**. Memorize all four with their use cases.
- ✅ The **hierarchy of controls** is: Inherent Safe Design → Safeguarding → Information for Use → **PPE (last)**.
- ✅ **Safety distance formula** (vertical light curtain): $S = K \cdot T + C$ where $K = 1600\ \text{mm/s}$, $C = 850\ \text{mm}$ (or actual reach if smaller).
- ✅ **Stop Categories** — 0 (uncontrolled), 1 (controlled then power removed), 2 (controlled, power retained).
- ✅ **Performance Levels** (PL a → PL e) are assigned per **ISO 13849-1**. E-stops and interlocked gates typically require **PL d, Category 3** minimum.
- ✅ **Kinetic energy** $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ — quantify hazard severity, not just speed.
- ✅ **Cobot PFL limit:** $\leq 50\ \text{N/cm}^2$ pressure, $\leq 140\ \text{N}$ transient force on hand.
- ✅ **E-stop must be hardwired** through a safety relay — it must **bypass** all PLC software.
- ✅ **Risk is iterative** — after every safeguard, re-evaluate. Safeguards can introduce new hazards.
- ✅ **Two standards for safety reliability:** ISO 13849-1 (PL, machinery) and IEC 61508 (SIL, process) — know which applies to your application.
- ✅ **Earthing impedance** must be $\mathbf{< 0.1\ \Omega}$; main disconnect must be lockable.
- ✅ **Category 4** monitoring of contactors detects *welded* faults via auxiliary feedback — required for the highest-integrity safety functions.
- ✅ **Fail-safe design** uses spring-set, *energize-to-release* brakes — they engage on power loss automatically.
- ✅ **Reach over/under** a light curtain is a common bypass — design the curtain's *height* and *mounting position* to defeat this.
- ✅ **Light curtain mounting** is critical: 300 mm minimum height for finger detection; 900 mm for full-body detection per ISO 13855.
- ✅ **Lockout-Tagout (LOTO)** is a mandatory procedural control for any maintenance activity on a robot cell.

---

*End of KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 Notes — Safety Considerations in Robot Design*
*Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Protocol*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Installation and Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT831 - SAFETY AND RELIABILITY IN ROBOTIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Installation and Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robot Installation** refers to the systematic, standards-compliant procedure of physically mounting, electrically connecting, mechanically calibrating, and functionally commissioning an industrial robot cell or work cell within a designated industrial environment, while strictly adhering to safety integrity level (SIL) and performance level (PL) requirements as defined in ISO 10218-1 and ISO 10218-2.

**Robot Operations** encompass the entire lifecycle of productive robot activity after commissioning, including normal production cycles, programming, teaching, maintenance, troubleshooting, and emergency response, all governed by documented safety procedures that ensure human-robot collaboration (HRC) or human-robot separation risks remain within tolerable risk thresholds as per ISO 12100 and ISO/TS 15066.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Installation and Operations is the bridge between *design-time safety* (covered in earlier modules) and *runtime safety* (covered in subsequent modules). KTU examiners frequently frame questions around "translating risk assessment into installation layout" — this is a high-weightage topic.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are setting up a **massive industrial mixer in a commercial bakery**:

- **Installation phase** = Choosing where the mixer stands, securing its bolts to the floor, wiring the power safely, testing the emergency stop, putting up protective barriers, and training the bakers on what NOT to touch while it's running.
- **Operations phase** = Daily use — turning it on, loading dough, cleaning it, knowing exactly when and how to hit the big red emergency stop, and what to do if someone gets their hand too close.

In a robot system, the **mixer** is the **robot arm**, the **bakers** are the **operators**, the **barriers** are **safety light curtains / fences**, and the **big red button** is the **Emergency Stop (E-Stop)** circuit. Installation makes sure everything is bolted down and safe BEFORE the first dough (or workpiece) goes in. Operations makes sure that safety is preserved EVERY DAY.

### 1.3 Key Physical Standards & Constants

The following standard metrics and safety thresholds are **mandatory** for KTU-level answers:

- **ISO 10218-1:2011** — Robots and robotic devices — Safety requirements for industrial robots — Part 1: Robots.
- **ISO 10218-2:2011** — Safety requirements for industrial robots — Part 2: Robot systems and integration.
- **ISO/TS 15066:2016** — Collaborative robots — Technical specification.
- **ISO 13849-1** — Safety-related parts of control systems.
- **ISO 13855:2010** — Positioning of safeguards with respect to the approach speeds of parts of the human body.
- **ANSI/RIA R15.06-2012** — American National Standard for Industrial Robots and Robot Systems — Safety Requirements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Hand-Arm Approach Speed:** **K = 1600 mm/s** (1.6 m/s) per ISO 13855.
> **Standard Whole-Body Approach Speed:** **K = 2000 mm/s** (2.0 m/s).
> **Performance Level required for most robot safety functions:** **PL d** (Category 3) or higher.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Installation Workflow — Structured Logic Steps

Installation is NOT a single event; it is a **6-stage gated process**. Each gate must be signed off before the next begins.

**Stage 1 — Site Survey & Layout Planning**
- Identify floor load capacity (robot bases transmit high dynamic forces, often **5–10 kN** peak).
- Map the **robot's maximum reach envelope** (the swept volume of all joints at full extension).
- Define **restricted space** (where the robot moves) vs. **operating space** (where operators can stand).
- Allocate zones for: controller cabinet, teach pendant, power panel, safety scanners, fencing.

**Stage 2 — Mechanical Mounting & Anchoring**
- Bolt the robot base to a **pedestal of sufficient rigidity** (concrete or steel).
- Vibration isolation pads reduce resonance with the floor.
- Levelling must be within the manufacturer's tolerance (typically **< 0.1 mm/m**).
- Tooling and end-effectors are mounted per the manufacturer's torque specs.

**Stage 3 — Electrical & Pneumatic Integration**
- Connect to a **dedicated power line** with a lockable disconnect (per IEC 60204-1).
- Earth/ground resistance must be **< 0.1 Ω** for safety circuits.
- **Protective Earth (PE)** bonding verified.
- Pneumatic lines include **lockout valves** and **pressure relief**.
- Cable routing separates power (high EMI) from signal (low voltage).

**Stage 4 — Safety System Commissioning**
- Install physical guards (fencing), light curtains, safety mats, or area scanners per the **risk assessment** output.
- Wire **Emergency Stop (E-Stop)** circuits — must be **dual-channel, force-guided contactor** type, **Category 1 stop** per IEC 60204-1.
- Configure **Safeguarded Stop (SS1/SS2)** functions in the safety PLC.
- Verify **Safety-rated I/O** mapping (e.g., PROFIsafe, CIP Safety, FSoE).

**Stage 5 — Functional Testing & Validation**
- Run **reduced-speed test cycles** in step mode.
- Verify each E-Stop physically halts all hazardous motion.
- Measure **stopping time** and **stopping distance** — these values feed into the safety distance calculation.
- Validate **muting, override, and hold-to-run** functions.

**Stage 6 — Documentation & Hand-over**
- Deliver the **Technical File** (per ISO 10218-2 Annex A).
- Sign the **Declaration of Conformity (DoC)**.
- Conduct **operator training** with documented competency records.

### 2.2 The Operations Workflow — Day-to-Day Safety Logic

Once installed, operations follow a strict lifecycle:

- **Normal Production Mode:** Operator outside the safeguarded space; robot runs autonomous cycles at rated speed; safety circuits are continuously monitored.
- **Teach/Program Mode:** Operator inside the safeguarded space with teach pendant; robot runs at **reduced speed (≤ 250 mm/s)** per ISO 10218-1; **hold-to-run** device is mandatory; **enabling device** (3-position) must be held in the middle position.
- **Maintenance Mode:** Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) procedure — energy isolation, verification of zero energy state, then maintenance work.
- **Abnormal/Emergency:** E-Stop activation → Category 1 stop (controlled, monitored) → energy removal → lockout → investigation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL:** The vertical pipe `|` is replaced by `\vert` to prevent breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Standard / Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Minimum Safety Distance (vertical reach over a safeguard) | $S = (K \times T) + C$ | mm | ISO 13855 |
| 2 | Approach speed (hand-arm) | $K = 1600$ | mm/s | ISO 13855 |
| 3 | Approach speed (whole body) | $K = 2000$ | mm/s | ISO 13855 |
| 4 | Total Stopping Time | $T = t_{r} + t_{b} + t_{s}$ | s | ISO 13855 |
| 5 | Stopping Distance | $d = v_{max} \times T$ | mm | Kinematics |
| 6 | Risk Reduction Factor | $R = P \times S$ | dimensionless | ISO 12100 |
| 7 | Performance Level (probability of dangerous failure per hour) | $PFH_{d} \leq 10^{-7}$ for PL e | 1/h | ISO 13849-1 |
| 8 | Mean Time To Dangerous Failure | $MTTF_{d}$ | years | ISO 13849-1 |
| 9 | Diagnostic Coverage | $DC$ | \% | ISO 13849-1 |
| 10 | Safety Integrity Level (probability of failure on demand) | $PFD = 10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$ | — | IEC 61508 |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Installation and Operations practices are the *backbone* of every robotic deployment worldwide — from automotive welding cells (where 6-axis robots operate alongside humans behind light curtains) to collaborative robot (cobot) workstations on assembly lines (where power-and-force-limiting enables human contact). Without rigorous installation and operational discipline, a robotic system becomes one of the most dangerous machines in a factory, capable of exerting forces exceeding several **kN** at high speed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Safety Distance per ISO 13855

The minimum safety distance is the single most important quantitative result in installation planning. It is the **derived expression** that determines where a safeguard (e.g., light curtain) must be placed relative to the hazard zone.

#### 3.1.1 Start with the Physical Model

Consider a human operator moving toward a hazard (the robot's reach envelope) at a constant approach speed $K$. The operator's motion must be detected by the safeguard in time for the robot to come to a **safe, controlled stop** before any part of the human body enters the hazard zone.

Let:
- $K$ = approach speed of the human body part toward the hazard (in mm/s)
- $T$ = total stopping time of the robot system (in seconds)
- $C$ = intrusion distance — the distance a body part can penetrate past the sensing field before the safeguard is triggered (in mm)
- $S$ = minimum safety distance from the safeguard to the hazard (in mm)

#### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Algebraic Derivation

**Step 1: Distance covered by the operator during the robot's stopping time**

The operator moves at speed $K$ for a duration equal to the total stopping time $T$. The distance the operator covers during this interval is:

$$d_{1} = K \times T$$

**Step 2: Add the intrusion distance**

After the safeguard is triggered, the operator's body part may have already moved an additional distance $C$ beyond the detection plane. The total minimum distance the operator can travel before being safely stopped is:

$$d_{total} = d_{1} + C = K \times T + C$$

**Step 3: Equate to minimum safety distance**

To guarantee that the operator cannot reach the hazard, the safeguard must be placed at a distance $S$ such that:

$$S = K \times T + C$$

#### 3.1.3 Generalised Form (Perpendicular Approach to a Vertical Light Curtain)

For a **vertical light curtain** with the operator approaching perpendicularly, ISO 13855 specifies the general formula:

$$S = (K \times T) + C$$

where:
- $C = 8 \times (d - 14)$ for light curtains, with $d$ = detection capability (resolution) in mm, and $C \geq 0$.
- For $d \leq 14$ mm, $C = 0$.

#### 3.1.4 Generalised Form (Parallel Approach)

For an approach **parallel** to the sensing field:

$$S = (K \times T) + (1200 \text{ mm} - 0.4 \times H)$$

where $H$ = height of the safeguard above the reference plane (850 mm to 1000 mm typical).

#### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Standard)

> **Problem:** An operator approaches a robot cell with a hand speed of **K = 1600 mm/s**. The total stopping time of the system is **T = 0.45 s**. A horizontal safety mat with detection capability **d = 30 mm** is used. The intrusion distance formula gives **C = 8 × (30 − 14) = 128 mm**. Calculate the minimum safety distance.

**Solution:**

$$S = (K \times T) + C$$

$$S = (1600 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.45 \text{ s}) + 128 \text{ mm}$$

$$S = 720 \text{ mm} + 128 \text{ mm}$$

$$S = 848 \text{ mm}$$

**Result:** The light curtain / safeguard must be placed at least **848 mm** from the nearest hazard point.

#### 3.1.6 Sensitivity Insight

Notice that $T$ has the **largest impact** on $S$ because $K$ is standardised. Reducing stopping time by 100 ms reduces $S$ by **160 mm** — this is why KTU and ISO emphasise **brake performance** as a critical design parameter.

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Stopping Time Components

The total stopping time $T$ is itself a sum:

$$T = t_{r} + t_{b} + t_{s}$$

where:
- $t_{r}$ = reaction time of the safeguard sensor (e.g., light curtain response)
- $t_{b}$ = reaction time of the safety control system (PLC scan + logic)
- $t_{s}$ = mechanical / electrical braking time of the robot (motor deceleration to zero velocity)

Each component is **measured empirically** during commissioning and documented in the validation report. The **largest value** in the worst case is used for the safety distance calculation (the *worst-case principle*).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Installation Pitfalls vs. Required Safeguard

| Installation Scenario | Common Pitfall | Required Safeguard (per ISO 10218) | Minimum PL |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operator loading parts into a press-loaded cell | Operator reaches over the fence | Light curtain at calculated $S$ | PL d |
| Cobot cell with shared workspace | No power-and-force-limiting configured | PFL mode + speed/separation monitoring | PL d |
| Teach pendant programming | Speed set too high | Hold-to-run + enabling device + reduced speed | PL d |
| Maintenance access | LOTO not performed | Padlock + tag + voltage check | PL c (administrative) |
| Emergency stop circuit | Single-channel E-Stop | Dual-channel, force-guided, Category 1 | PL e |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Installation Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Stage 1: Site Survey and Layout Planning] --> A2[Stage 2: Mechanical Mounting and Anchoring]
    A2 --> A3[Stage 3: Electrical and Pneumatic Integration]
    A3 --> A4[Stage 4: Safety System Commissioning]
    A4 --> A5[Stage 5: Functional Testing and Validation]
    A5 --> A6[Stage 6: Documentation and Hand-over]
    A6 --> A7[Operational Phase Begins]

    A1 -.-> S1[Output: Floor plan, reach envelope map]
    A2 -.-> S2[Output: Levelling report, torque logs]
    A3 -.-> S3[Output: Earth bond test, LOTO points]
    A4 -.-> S4[Output: Safety distance calculation]
    A5 -.-> S5[Output: Stop time and distance report]
    A6 -.-> S6[Output: Technical File, DoC, training records]

    A7 --> O1[Normal Production Mode]
    A7 --> O2[Teach and Program Mode]
    A7 --> O3[Maintenance Mode with LOTO]
    A7 --> O4[Emergency Stop and Reset]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Safety Subsystem Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Human Operator] -->|Approach| SF1[Light Curtain]
    H1 -->|Step on| SF2[Safety Mat]
    H1 -->|Press| SF3[E-Stop Button]
    H1 -->|Hold| SF4[Enabling Device on Teach Pendant]

    SF1 --> SS1[Safety PLC - Dual Channel]
    SF2 --> SS1
    SF3 --> SS1
    SF4 --> SS1

    SS1 -->|Safe Torque Off| RC1[Robot Controller]
    SS1 -->|Stop Cat 1| RC1
    SS1 -->|Reduced Speed| RC1

    RC1 --> MA1[Robot Manipulator 6-Axis]
    RC1 --> MA2[End Effector]

    SS1 -->|Status| HU1[Stack Light Red Yellow Green]
    SS1 -->|Event Log| HU2[Safety Historian]
```

### 4.3 Operational State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PwrOff
    PwrOff --> Initialising : Power On
    Initialising --> AutoMode : All Safeties OK
    Initialising --> Fault : Safety Check Failed
    Fault --> PwrOff : Reset and Acknowledge
    AutoMode --> TeachMode : Mode Selector to Teach
    TeachMode --> AutoMode : Mode Selector to Auto
    AutoMode --> EStop : E-Stop Pressed
    TeachMode --> EStop : E-Stop Pressed
    EStop --> Fault : Latch
    Fault --> Initialising : Acknowledge
    AutoMode --> Maintenance : LOTO Engaged
    TeachMode --> Maintenance : LOTO Engaged
    Maintenance --> PwrOff : Maintenance Complete
```

### 4.4 Safety Distance Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Approach Type?] --> Q2[Perpendicular to Light Curtain]
    Q1 --> Q3[Parallel to Light Curtain]
    Q1 --> Q4[Vertical Reach Over]

    Q2 --> F1[Formula S = K T plus C]
    Q3 --> F2[Formula S = K T plus 1200 minus 0.4 H]
    Q4 --> F3[Formula S = K T plus C with reach offset]

    F1 --> V1[Validate against ISO 13855 Table]
    F2 --> V1
    F3 --> V1
    V1 --> V2[Document in Risk Assessment File]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Minimum Safety Distance" as applied to the installation of industrial robot safeguards. State the standard reference for its calculation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The minimum safety distance is the minimum permissible separation between a safeguard (e.g., light curtain) and the identified hazard zone, calculated to ensure that any human intrusion is detected in time for the machine to reach a safe state before contact occurs. It is calculated using the standard **ISO 13855:2010**, which provides formulae based on the approach speed of the human body part and the total stopping time of the machine.

*(Marks: Definition = 2, Standard reference = 1)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List any three differences between "Normal Production Mode" and "Teach/Program Mode" in a robotic system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Speed:** Normal production runs at rated (full) speed; Teach mode runs at **reduced speed (≤ 250 mm/s)** per ISO 10218-1.
2. **Operator Location:** Normal production — operator outside safeguarded space; Teach mode — operator inside safeguarded space with teach pendant.
3. **Control Device:** Normal production uses start/stop pushbuttons; Teach mode requires an **enabling device (3-position hold-to-run)** that the operator must actively press in the middle position to permit motion.

*(Marks: 1 mark per valid difference, max 3)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the **6-stage installation workflow** for an industrial robot system as per ISO 10218-2, with emphasis on the Safety System Commissioning stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot cell uses a vertical light curtain with **detection capability d = 25 mm**. The hand approach speed is **K = 1600 mm/s**, the sensor reaction time is **15 ms**, the safety PLC scan time is **10 ms**, and the robot mechanical braking time is **380 ms**. Calculate:
   (i) Total stopping time T
   (ii) Intrusion distance C
   (iii) Minimum safety distance S **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

The 6-stage installation workflow per ISO 10218-2:

1. **Site Survey & Layout Planning** (1 mark): Identify floor loading, define robot reach envelope, map restricted vs. operating spaces, plan safety device positions.
2. **Mechanical Mounting** (1 mark): Anchor robot base to rigid pedestal, level within tolerance, mount end-effector.
3. **Electrical & Pneumatic Integration** (1 mark): Connect to dedicated power with lockable disconnect, verify earth bonding (< 0.1 Ω), install LOTO points.
4. **Safety System Commissioning** (2 marks): Install guards/light curtains/mats/scanners, wire dual-channel E-Stop circuits, configure safety PLC, **calculate safety distance S**, set up muting/override logic.
5. **Functional Testing & Validation** (1 mark): Reduced-speed step tests, verify each E-Stop halts motion, measure stopping time/distance, document results.
6. **Documentation & Hand-over** (1 mark): Compile Technical File, sign DoC, conduct operator training with competency records.

**(b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Total Stopping Time T:**

$$T = t_{r} + t_{b} + t_{s}$$

$$T = 15 \text{ ms} + 10 \text{ ms} + 380 \text{ ms} = 405 \text{ ms} = 0.405 \text{ s}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Intrusion Distance C:**

Since $d = 25$ mm and $d > 14$ mm:

$$C = 8 \times (d - 14) = 8 \times (25 - 14) = 8 \times 11 = 88 \text{ mm}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Minimum Safety Distance S:**

$$S = (K \times T) + C$$

$$S = (1600 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.405 \text{ s}) + 88 \text{ mm}$$

$$S = 648 \text{ mm} + 88 \text{ mm} = 736 \text{ mm}$$

**Result:** S = **736 mm** (1 mark for formula, 2 marks for substitution and final answer).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Discuss the **operational safety modes** of an industrial robot, including Normal Production, Teach/Program, Maintenance (LOTO), and Emergency Stop modes. For each mode, state the operator location, the speed/control logic, and the ISO reference. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During installation, the calculated safety distance is **S = 1200 mm** using the standard hand-arm approach speed. If the risk assessment team considers a whole-body approach possible, recalculate the safety distance for the same stopping time T = 0.5 s and intrusion distance C = 200 mm. State which scenario (hand-arm vs. whole-body) yields a more conservative (larger) safety distance and explain the engineering implication. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks (1.75 marks per mode)**

| Mode | Operator Location | Speed/Control | ISO Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Normal Production | Outside safeguarded space | Rated full speed; auto cycle | ISO 10218-1 §5.2 |
| Teach/Program | Inside safeguarded space | Reduced speed (≤ 250 mm/s); enabling device | ISO 10218-1 §5.3 |
| Maintenance (LOTO) | Inside after isolation | Zero energy; padlock + tag | ISO 10218-1 §5.5 / IEC 60204-1 |
| Emergency Stop | Anywhere accessible | Category 1 stop (controlled, then energy removal) | IEC 60204-1 §9.2.5.4 / ISO 13850 |

**(b) — 7 marks**

For **whole-body approach**, K = 2000 mm/s (per ISO 13855).

$$S_{wholebody} = (K \times T) + C$$

$$S_{wholebody} = (2000 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.5 \text{ s}) + 200 \text{ mm}$$

$$S_{wholebody} = 1000 \text{ mm} + 200 \text{ mm} = 1200 \text{ mm}$$

**Comparison:**
- Hand-arm: $S = (1600 \times 0.5) + 200 = 800 + 200 = 1000$ mm
- Whole-body: $S = 1200$ mm
- **The whole-body scenario yields the more conservative (larger) safety distance.** **(3 marks for calculation, 2 marks for comparison, 2 marks for engineering implication)**

**Engineering Implication:** Designers must always use the **worst-case approach speed** (i.e., K = 2000 mm/s if whole-body access is possible). Underestimating K can lead to a safeguard placed too close to the hazard, resulting in the operator reaching the hazard zone before the robot has fully stopped — a potentially fatal failure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert ms to s** when computing T — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for unit inconsistency.
> 2. **Using K = 1600 mm/s by default** even when the question specifies whole-body access — read the question carefully.
> 3. **Forgetting the intrusion distance C** in the safety distance formula — this loses up to **2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing PL d with SIL 2** — they are different standards (ISO 13849-1 vs. IEC 61508). Be precise.
> 5. **Omitting the enabling device** in Teach mode answers — examiners specifically look for the *3-position hold-to-run* device.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Safety distance formula:** $S = K \times T + C$ — this is the **single most important equation** for this topic.
- **Hand-arm approach speed:** K = **1600 mm/s**; **Whole-body approach speed:** K = **2000 mm/s**.
- **Total stopping time T = sensor reaction + PLC scan + mechanical braking.**
- **Intrusion distance C = 8 × (d − 14)** for light curtains, where d > 14 mm; **C = 0** if d ≤ 14 mm.
- **6-stage installation workflow:** Site Survey → Mechanical → Electrical → Safety Commissioning → Testing → Documentation.
- **4 operational modes:** Normal Production, Teach (reduced speed + enabling device), Maintenance (LOTO), Emergency Stop.
- **Teach mode speed limit:** ≤ **250 mm/s** per ISO 10218-1.
- **E-Stop type:** Dual-channel, force-guided, Category 1 stop per IEC 60204-1.
- **Performance Level target:** PL d (Category 3) for most robot safety functions; **PL e** for E-Stop circuits.
- **Earth bond resistance:** < **0.1 Ω** for safety circuits.
- **LOTO** = Lockout/Tagout — mandatory before any maintenance.
- **Documentation deliverables:** Technical File (per ISO 10218-2 Annex A), Declaration of Conformity, training records.
- **Always use the worst-case value of T** (largest measured stopping time) in safety distance calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Safeguard Approaches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT831 - SAFETY AND RELIABILITY IN ROBOTIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robot Safeguard Approaches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Robot Safeguard Approaches** refer to the integrated set of engineered protective measures, control strategies, and operational procedures designed to eliminate or reduce risks to human operators, other machinery, and the robot system itself during all phases of operation—including normal production, programming, maintenance, and fault conditions. As per ISO 10218-1/-2 and ISO/TS 15066, these approaches form the **hierarchical risk reduction stack** in a robot cell, ranging from inherently safe design (elimination) to administrative controls (warnings, training, procedures).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Robot Safeguard Approaches are categorized into **Perimeter Safeguarding (Hard Guards)**, **Presence-Sensing Safeguarding (PSS)**, and **Collaborative Operation Safeguarding (COBOTS)**. Each category has specific risk reduction metrics tied to ISO 13849-1 (Performance Level) and IEC 62061 (Safety Integrity Level).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Bank Vault" Concept

Imagine a high-security bank vault protecting gold bars:

- **Inherent Safety** = The vault was *built* without windows (you cannot break what does not exist).
- **Hard Guards** = A 1-foot thick concrete wall with one locked door (a physical barrier).
- **Presence-Sensing** = Motion sensors, pressure mats, and laser tripwires that *detect* the thief and trigger an alarm.
- **Administrative Controls** = "Trespassers will be prosecuted" signs and security guard patrols.

A robot safeguard system works **exactly** the same way: instead of preventing a thief from reaching gold, it prevents a human hand, limb, or body from reaching a moving robot arm or end-effector. The hierarchy follows the **STOP Principle** of the **INHERENTLY SAFER DESIGN HIERARCHY** (Eliminate → Substitute → Engineer → Administer).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics in Robot Safeguarding (ISO 13849-1):**
> - **Performance Level (PLr)**: Discrete safety reliability level — ranges from **PL a** (lowest) to **PL e** (highest).
> - **Category (B, 1, 2, 3, 4)**: Structural requirement for resistance to faults.
> - **MTTFd (Mean Time To Dangerous Failure)**: Low = 3 yrs, Medium = 10 yrs, High = 30 yrs.
> - **PFHd (Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour)**: For PL e, PFHd must be **between 10⁻⁸ and 10⁻⁷** per hour.
> - **DCavg (Diagnostic Coverage)**: Average percentage of dangerous failures detected.

### 1.3 Why Robot Safeguarding is a Non-Negotiable Engineering Discipline

Industrial robots operate at speeds up to **2 m/s** with payloads exceeding **1000 kg** and force outputs that can easily exceed **250 N** on contact. ISO/TS 15066 defines the **quasi-static** and **transient** contact force limits for collaborative robot operation, with permissible pressures on the human body limited to **140 N/cm²** for the hand and **110 N/cm²** for the chest region. Without structured safeguard approaches, the consequences of a robot-human collision range from minor bruising to fatal crushing injuries.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Hierarchy of Robot Safeguard Approaches

Robot safeguarding follows a **layered defense-in-depth model** codified in **ANSI/RIA R15.06-2012** and **ISO 10218-1:2011 / ISO 10218-2:2011**. The four primary safeguard approach categories are:

#### **Tier 1: Inherent Safety by Design (Elimination)**

- Reduce hazardous energy *at the source*.
- Examples: Reduce maximum robot speed, use soft/rounded end-effectors, limit reach envelope, use lightweight materials, gravity-balanced arms.
- **Most effective** because it removes the hazard entirely.

#### **Tier 2: Engineering Controls (Guarding & Separation)**

- **Perimeter Safeguarding (Hard Guards)**:
  - Fixed physical barriers (fencing, cages, walls).
  - Interlocked guards with guard-locking switches (per ISO 14119).
  - Minimum safety distance calculated using **ISO 13855** formula.
- **Awareness Barriers**:
  - Painted yellow lines on the floor (no physical barrier, but demarcates safe zones).
  - Used for low-risk, slow-speed robots.

#### **Tier 3: Presence-Sensing Safeguards (PSS)**

These are the **most common in modern cells** and are of four sub-types:

| PSS Sub-Type | Operating Principle | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Light Curtains (AOPD)** | Array of infrared beams; trip on beam break | Perimeter access, muting during material transfer |
| **Safety Mats (Pressure-Sensitive)** | Short-circuit when stepped on | Floor-level zone protection |
| **Laser Scanners (Safety LIDAR)** | Time-of-flight; 2D/3D field monitoring | Mobile robots, dynamic zones |
| **Safety Camera Systems (SLC)** | 3D vision with depth mapping | Collaborative cells, body tracking |

#### **Tier 4: Collaborative Operation Safeguards (COBOTS)**

Per **ISO/TS 15066:2016**, four collaborative modes exist:

1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)** — Robot halts when human enters collaborative workspace.
2. **Hand Guiding (HG)** — Operator directly grasps the robot; teach pendant becomes the robot.
3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** — Robot speed adapts to maintain minimum protective distance.
4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** — Robot force/torque is intrinsically limited to biomechanical thresholds.

### 2.2 The Risk Graph & Performance Level Determination

The **Risk Graph Method** of **ISO 13849-1** determines the required Performance Level (PLr) by evaluating four severity parameters:

- **S** = Severity of injury (S1 = light, S2 = serious/fatal)
- **F** = Frequency of exposure to hazard (F1 = rare, F2 = frequent)
- **P** = Possibility of avoidance (P1 = possible, P2 = hardly possible)
- **C** = Probability of occurrence of hazardous event (C1 = low, C2 = high)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Concept:** Each safeguarding method has a **verified PL (PL)** calculated from the components used. The PL **must be ≥ PLr**; otherwise the safeguard is inadequate and the cell is non-compliant. This is a frequent 7-mark question.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimum Safety Distance (Light Curtain) | $S = K \cdot T + C$ | mm | ISO 13855 |
| Minimum Safety Distance (Floor Mats) | $S = 1200 \cdot (t_1 + t_2) + 1200 \cdot d$ | mm | ISO 13855 |
| Separation Distance (SSM Mode) | $S_p = v_h \cdot T_R + v_r \cdot T_R + S_h + Z_d + Z_r$ | mm | ISO/TS 15066 |
| Stopping Time | $T_s = T_r + T_s$ | s | Total system stopping time |
| PFHd Calculation (Category 1) | $PFH_d = \frac{1}{MTTF_d}$ | h⁻¹ | For single-channel non-redundant |
| PFHd Calculation (Category 3, DC) | $PFH_d = \lambda_D^2 \cdot T \cdot DC_{avg}$ | h⁻¹ | For redundant + diagnostic |
| Risk Score | $R = S \cdot F \cdot P \cdot C$ | unitless | Risk Graph output |
| Biomechanical Limit (PFL) | $F_{max} = 140 \text{ N/cm}^2$ (hand) | N/cm² | ISO/TS 15066 |
| MTTFd to PL Translation | $MTTF_d \geq 100 \text{ yrs} \Rightarrow \text{PL e}$ | years | ISO 13849-1 |
| Diagnostic Coverage | $DC = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{Dtotal}}$ | ratio (0–1) | ISO 13849-1 Table 5 |

> **Legend:** $K$ = approach speed (1600 mm/s for walking), $T$ = total stopping time, $C$ = intrusion depth, $v_h$ = human speed, $v_r$ = robot speed, $S_h$ = operator displacement, $Z_d$ / $Z_r$ = position uncertainty terms.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Robot safeguard approaches are non-negotiable in **automotive welding cells** (FANUC, KUKA), **pharmaceutical pick-and-place** (ABB YuMi), **Cobots in SME manufacturing** (Universal Robots UR10e), and **AMR/AGV warehouses** (MiR, Fetch). They enable **productivity gains** by allowing humans to work *near* robots rather than isolating them entirely — a paradigm shift enabled by structured safeguard approaches.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Safety Distance for a Light Curtain (Per ISO 13855)

**Problem Setup:** A vertical light curtain is installed as a perimeter safeguard. Determine the minimum distance $S$ (in mm) at which it must be placed from the hazard zone, given the following operational parameters:

- Approach speed of operator: $K = 1600$ mm/s (walking speed per ISO 13855)
- Total system stopping time: $T = 250$ ms $= 0.25$ s
- Intrusion depth into the light curtain (reach distance): $C = 850$ mm

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

The general minimum safety distance formula for vertical light curtains (perpendicular approach) is:

$$
S = K \cdot T + C
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
S = 1600 \cdot 0.25 + 850
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the kinetic term.**

$$
K \cdot T = 1600 \cdot 0.25 = 400 \text{ mm}
$$

This represents the distance the operator travels during the time it takes the system to detect the intrusion and bring the robot to a safe stop.

**Step 4 — Add the intrusion depth term.**

$$
S = 400 + 850 = 1250 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Result.**

$$
\boxed{S = 1250 \text{ mm} = 1.25 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation.** The light curtain must be installed no closer than **1.25 m** from the hazard point. If a value of $C$ greater than **500 mm** is used (i.e., reach *over* the curtain), the formula switches to a more complex one involving $\sqrt{K^2 T^2 + C^2}$ per ISO 13855 Table 1.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Separation Distance for Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)

**Problem Setup:** In a collaborative cell using SSM, calculate the minimum protective separation distance $S_p$.

**Given:**
- Human approach speed: $v_h = 1600$ mm/s
- Robot velocity toward operator: $v_r = 250$ mm/s
- Robot reaction time: $T_R = 100$ ms $= 0.1$ s
- Operator body displacement (hand movement): $S_h = 250$ mm
- Position uncertainty of detection system: $Z_d = 50$ mm
- Robot position uncertainty: $Z_r = 30$ mm

**Step 1 — Governing Equation (ISO/TS 15066):**

$$
S_p = v_h \cdot T_R + v_r \cdot T_R + S_h + Z_d + Z_r
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the human motion during reaction.**

$$
v_h \cdot T_R = 1600 \cdot 0.1 = 160 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the robot motion during reaction.**

$$
v_r \cdot T_R = 250 \cdot 0.1 = 25 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum all components.**

$$
S_p = 160 + 25 + 250 + 50 + 30
$$

**Step 5 — Result.**

$$
\boxed{S_p = 515 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation.** The robot's speed controller must maintain the operator's distance at no less than **515 mm**. As the operator approaches, robot speed is dynamically reduced (or stopped) to preserve this separation.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: PFHd for a Category 3 Safety Circuit (Redundant with Diagnostics)

**Problem Setup:** A dual-channel safety relay with diagnostic coverage is used. Compute $PFH_d$.

**Given:**
- Dangerous failure rate per channel: $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ h⁻¹
- Diagnostic Coverage: $DC_{avg} = 0.99$ (99%)
- Diagnostic test interval: $T = 8$ h

**Step 1 — Governing Formula (Category 3, single fault tolerant):**

$$
PFH_d = \lambda_D^2 \cdot T \cdot DC_{avg}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the squared failure rate.**

$$
\lambda_D^2 = (5 \times 10^{-7})^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-13} \text{ h}^{-2}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by diagnostic test interval.**

$$
\lambda_D^2 \cdot T = 2.5 \times 10^{-13} \cdot 8 = 2.0 \times 10^{-12} \text{ h}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by DCavg.**

$$
PFH_d = 2.0 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 0.99 = 1.98 \times 10^{-12} \text{ h}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Result.**

$$
\boxed{PFH_d \approx 2 \times 10^{-12} \text{ h}^{-1}}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare to PL e threshold.** For **PL e**, $PFH_d$ must lie in $[10^{-8}, 10^{-7}]$ h⁻¹. Our value of $2 \times 10^{-12}$ h⁻¹ is **two orders of magnitude better than PL e** — a Category 4 design, confirming the safety circuit is appropriate for high-risk applications.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Safeguard Approach to Application

| Safeguard Approach | Performance Level Typical | Best For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hard Guard (Fence + Interlock) | PL d / PL e | High-speed, high-payload cells | Reduces floor space, slows workflow |
| Light Curtain (AOPD) | PL e (Cat 4) | Frequent operator entry | Bypassed by reach-over |
| Safety Mat | PL c / PL d | Large floor zones, low-cycle | Wear, mechanical fatigue |
| Laser Scanner (SLC) | PL d / PL e | Mobile robots, dynamic zones | Cost, software complexity |
| PFL Cobot | PL d (limited) | True collaboration, SME use | Force/payload severely limited |
| SSM Cobot | PL d | Shared workspace | Requires accurate tracking |
| SRMS | PL e | Tool change, hand-over | No simultaneous operation |
| Hand Guiding | PL d | Teaching, low-volume | Operator fatigue |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Safeguard Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Robot Hazard Source] --> B[Layer 1: Inherent Safe Design]
    B --> B1[Reduce Speed]
    B --> B2[Soft End Effector]
    B --> B3[Limit Reach Envelope]
    A --> C[Layer 2: Engineering Controls]
    C --> C1[Hard Guard Fence]
    C --> C2[Interlocked Door]
    C --> C3[Safety Distance S]
    A --> D[Layer 3: Presence Sensing Safeguards]
    D --> D1[Light Curtain AOPD]
    D --> D2[Safety Mat]
    D --> D3[Laser Scanner LIDAR]
    D --> D4[3D Safety Camera]
    A --> E[Layer 4: Collaborative Operation]
    E --> E1[SRMS Stop]
    E --> E2[Hand Guiding]
    E --> E3[Speed and Separation Monitoring]
    E --> E4[Power and Force Limiting]
    A --> F[Layer 5: Administrative Controls]
    F --> F1[Lockout Tagout]
    F --> F2[Training]
    F --> F3[Painted Floor Zones]
    F --> F4[E Stop Devices]
```

### 4.2 Safety Distance Calculation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[Identify Hazard Zone] --> stepB[Measure Total Stopping Time T]
    stepB --> stepC[Determine Approach Speed K]
    stepC --> stepD[Determine Intrusion Depth C]
    stepD --> stepE[Apply S equals K times T plus C]
    stepE --> stepF{Is Reach Over greater than 500 mm}
    stepF --> stepG[Use S equals square root of K squared T squared plus C squared]
    stepF --> stepH[Use Standard Linear Formula]
    G --> stepI[Final Minimum Safety Distance]
    H --> stepI[Final Minimum Safety Distance]
```

### 4.3 Risk Graph to Performance Level Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Start Risk Assessment] --> node2[Evaluate Injury Severity S]
    node2 --> node3[Evaluate Exposure Frequency F]
    node3 --> node4[Evaluate Avoidance Possibility P]
    node4 --> node5[Evaluate Occurrence Probability C]
    node5 --> node6[Look Up Risk Graph ISO 13849]
    node6 --> node7[Determine Required PLr]
    node7 --> node8[Select Safeguard Approach]
    node8 --> node9[Verify PL achieved greater or equal PLr]
    node9 --> node10[Compliant Robot Cell]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Interpretation:** The diagrams above model the *defense-in-depth safeguard hierarchy* and the *risk-driven performance level selection process*. In the exam, students may be asked to draw or explain such hierarchies for full marks (often 7–8 marks on a 14-mark question).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Performance Level (PL)" as per ISO 13849-1. List the five discrete levels.**

**Model Answer:**
Performance Level (PL) is a discrete level used to specify the ability of safety-related parts of control systems (SRP/CS) to perform a safety function under foreseeable conditions. The five levels per ISO 13849-1 are:
**PL a, PL b, PL c, PL d, PL e**, where **PL a** has the lowest reliability and **PL e** has the highest. PL e corresponds to a PFHd in the range $[10^{-8}, 10^{-7}]$ per hour. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List the four collaborative operation modes defined in ISO/TS 15066.**

**Model Answer:**
The four collaborative operation modes per ISO/TS 15066:2016 are:
1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)**
2. **Hand Guiding (HG)**
3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)**
4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** **(3 marks)**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) Explain in detail the four primary categories of robot safeguard approaches with suitable industrial examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The four primary categories of robot safeguard approaches, organized in the order of the **Inherently Safer Design Hierarchy**, are:

**1. Inherent Safety by Design:** Hazards are eliminated or reduced at the source. Examples include: reducing maximum robot speed, using soft/compliant end-effectors, limiting the reach envelope, gravity-balancing arm designs, and using lightweight composite links. **Industrial example:** A delta robot designed with limited reach to prevent operator reach-into the work envelope. **(1.5 marks)**

**2. Engineering Controls — Perimeter Safeguarding (Hard Guards):** Physical barriers such as fixed fencing, interlocked gates, and fixed walls are installed around the robot cell. The safety distance is calculated per ISO 13855. Interlocks (per ISO 14119) ensure the robot stops if the gate opens. **Industrial example:** A welding cell enclosed by a 2 m weld mesh fence with interlocked sliding gate. **(2 marks)**

**3. Presence-Sensing Safeguards (PSS):** Electro-sensitive protective devices that detect human presence. Sub-types include light curtains (AOPD), safety mats, laser scanners (safety LIDAR), and 3D safety cameras. **Industrial example:** A SICK light curtain installed at the perimeter of a palletizing cell, muting during material transfer. **(2 marks)**

**4. Collaborative Operation Safeguards (COBOTS):** Four modes per ISO/TS 15066 — SRMS, Hand Guiding, SSM, PFL. **Industrial example:** A Universal Robots UR10e operating in PFL mode for screw-driving alongside an operator. **(1.5 marks)**

---

**(b) With a neat sketch, explain the components and operation of a Safety Light Curtain (AOPD). How is the minimum safety distance determined? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Components of an AOPD (Active Optoelectronic Protective Device):**
- **Emitter Column:** Contains an array of infrared LEDs producing parallel light beams.
- **Receiver Column:** Contains photodiodes/phototransistors aligned with the emitter.
- **Control Unit (Safety Relay / Safety PLC):** Evaluates beam status; triggers stop on interruption.
- **Power Supply & Diagnostics Module.**
- **Muting Sensors (optional):** Allow beam bypass during material entry/exit.

**Operation:** The emitter projects a series of parallel infrared beams to the receiver. If *any* single beam is broken (e.g., by an operator's hand), the receiver sends a signal to the safety control system to initiate a Category 0 or Category 1 stop of the robot.

**Minimum Safety Distance (per ISO 13855):**

$$
S = K \cdot T + C
$$

where:
- $S$ = minimum safety distance (mm)
- $K$ = approach speed of operator (typically **1600 mm/s** for walking)
- $T$ = total system stopping time (s)
- $C$ = intrusion depth into the light curtain (mm)

**Worked numerical:** If $K = 1600$ mm/s, $T = 0.3$ s, $C = 700$ mm, then $S = 1600 \cdot 0.3 + 700 = 1180$ mm. **(7 marks — full distribution: 2 marks components, 2 marks operation, 2 marks formula, 1 mark numerical)**

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) Describe the Risk Graph Method of ISO 13849-1 for determining the required Performance Level (PLr) of a robot safety system. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Risk Graph Method evaluates four severity parameters to derive the required Performance Level (PLr):

1. **S — Severity of Injury:** S1 (light, reversible) or S2 (serious, irreversible, fatal).
2. **F — Frequency of Exposure to Hazard:** F1 (rare, less than once per shift) or F2 (frequent, once or more per shift).
3. **P — Possibility of Avoidance:** P1 (possible, operator can escape) or P2 (impossible, e.g., trapping).
4. **C — Probability of Hazardous Event Occurrence:** C1 (very low) or C2 (high).

Each parameter is assigned a binary value, and the resulting combination is mapped to a PLr in the range PL a to PL e using the risk graph. The chosen safeguard approach must achieve a verified PL ≥ PLr. **Industrial example:** A high-speed palletizing robot (S2, F2, P1, C2) would typically require **PL d or PL e**. **(7 marks — 1.5 marks per parameter + 1 mark graph mapping + 1 mark industrial example)**

---

**(b) Explain the four collaborative operation modes of ISO/TS 15066 with appropriate diagrams. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS):** When the operator enters the collaborative workspace, a safety-rated signal stops the robot, and the system monitors the stop. Robot resumes only after operator exits. **(1.5 marks)**

**2. Hand Guiding (HG):** The operator grasps an enabling device on the robot arm and physically moves it. The robot only moves under direct force. The enable switch and speed monitoring are required. **(1.5 marks)**

**3. Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM):** The robot dynamically adjusts its speed based on the operator's distance. Separation distance: $S_p = v_h T_R + v_r T_R + S_h + Z_d + Z_r$. **(2 marks)**

**4. Power and Force Limiting (PFL):** Robot force, torque, and speed are intrinsically limited to biomechanical thresholds (e.g., 140 N/cm² for hand contact). Requires inherently safe robot design with force/torque sensors. **(2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in formula substitutions** (e.g., converting $T$ from ms to s).
> 2. **Confusing PLr (Required) with PL (Achieved)** — both must be stated and compared.
> 3. **Omitting a diagram in 7-mark questions** — at least a labeled block diagram is mandatory.
> 4. **Confusing Category 3 vs Category 4** — Category 3 is single-fault tolerant; Category 4 tolerates *accumulation* of faults.
> 5. **Quoting PFHd thresholds incorrectly** — PL d is $[10^{-7}, 10^{-6}]$ h⁻¹, NOT $[10^{-8}, 10^{-7}]$.
> 6. **Forgetting ISO/TS 15066's role** — many students skip this in PFL mode questions.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Robot Safeguard Approaches follow the **INHERENTLY SAFER DESIGN HIERARCHY**: Elimination → Engineering → PSS → Administrative.
- ✅ **Three pillars:** ISO 10218-1/-2 (industrial robots), ISO/TS 15066 (collaborative), ISO 13849-1 (safety control reliability).
- ✅ **Four Collaborative Modes (ISO/TS 15066):** SRMS, Hand Guiding, SSM, PFL — memorize cold.
- ✅ **Minimum Safety Distance (Light Curtain):** $S = K \cdot T + C$ — approach speed × stopping time + intrusion depth.
- ✅ **SSM Separation Distance:** $S_p = v_h T_R + v_r T_R + S_h + Z_d + Z_r$.
- ✅ **PFHd for Cat 3:** $PFH_d = \lambda_D^2 \cdot T \cdot DC_{avg}$.
- ✅ **PL e threshold:** PFHd between $10^{-8}$ and $10^{-7}$ per hour.
- ✅ **MTTFd values:** Low = 3 yrs, Medium = 10 yrs, High = 30 yrs (ISO 13849-1 Table 4).
- ✅ **Biomechanical force limit (hand):** **140 N/cm²** — frequently asked in numerical.
- ✅ **Always state both PLr and PL** in long answers; showing the comparison earns full marks.
- ✅ **PFL is the most stringent** for true collaboration — limited to low-payload, low-speed tasks.
- ✅ **Hard guards + interlocks** still dominate traditional high-payload cells; cobots are a paradigm shift, not a replacement.

> **Final Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question, structure your answer as: **(i) Definition/Concept → (ii) Standard Reference → (iii) Mathematical Formulation → (iv) Worked Numerical → (v) Industrial Example → (vi) Diagram/Block Schematic.** This KTU-validated structure typically fetches 85–100% marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot-Related Safety Problems Causing Weak Points in Planning, Design, and Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT831 - SAFETY AND RELIABILITY IN ROBOTIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robot-Related Safety Problems Causing Weak Points in Planning, Design, and Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robot-Related Safety Problems** are defined as the systematic vulnerabilities, latent faults, and design deficiencies that manifest across the entire robot system lifecycle (planning, design, and operation) and that compromise the safety of human operators, co-workers, and surrounding equipment. According to KTU 2024 scheme nomenclature, these problems are categorized as **Weak Points** — critical failure nodes where a hazard event can directly translate into an accident, injury, or fatality.

The three canonical lifecycle phases where weak points originate are:

- **Planning Phase** — Conceptual mistakes made before any hardware exists (workplace layout, task allocation, risk classification).
- **Design Phase** — Engineering-level oversights embedded into mechanical, electrical, control, and software subsystems.
- **Operation Phase** — Behavioral, procedural, and maintenance-induced failures that arise once the robot is deployed on the shop floor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Syllabus Highlight):**
> *Robot-related safety problems causing weak points refer to those inherent or introduced flaws during any of the three lifecycle phases that reduce the inherent safety margin of the robotic cell, making it vulnerable to uncontrolled energy release, unintended motion, or hazardous human-machine interaction.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are constructing a **high-rise building** in Kerala during the monsoon season:

- **Planning Weak Point** = You forgot to check the soil type (laterite vs. clay) before drawing the foundation plan. The building *looks* fine on paper, but the ground will not hold it.
- **Design Weak Point** = You used sub-standard rebar or forgot to include a lightning conductor. The structure is now physically *weak* even if the ground is perfect.
- **Operation Weak Point** = You allow workers to remove a support pillar to "create space" without engineering approval. The building collapses during use.

A robot cell is identical. **Planning** decides *where* the robot works and *who* goes near it. **Design** decides *how* the hardware protects people. **Operation** decides *whether* humans follow the safety rules every single day. A failure in any one of the three creates a **weak point** — a single point of failure where an accident can be triggered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Safety Metric (Bolded Constant):**
> The internationally accepted risk reduction target is **Risk = Severity × Probability ≤ Tolerable Threshold**, where Severity and Probability are normalized categorical variables (typically on a 1–5 scale as per ISO 12100 and ANSI/RIA R15.06).

## 1.3 KTU Course Outcome Mapping (CO1)

> [!NOTE]
> This topic primarily maps to **CO1**: *Identify and analyze safety hazards and weak points associated with industrial robotic systems across planning, design, and operational lifecycle stages.* It belongs to **Cognitive Level L2 (Understand) and L4 (Analyze)** under Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phase-Wise Decomposition of Robot Safety Weak Points

The following structured logic decomposes the topic into the three lifecycle phases recognized by KTU 2024.

### 2.1.1 Weak Points During the **Planning Phase**

These are *latent* weak points — they do not exist physically yet, but are written into the cell specification document.

- **Inadequate Risk Assessment**: Failure to perform a formal hazard analysis (e.g., HAZOP, FMEA) before procurement.
- **Improper Workplace Layout**: Robot reach envelope overlaps with pedestrian walkways, operator stations, or maintenance zones.
- **Undefined Collaborative Zones**: No clear demarcation between **Collaborative Workspace** and **Restricted Workspace** as defined in ISO/TS 15066.
- **Missing Emergency Stop Philosophy**: No topographical plan for E-stop reachability (operators must reach an E-stop within **0.5 seconds** of an emergency).
- **Wrong Robot Selection**: Choosing a high-payload industrial robot (e.g., 200 kg) for a delicate assembly task that requires collaborative operation.
- **Insufficient Task Allocation Analysis**: Failure to decide which tasks are *human-exclusive*, *robot-exclusive*, or *human-robot shared*.

### 2.1.2 Weak Points During the **Design Phase**

These are *engineered* weak points — flaws physically built into the system.

- **Mechanical Design Deficiencies**:
  - **Pinch Points** at joint interfaces where fingers or clothing can be trapped.
  - **Sharp Edges** on end-effectors or structural members.
  - **Stored Energy Hazards** in hydraulic / pneumatic accumulators.
  - **Unguarded Rotating Shafts** or exposed drive mechanisms.
- **Electrical Design Deficiencies**:
  - Single-channel E-stop wiring (no redundancy).
  - Inadequate insulation or grounding, leading to **electrocution** hazards.
  - Missing **lockout-tagout (LOTO)** isolation points.
- **Control System Vulnerabilities**:
  - Software bugs in motion planning algorithms.
  - Single-point-of-failure in the safety-rated PLC.
  - Sensor blind spots (e.g., blind corners not covered by safety scanners).
  - No **watchdog timer** to detect controller hang.
- **Safety System Omissions**:
  - Absence of **light curtains**, **safety mats**, or **area scanners**.
  - No **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** classification for safety functions.
  - Absence of **speed and separation monitoring (SSM)** in collaborative cells.

### 2.1.3 Weak Points During the **Operation Phase**

These are *behavioral* weak points — the system was sound on paper, but human factors and operational realities degrade safety.

- **Operator Training Deficiency**: Workers unaware of robot's full reach, programmed speeds, or E-stop locations.
- **Safety Bypassing**: Operators deliberately overriding interlocks to speed up production.
- **Maintenance During Operation**: Performing tool changes, lubrication, or cleaning while the robot is energized.
- **Program Modification Errors**: Unverified code edits that disable safety checks.
- **Environmental Changes**: Introduction of reflective surfaces (confuse light curtains), new obstacles, or lighting changes (affect vision systems).
- **Unanticipated Human Behavior**: Workers entering the cell for "quick" checks without following lockout procedures.
- **Degraded Hardware**: Worn-out grippers, frayed cables, misaligned sensors that are not replaced proactively.

## 2.2 The 5-Phase Causal Chain of a Robot Accident

The textbook reasoning behind every robot accident follows this deterministic chain:

1. **Hazard Source** exists in the cell (e.g., high-speed rotating arm).
2. **Initiating Event** triggers the hazard (e.g., software glitch → arm moves unexpectedly).
3. **Weak Point** allows the hazard to propagate (e.g., missing safety fence, no light curtain).
4. **Person-at-Risk** is exposed (e.g., operator inside the cell during unexpected motion).
5. **Injury or Damage** results (e.g., crushing injury, fatality).

> **The "Why"**: A weak point in **any** of the three lifecycle phases (planning, design, operation) is what allows step 3 to occur. If weak points are eliminated at every phase, the causal chain is broken.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **examination-ready cheat sheet** for quantitative risk assessment in this topic. Master these equations — they appear frequently in 14-mark derivations.

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Meaning | Standard / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Risk Score** | $R = S \times P$ | $S$ = Severity (1–5), $P$ = Probability (1–5) | Dimensionless (ISO 12100) |
| **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** | $RRF = \dfrac{R_{\text{initial}}}{R_{\text{residual}}}$ | Ratio of risk before vs. after safeguard | Dimensionless, must be $\geq 1$ |
| **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** | $PFD_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\lambda_D \times T_{\text{proof}}}{2}$ | $PFD$ = Probability of Failure on Demand, $\lambda_D$ = Dangerous failure rate ($\text{hr}^{-1}$), $T_{\text{proof}}$ = Proof test interval (hr) | IEC 61508, dimensionless |
| **Hazard Rate (Bathtub Model)** | $Z(t) = \alpha \beta t^{\beta-1}$ | Weibull distribution; $\alpha$ = scale, $\beta$ = shape parameter | $\text{fit}^{-1}$ |
| **Stop Time (Safety Distance)** | $S = (K \times T) + C$ | $K$ = approach speed (mm/s, typically 1600), $T$ = total stop time (s), $C$ = intrusion distance (mm) | mm (ISO 13855) |
| **Mean Time Between Failures** | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ = total failure rate | hours |
| **Safe Separation Distance (SSM)** | $S_{sp} = (v_{\text{robot}} \times T_{\text{reaction}}) + v_{\text{human}} \times T_{\text{reaction}} + d_{\text{protective}}$ | Variables defined in ISO/TS 15066 | mm |
| **Collaborative Power Limit** | $P_{\text{max}} = 80 \text{ W}$ (quasi-static contact) | ISO/TS 15066 biomechanical limit | Watts |
| **Maximum Quasi-Static Force** | $F_{\text{max}} = 140 \text{ N}$ (abdomen region) | ISO/TS 15066 limit | Newtons |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The risk equation $R = S \times P$ is the most frequently asked formula. Always remember to convert the categorical result into a **Risk Level Matrix** (Low / Medium / High / Very High) and propose a corresponding safeguard hierarchy.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the industry, identifying weak points is the foundation of **CE Marking** (Europe) and **ANSI/RIA R15.06** (USA) compliance. A robot integrator who skips the planning-phase risk assessment will face:
- Litigation risk when a worker is injured.
- Insurance claim rejection.
- Mandatory shutdown by factory inspectorate (e.g., Kerala Factories and Boilers).
- Loss of ISO 9001 / ISO 45001 certification.

In the **automotive sector**, weak-point analysis prevents line-side fatalities in robotic welding cells. In **medical robotics**, the same methodology prevents surgical instrument mishandling. The methodology is therefore **domain-agnostic** and **lifecycle-universal**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Quantitative Risk Scoring for a Robot Cell

> **Problem Statement (Sample KTU 14-Mark Sub-Part):**
> A 6-axis articulated robot is used for a palletizing task. The safety engineer assigns the following categorical scores:
> - Severity of injury (crushing) = 4
> - Probability of occurrence (without safeguard) = 4
> - Probability of occurrence (with light curtain) = 1
> - Probability of operator avoiding harm = 2
>
> Compute the **Initial Risk**, the **Residual Risk**, the **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)**, and conclude whether the safeguard is acceptable per ISO 12100 (Acceptable RRF $\geq$ 4).

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Identify the categorical variables.**

$$
S = 4 \quad ; \quad P_{\text{initial}} = 4 \quad ; \quad P_{\text{residual}} = 1 \quad ; \quad A = 2
$$

Here $A$ is the **Avoidance Factor** (probability that the operator can avoid harm, ranging 1–5).

**Step 2: Compute the Initial Risk Score.**

$$
R_{\text{initial}} = S \times P_{\text{initial}} \times A
$$

$$
R_{\text{initial}} = 4 \times 4 \times 2 = 32
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Residual Risk Score (after light curtain installation).**

$$
R_{\text{residual}} = S \times P_{\text{residual}} \times A
$$

$$
R_{\text{residual}} = 4 \times 1 \times 2 = 8
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Risk Reduction Factor (RRF).**

$$
RRF = \frac{R_{\text{initial}}}{R_{\text{residual}}} = \frac{32}{8} = 4
$$

**Step 5: Decision on Acceptability.**

$$
RRF_{\text{achieved}} = 4 \quad ; \quad RRF_{\text{required}} \geq 4
$$

$$
\therefore \text{Safeguard (light curtain) is marginally acceptable per ISO 12100.}
$$

**Step 6: Conclusion (Mandatory for 14-Mark Answers):**

Although the light curtain meets the minimum RRF, the engineer should consider **layered protection** (e.g., adding a safety mat in addition to the light curtain) to achieve a more robust RRF in the range of **6–10**, which is best-practice in high-payload robot cells.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters for KTU:** This single derivation tests **CO1, CO3** and Bloom's levels L3 (Apply) and L5 (Evaluate). Examiners frequently substitute the categorical numbers, so the methodology is what earns marks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Safe Separation Distance for a Collaborative Robot

> **Problem Statement:**
> A collaborative robot moves at a maximum speed of $v_{\text{robot}} = 250 \text{ mm/s}$. The safety-rated laser scanner has a reaction time of $T_{\text{reaction}} = 100 \text{ ms}$. The operator approaches at $v_{\text{human}} = 1600 \text{ mm/s}$. The robot's protective stop distance is $d_{\text{protective}} = 50 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the **minimum safe separation distance** $S_{sp}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Convert all times to SI units.**

$$
T_{\text{reaction}} = 100 \text{ ms} = 0.1 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the distance covered by the robot during the reaction time.**

$$
d_{\text{robot}} = v_{\text{robot}} \times T_{\text{reaction}}
$$

$$
d_{\text{robot}} = 250 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.1 \text{ s} = 25 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the distance covered by the operator during the reaction time.**

$$
d_{\text{human}} = v_{\text{human}} \times T_{\text{reaction}}
$$

$$
d_{\text{human}} = 1600 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.1 \text{ s} = 160 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the ISO/TS 15066 safe separation formula.**

$$
S_{sp} = d_{\text{robot}} + d_{\text{human}} + d_{\text{protective}}
$$

$$
S_{sp} = 25 \text{ mm} + 160 \text{ mm} + 50 \text{ mm} = 235 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5: Add a safety margin (engineering best practice).**

$$
S_{sp,\text{design}} = 1.25 \times S_{sp} = 1.25 \times 235 = 293.75 \text{ mm} \approx 300 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6: Conclusion.**

The robot cell's collaborative workspace boundary must be located at least **300 mm** away from any human-accessible zone, with the laser scanner set to trigger a protective stop at this distance.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Weak-Point Identification Table

> **How to use this matrix in the exam:** A 14-mark question may present a scenario (e.g., "A factory reports repeated near-misses in a robotic welding cell"). You must populate the table by identifying at least one weak point per phase, propose a safeguard, and justify the safeguard using the relevant ISO/ANSI standard.

| Lifecycle Phase | Typical Weak Point | Resulting Hazard | Recommended Safeguard | Governing Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Planning | No risk assessment performed | Unidentified hazards propagate to design | Conduct HAZOP / risk grid analysis | ISO 12100 |
| Planning | Robot reach envelope overlaps with walkway | Crushing injury to pedestrians | Re-layout cell, install perimeter fencing | ISO 13857 |
| Design | Single-channel E-stop | E-stop failure during emergency | Dual-channel safety E-stop with redundancy | ISO 13850 |
| Design | No light curtain at cell entry | Operator enters during robot motion | Install Type 4 safety light curtain | IEC 61496 |
| Design | No safe-speed limit in collaborative mode | High-speed collision injury | Implement Power and Force Limiting (PFL) | ISO/TS 15066 |
| Operation | Operator bypasses interlock | Robot operates with door open | Procedural enforcement + safety PLC lock | ANSI/RIA R15.06 |
| Operation | Maintenance during energised state | Stored energy release | Implement Lockout-Tagout (LOTO) | OSHA 1910.147 |
| Operation | No operator training | Workers enter danger zone unknowingly | Mandatory certified training program | ISO 10218-1 |

## 4.3 Robot Safety Function Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENSOR[Safety Sensors<br>Light Curtain, Mat, Scanner] --> PLC[Safety PLC<br>Dual-Channel Logic]
    ESTOP[Emergency Stops<br>Dual Channel] --> PLC
    PLC --> RELAY[Safety Relays / Contactors]
    RELAY --> MOTOR[Robot Drive Power Cut]
    PLC --> LOG[Event Logger<br>Fault Memory]

```

> This block diagram illustrates how weak points in *any single* sensor or control link can propagate to a complete safety failure. A dual-channel architecture is the KTU 2024-standard mitigation.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]
**Define weak points in the context of robot safety. List the three lifecycle phases in which they originate.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Weak points are defined as the inherent or introduced vulnerabilities in a robotic system that reduce its inherent safety margin and act as single points of failure in the accident causal chain. They originate in three lifecycle phases:
1. **Planning Phase** — conceptual and layout-level flaws (3 sub-types: risk assessment, layout, robot selection).
2. **Design Phase** — engineering-level flaws in mechanical, electrical, control, and software subsystems.
3. **Operation Phase** — behavioral, procedural, and maintenance-induced failures in the deployed cell.

*(Examiner allocates: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for naming phases + 1 mark for one example per phase.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2]
**State the international standard that governs collaborative robot safety, and write the two biomechanical limits it specifies.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The governing standard is **ISO/TS 15066:2016** — *Technical Specification for Collaborative Robots*. The two key biomechanical limits are:
- **Maximum Quasi-Static Contact Force** = **140 N** (for the abdomen region, the most restrictive).
- **Maximum Collaborative Power Limit** = **80 W** (for quasi-static contact scenarios).

*(Examiner allocates: 1 mark for standard name, 1 mark for force limit with value, 1 mark for power limit with value.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **hierarchy of controls** used to mitigate weak points in robotic systems. Discuss why PPE is positioned at the bottom of this hierarchy. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 6-axis robot is used in a CNC machine-tending cell. The following risk grid data is available: Severity = 3, Probability (initial) = 5, Avoidance = 3.
