> **Series:** Failure of **any one** subsystem breaks the chain → lower reliability. **Parallel:** All subsystems must fail → dramatically higher reliability, at the cost of weight, cost, and complexity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Classify robot failures based on **temporal onset**. Give one example for each class.

**Model Answer:**
Robot failures by temporal onset are classified into three categories:
1. **Sudden (Abrupt) Failures** — Occur without warning, e.g., servo motor winding burnout, circuit short-circuit.
2. **Gradual (Degradation) Failures** — Progressive deterioration with detectable precursors, e.g., gear-tooth wear, insulation aging, encoder disc contamination buildup.
3. **Intermittent Failures** — Sporadic, often environment-dependent, e.g., loose connector, EMI-induced sensor false triggers.

**(3 marks — 1 mark for classification, 2 marks for examples)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Differentiate between **MTBF** and **MTTF**. When is each used?

**Model Answer:**
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)** is used for **non-repairable** components (e.g., bearings, fuses). It represents the expected time until first failure.
$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt
$$
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** is used for **repairable** systems (e.g., a complete robot, controller). It represents the average time interval between successive failures, including repair time.
$$
\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}
$$

**(3 marks — clear distinction with formulas and use cases)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **omit formulas** when computing reliability — always state the formula before substituting.
> - Confusing **series vs. parallel** architectures loses 3–4 marks instantly.
> - For FMEA-style questions, you **must list severity, occurrence, detectability scores** explicitly — vague answers get zero.
> - Drawing **unlabeled diagrams** wastes effort; **label all blocks** for partial credit.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** Classify robot failures by **subsystem of origin**. For each subsystem, list **two specific failure modes** and their **corrective measures**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot manipulator has three critical sensors in series: $R_1 = 0.98$, $R_2 = 0.97$, $R_3 = 0.99$ over a 500-hour mission. **(i)** Compute the system reliability. **(ii)** If a redundant backup is added to the lowest-reliability sensor (in parallel), recompute the system reliability. **(iii)** Comment on the improvement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Classification by subsystem:

1. **Mechanical Failures** (e.g., gear wear → scheduled lubrication, predictive maintenance via vibration analysis; bearing failure → acoustic emission monitoring, replacement cycles).
2. **Actuator Failures** (e.g., servo stall → current/torque limiters, thermal sensors; hydraulic leak → leak detection sensors, scheduled seal replacement).
3. **Sensor Failures** (e.g., encoder loss → redundant encoders, shielded cables; vision occlusion → auto-cleaning nozzles, AI anomaly detection).
4. **Controller/Electronic Failures** (e.g., PLC I/O failure → surge protectors, opto-isolation; microcontroller crash → watchdog timers, ECC memory).
5. **Software Failures** (e.g., real-time overrun → RTOS with hard deadlines, rate-monotonic scheduling; deadlock → resource ordering algorithms).
6. **Power/Communication/Environmental Failures** — covered above in summary table.

**(7 marks — 1 mark per subsystem with 0.5 for failure mode + 0.5 for corrective measure)**

**(b)(i) — Series reliability:**

$$
R_s(500) = 0.98 \times 0.97 \times 0.99
$$

Step-by-step:
$$
\begin{aligned}
0.98 \times 0.97 &= 0.9506 \\
0.9506 \times 0.99 &= 0.941094
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_s(500\,\text{h}) \approx 0.9411 \ \text{or}\ 94.11\%}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(b)(ii) — Add redundant sensor 2 (lowest reliability, $R_2 = 0.97$):**

For two parallel sensors each with $R = 0.97$:

$$
R_{2,\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - 0.97)(1 - 0.97) = 1 - (0.03)^2 = 1 - 0.0009 = 0.9991
$$

New series reliability:

$$
R_{\text{new}} = 0.98 \times 0.9991 \times 0.99
$$

Step-by-step:
$$
\begin{aligned}
0.98 \times 0.9991 &= 0.979118 \\
0.979118 \times 0.99 &= 0.969327
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{\text{new}}(500\,\text{h}) \approx 0.9693 \ \text{or}\ 96.93\%}
$$

**(3 marks)**

**(b)(iii) — Comment:**

Adding a single redundant sensor to the weakest link improved system reliability from **94.11% → 96.93%**, a gain of **~2.82 percentage points**. This demonstrates the **disproportionate impact of redundancy on the least-reliable component** in a series chain — a key principle in robot safety engineering. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** methodology. Compute the **RPN** for a robot's pneumatic pressure-drop failure given: Severity $S = 9$, Occurrence $O = 5$, Detectability $D = 6$. Suggest **three corrective measures** to reduce this RPN. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **availability** and compute it for a robot arm with MTBF = 800 hours and MTTR = 6 hours. If two such arms operate in **hot-standby parallel** (each independently repairable, same reliability), derive the new system availability formula and calculate it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) FMEA Methodology:**

FMEA is a **systematic, bottom-up** reliability analysis technique used during the design and operational phases. For each component:
1. Identify all possible **failure modes**.
2. Assess **effects** on subsystem and system.
3. Score **Severity (S, 1–10)**, **Occurrence (O, 1–10)**, **Detectability (D, 1–10)**.
4. Compute **RPN = S × O × D**.
5. Prioritize corrective actions for high-RPN items.

**RPN Calculation:**

$$
\text{RPN} = 9 \times 5 \times 6 = 270
$$

Since RPN > 200, this is a **high-priority item** requiring immediate corrective action.

**(4 marks — 2 for methodology, 2 for RPN)**

**Three Corrective Measures** (to reduce RPN by lowering O or D):
1. Install **redundant pressure sensors** with auto-alarm → reduces Occurrence (catches failure early) and Detectability.
2. Use **dual compressor** with auto-switchover → reduces Occurrence of pressure drop.
3. Add **scheduled predictive maintenance** via pressure-trend monitoring → reduces Detectability score.

**(3 marks — 1 per measure)**

**(b) Availability Definition:**

Availability $A$ is the **fraction of time** a system is in an operational state over the long run.

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

**Single arm:**

$$
A_1 = \dfrac{800}{800 + 6} = \dfrac{800}{806} \approx 0.9926
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Hot-standby parallel system:**

In hot-standby, both arms operate; the system fails only if **both** fail. Since each arm is independently repairable, the system availability is:

$$
A_{\text{system}} = 1 - (1 - A_1)^2
$$

This is the **parallel availability formula for independently repairable units**.

**Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{system}} &= 1 - (1 - 0.9926)^2 \\
                  &= 1 - (0.0074)^2 \\
                  &= 1 - 0.00005476 \\
                  &= 0.99994524
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{\text{system}} \approx 0.99995 \ \text{or}\ 99.995\%}
$$

**(3 marks for formula, 2 marks for calculation)**

**Comment:** The dual-arm hot-standby configuration boosts availability from **99.26% → 99.995%**, a **75× reduction in downtime fraction** — a critical design choice for high-value, safety-critical robotic workcells. **(Included in marks)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE:**

- ✅ Robot failures are classified by **subsystem, severity, temporal onset, recoverability, and dependency**.
- ✅ The **5 main subsystems** are: Mechanical, Actuator, Sensor, Controller, Software (+ Power, Communication, Environmental).
- ✅ **Series system reliability** = product of individual reliabilities → $R_s = \prod R_i$.
- ✅ **Parallel (redundant) system reliability** = $1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$.
- ✅ **Exponential reliability model**: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$; **MTBF** = $1/\lambda$.
- ✅ **Availability**: $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$.
- ✅ **MTTF** is for non-repairable; **MTBF** is for repairable.
- ✅ **FMEA** uses RPN = $S \times O \times D$; RPN > 100 demands action; > 200 = critical.
- ✅ **Redundancy** should be applied to the **weakest (lowest-reliability) link** in a series chain for maximum impact.
- ✅ Common corrective measures: **predictive maintenance, sensor fusion, watchdog timers, RTOS, EMI shielding, IP-rated enclosures, FMEA-driven design, dual compressor/UPS, safety light curtains for cobots**.
- ✅ Always **state the formula → substitute → compute step-by-step** in KTU numericals.
- ✅ Always **label diagrams** in KTU answers to gain partial credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Effectiveness Dictating Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT831 - SAFETY AND RELIABILITY IN ROBOTIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Robot Effectiveness Dictating Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robot Effectiveness** is formally defined as the qualitative and quantitative measure of a robotic system's ability to accomplish its designated task in a real-world operating environment, while maintaining the required safety thresholds, reliability metrics, and operational consistency. In the KTU 2024 OBE framework, robot effectiveness is evaluated as a **multi-attribute composite index** that integrates kinematic performance, dynamic performance, dependability, and safety compliance.

The **Robot Effectiveness Dictating Factors (REDFs)** are the set of performance-defining, design-level, and operational parameters that directly govern whether a robot will successfully execute its intended mission over its operational lifecycle. These factors are typically partitioned into the following KTU-aligned taxonomies:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Taxonomy of Robot Effectiveness Factors:**
> 1. **Geometric / Kinematic Factors** — Accuracy, Repeatability, Resolution, Workspace, Degrees of Freedom (DoF)
> 2. **Dynamic Factors** — Speed, Acceleration, Cycle Time, Payload Capacity
> 3. **Dependability / Reliability Factors** — MTBF, MTTR, Availability, Failure Rate (λ)
> 4. **Environmental & Safety Factors** — IP rating, Operating temperature, Safety Integrity Level (SIL), Fault tolerance
> 5. **Control & Sensing Factors** — Controller bandwidth, Sensor resolution, Feedback latency

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note (Module 2 Context):**
> Within the *Safety and Reliability* scope of Module 2, the "effectiveness" of a robot is **not just about doing the task** — it is about doing the task *safely, repeatably, and predictably* across the system's lifetime. The 2024 scheme expects students to clearly distinguish between *performance* (can it do it?) and *effectiveness* (can it do it well, safely, and reliably?).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you hire a **chess player** to play on your behalf. Several factors decide whether this player is "effective":

| Chess Player Analogy | Robotic Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Wins most of the time (correct moves) | **Accuracy** |
| Plays the *same opening* every single time | **Repeatability** |
| Notices tiny differences on the board | **Resolution** |
| Reaches the table in 2 minutes | **Speed / Cycle Time** |
| Can play for hours without tiring | **Reliability (MTBF)** |
| Recovers quickly from a blunder | **MTTR / Maintainability** |
| Plays on any board size | **Workspace / Reach** |
| Doesn't push pieces off the table | **Safety / Fault Tolerance** |

Just as a chess player who is fast *but* makes random errors is **not effective**, a robot that is precise *but* breaks down weekly is **not truly effective** for industrial deployment.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exam:** A robot's effectiveness = *f*(Accuracy, Repeatability, Speed, Reliability, Safety). Always remember the **"ARSS"** mnemonic — **A**ccuracy, **R**epeatability, **S**peed, **S**afety.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used by KTU Examiners

The following **standardized industrial metrics** (per ISO 9283 and ISO 13849) are highlighted in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

- **AP (Accuracy of Position)** — measured in **mm**
- **RP (Repeatability of Position)** — measured in **mm**
- **ST (Static Compliance)** — measured in **mm/N**
- **MTBF** — measured in **hours**
- **MTTR** — measured in **hours**
- **Availability (A)** — dimensionless, expressed as a percentage
- **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** — levels 1 to 4
- **PFH (Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour)** — dimensionless

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Structured Breakdown of Each Effectiveness Factor

### 2.1.1 ACCURACY (Geometric Factor)

**Definition:** The ability of a robot to move its end-effector to a *commanded* target position in space, expressed as the deviation between the *desired position* and the *achieved position*.

**Key Engineering Insight:** Accuracy is a **systematic error** metric — it measures the *bias* between what you ask for and what you get. A highly accurate robot has a *small systematic deviation*.

**Why it matters in safety-critical robotics:**
- In surgical robots, an inaccuracy of even **0.5 mm** can cause nerve damage.
- In collaborative robots (cobots), accuracy determines the **safety zone** dimensioning.
- In CNC-loading robots, accuracy affects **end-product quality** and scrap rates.

### 2.1.2 REPEATABILITY (Geometric Factor)

**Definition:** The ability of a robot to return to the *same* target position over *multiple cycles* under *identical* commanded conditions. Measured statistically.

**Key Engineering Insight:** Repeatability is a **random error / precision** metric — it measures the *spread* (variance) of the achieved positions around a mean. A highly repeatable robot has *tight clustering* of its actual end positions.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Accuracy vs. Repeatability" Question — Always Asked in KTU:**
> Accuracy and repeatability are **independent**. A robot can be highly repeatable (always lands at the *same* wrong spot) but inaccurate. Conversely, a robot can be accurate on average but have poor repeatability (scattered around the target). An **effective robot must be BOTH accurate AND repeatable**.

### 2.1.3 RESOLUTION (Geometric Factor)

**Definition:** The *smallest incremental movement* that the robot's control system can command and the mechanical system can execute. Determined by the encoder resolution, controller bit-depth, and backlash.

**Physical unit:** **mm** (or micrometers for high-precision systems).

**Practical implication:** A resolution finer than the application's tolerance is necessary but **not sufficient** — accuracy and repeatability must also be finer.

### 2.1.4 WORKSPACE / WORKING VOLUME (Geometric Factor)

**Definition:** The total 3D region of space that the robot's end-effector can physically reach. Bounded by the robot's reach, joint limits, and singular configurations.

**Engineering utility:** Determines whether the robot can physically access all required task points. For a 6-DoF articulated robot, the workspace is approximately a **partial sphere** minus joint-limit shadow regions.

### 2.1.5 DEGREES OF FREEDOM (Kinematic Factor)

**Definition:** The number of *independent* parameters that define the configuration of the robot's end-effector in 3D space. For full 6-DoF: 3 for position (x, y, z) + 3 for orientation (roll, pitch, yaw).

**Engineering rule of thumb:** The number of DoF should be **equal to** the task's required positioning complexity. Under-actuated robots (fewer DoF) are cheaper but less versatile.

### 2.1.6 SPEED AND CYCLE TIME (Dynamic Factor)

**Definition:** Speed is the maximum linear or angular velocity of the end-effector or joints. Cycle time is the total time to complete one full pick-and-place or manufacturing cycle.

**Engineering utility:** Directly impacts **throughput** and **economic ROI**. However, excessive speed compromises safety and accuracy (more vibration, more overshoot).

### 2.1.7 PAYLOAD CAPACITY (Dynamic Factor)

**Definition:** The maximum mass the robot can manipulate while maintaining its rated accuracy, repeatability, and acceleration, throughout its entire workspace.

**Critical insight:** Payload rating is **position-dependent** — a robot rated for 10 kg at full extension may only handle 5 kg at full reach due to moment-arm torque limits on the wrist joints.

### 2.1.8 RELIABILITY — MTBF, FAILURE RATE (Dependability Factor)

**Definition:** Reliability R(t) is the probability that the robot performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified time interval [0, t].

**Failure rate (λ)** is the rate at which failures occur, typically modeled as constant for the useful life (exponential distribution).

### 2.1.9 MAINTAINABILITY — MTTR (Dependability Factor)

**Definition:** Mean Time To Repair — the average time required to restore a failed robot to operational status, including diagnosis, repair, and re-calibration.

### 2.1.10 AVAILABILITY (Composite Dependability Factor)

**Definition:** The fraction of time the robot is operationally ready to perform its task.

### 2.1.11 SAFETY FACTORS (Safety & Compliance Factor)

**Definition:** The integration of safety-rated hardware, software, and control systems to mitigate hazards. Includes emergency stop response time, collision detection sensitivity, force-limiting thresholds, and safety-rated monitored stop (SRMS) functions per ISO 10218 and ISO/TS 15066.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Emphasis:**
> For safety-critical applications, the KTU 2024 scheme explicitly couples the safety factors with reliability factors via the **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** concept. A robot's *effectiveness* is meaningless if it is *unsafe*. Always state this in the introduction of any answer involving safety and reliability.

## 2.2 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute value notations use `\vert` to maintain markdown table integrity.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Accuracy of Position (AP)** | $$ AP = \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_{cmd})^2 + (\bar{y} - y_{cmd})^2 + (\bar{z} - z_{cmd})^2} $$ | mm | Mean deviation from commanded point |
| 2 | **Repeatability (RP) — ISO 9283** | $$ RP = \bar{l} + 3\sigma_l $$ | mm | Mean of 30 cycle distances + 3 standard deviations |
| 3 | **Single-axis Repeatability** | $$ RP_i = \pm 3\sigma_i \quad i \in \{x, y, z\} $$ | mm | Three-sigma spread on one axis |
| 4 | **Resolution (r)** | $$ r = \frac{\text{Full Travel}}{\text{Number of Increments}} $$ | mm | Smallest commanded motion |
| 5 | **Cycle Time (T_c)** | $$ T_c = T_{move} + T_{process} + T_{return} $$ | seconds | Total per-cycle time |
| 6 | **Reliability (Exponential)** | $$ R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} $$ | dimensionless | Probability of no failure by time t |
| 7 | **Failure Rate (λ)** | $$ \lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF} $$ | failures/hour | Constant hazard assumption |
| 8 | **Unreliability F(t)** | $$ F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} $$ | dimensionless | Probability of at least one failure by time t |
| 9 | **Mean Time To Repair** | $$ MTTR = \frac{1}{\mu} $$ | hours | Average repair duration |
| 10 | **Availability (Steady-State)** | $$ A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} $$ | ratio (0 to 1) | Fraction of time operational |
| 11 | **Downtime Fraction** | $$ \text{Downtime} = 1 - A = \frac{MTTR}{MTBF + MTTR} $$ | ratio | Fraction of time NOT operational |
| 12 | **Critical Payload Torque** | $$ \tau_{wrist} = m \cdot g \cdot L_{arm} $$ | N·m | Moment at wrist joint = mass × gravity × reach |
| 13 | **Workspace Volume (Spherical)** | $$ V_{ws} = \frac{4}{3}\pi (R_{max}^3 - R_{min}^3) $$ | m³ | Approx. workspace volume |
| 14 | **Required DoF** | $$ DoF_{req} = DoF_{pos} + DoF_{ori} $$ | count | 3 position + up to 3 orientation |
| 15 | **PFH to SIL Mapping** | $$ PFH = \sum \lambda_{dangerous} $$ | 1/hour | Probability of dangerous failure per hour |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In Part B questions worth 14 marks, you will likely be asked to **derive** the Availability formula from first principles. The derivation is in Section 3 below. Memorizing the formula alone is **insufficient** — you must show the derivation logic.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive welding cells:** Accuracy ≤ 0.1 mm, Repeatability ≤ 0.05 mm, Payload 50–200 kg, MTBF > 50,000 hours.
- **Surgical robots (Da Vinci, etc.):** Resolution < 10 μm, Repeatability < 50 μm, SIL-3 safety compliance.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots):** Localization accuracy ±10 cm, MTBF > 20,000 hours, Availability > 99.5%.
- **Collaborative robots (cobots):** Force-limited to ≤ 150 N (per ISO/TS 15066), speed ≤ 250 mm/s in collaborative mode, redundant encoders for SIL-3.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Repeatability From Statistical Principles (ISO 9283 Method)

**Problem Context:** KTU frequently asks: *A robot is commanded to return to the same point 30 times. The measured distances from the centroid to each point yield a mean of 0.04 mm and a standard deviation of 0.012 mm. Calculate the repeatability RP.*

**Step 1 — Define the measured variable:**

Let $d_i$ be the radial distance from the mean achieved position (centroid) to the $i$-th measured position in a single trial direction. For $n = 30$ cycles:

$$ d_i = \sqrt{(x_i - \bar{x})^2 + (y_i - \bar{y})^2 + (z_i - \bar{z})^2} $$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean radial distance $\bar{l}$:**

$$ \bar{l} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i $$

For the given problem:

$$ \bar{l} = 0.04 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the standard deviation $\sigma_l$ of the radial distances:**

$$ \sigma_l = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (d_i - \bar{l})^2} $$

For the given problem:

$$ \sigma_l = 0.012 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 4 — Apply the ISO 9283 Repeatability Definition:**

The ISO 9283 standard defines repeatability as the **mean radial distance plus three standard deviations** (covering ~99.73% of a normal distribution):

$$ RP = \bar{l} + 3\sigma_l $$

**Step 5 — Numerical substitution:**

$$ RP = 0.04 + 3 \times 0.012 $$

$$ RP = 0.04 + 0.036 $$

$$ \boxed{RP = 0.076 \text{ mm}} $$

**Engineering interpretation:** The robot will return to within ±0.076 mm of its mean position in approximately 99.73% of cycles. This is **sub-100-micron repeatability**, suitable for **precision assembly** tasks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Availability From Steady-State Renewal Theory

**Problem Context:** KTU frequently asks: *A robot has MTBF = 4000 hours and MTTR = 8 hours. Calculate steady-state availability, downtime per year, and the probability of failure-free operation over a 100-hour mission.*

**Step 1 — Define the operational state cycle:**

In steady state, the robot alternates between two states:
- **UP state** (operational) — average duration = MTBF
- **DOWN state** (failed, under repair) — average duration = MTTR

**Step 2 — Define steady-state availability A as the long-run fraction of time in the UP state:**

$$ A = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{\text{Total UP time in } [0, T]}{T} $$

For a renewal process with cycle (MTBF + MTTR):

$$ A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} $$

**Step 3 — Numerical substitution:**

$$ A = \frac{4000}{4000 + 8} = \frac{4000}{4008} $$

$$ A = 0.998004 \approx 99.80\% $$

**Step 4 — Compute downtime fraction per year:**

Total hours in a year = 8760 hours.

$$ \text{Downtime} = (1 - A) \times 8760 $$

$$ \text{Downtime} = (1 - 0.998004) \times 8760 $$

$$ \text{Downtime} = 0.001996 \times 8760 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{Downtime} \approx 17.48 \text{ hours/year}} $$

**Step 5 — Compute failure rate $\lambda$:**

$$ \lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF} = \frac{1}{4000} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour} $$

**Step 6 — Compute Reliability R(t) over a 100-hour mission:**

$$ R(100) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 100} $$

$$ R(100) = e^{-0.025} $$

Using the Taylor series approximation $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x + \frac{x^2}{2}$ for small $x$:

$$ R(100) \approx 1 - 0.025 + \frac{0.000625}{2} $$

$$ R(100) \approx 0.97531 + 0.000313 $$

$$ \boxed{R(100) \approx 0.9756 \text{ (or 97.56\%)}} $$

**Engineering interpretation:** There is approximately a **2.44% probability of at least one failure** during any 100-hour continuous mission. For a 24/7 production line, this translates to roughly **2.13 expected failures per year** (assuming 8760 hours/year).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Workspace Volume Bounded by Joint Limits

**Problem Context:** *A 3-DoF Cartesian robot has X-axis travel of 2 m, Y-axis travel of 1.5 m, and Z-axis travel of 1 m. Calculate the rectangular workspace volume.*

**Step 1 — Define rectangular workspace volume for Cartesian robot:**

A Cartesian robot's workspace is a **rectangular parallelepiped** since each prismatic joint operates independently.

$$ V_{ws} = L_X \times L_Y \times L_Z $$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$ V_{ws} = 2 \times 1.5 \times 1 $$

$$ \boxed{V_{ws} = 3.0 \text{ m}^3} $$

**Contrast with spherical workspace (articulated robot):** A 6-DoF articulated robot with maximum reach $R_{max}$ and minimum reach $R_{min}$ (limited by forearm-to-upper-arm clearance) has approximate spherical workspace:

$$ V_{ws} = \frac{4}{3}\pi (R_{max}^3 - R_{min}^3) $$

For a robot with $R_{max} = 1.5$ m and $R_{min} = 0.3$ m:

$$ V_{ws} = \frac{4}{3}\pi (1.5^3 - 0.3^3) = \frac{4}{3}\pi (3.375 - 0.027) $$

$$ V_{ws} = \frac{4}{3}\pi (3.348) = 4.464\pi $$

$$ \boxed{V_{ws} \approx 14.03 \text{ m}^3} $$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Effectiveness Factors

| Factor | Type of Error Captured | Affected by Wear? | KTU Typical Question Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | Systematic (Bias) | Yes (calibration drift) | Calculation + scenario-based |
| Repeatability | Random (Variance) | Yes (bearing degradation) | Statistical definition |
| Resolution | Discrete Step Size | No (hardware-fixed) | Numerical calculation |
| Workspace | Geometric | No (mechanical design) | Volume / reach problems |
| MTBF | Temporal Reliability | Yes (aging) | Reliability block diagram |
| Availability | Uptime fraction | Yes | MTBF/MTTR trade-off |
| Safety (SIL) | Risk Severity | Yes (proof-test interval) | Safety case analysis |
| Payload | Mechanical Load | Yes (joint fatigue) | Moment-arm problems |
| Speed | Dynamic | Yes (motor wear) | Cycle time optimization |
| DoF | Kinematic | No (design) | Task-DoF matching |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern (Module 2):** Expect a **14-mark question** asking you to:
> (a) Explain **5** major factors with diagrams / formulas, AND
> (b) Solve a **numerical** on either repeatability statistics, availability, or workspace volume.
> Use the derivations above as templates.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Robot Effectiveness Dictating Factors Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ROBOT EFFECTIVENESS]:::root --> B[GEOMETRIC / KINEMATIC FACTORS]:::branch
    A --> C[DYNAMIC FACTORS]:::branch
    A --> D[DEPENDABILITY FACTORS]:::branch
    A --> E[SAFETY FACTORS]:::branch
    A --> F[CONTROL AND SENSING FACTORS]:::branch

    B --> B1[Accuracy]
    B --> B2[Repeatability]
    B --> B3[Resolution]
    B --> B4[Workspace Volume]
    B --> B5[Degrees of Freedom]

    C --> C1[Speed]
    C --> C2[Acceleration]
    C --> C3[Cycle Time]
    C --> C4[Payload Capacity]

    D --> D1[MTBF]
    D --> D2[MTTR]
    D --> D3[Availability]
    D --> D4[Failure Rate lambda]

    E --> E1[Safety Integrity Level]
    E --> E2[Emergency Stop Response]
    E --> E3[Force Limiting]
    E --> E4[IP Rating / Environmental]

    F --> F1[Controller Bandwidth]
    F --> F2[Sensor Resolution]
    F --> F3[Feedback Latency]
    F --> F4[Fault Detection Coverage]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef branch fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Reliability → Safety Coupling Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Robot Task Assigned]:::start --> B{Has High Reliability?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Low Failure Rate]
    B -- No --> D[High Failure Rate]
    C --> E{Has Safety Mechanisms?}
    D --> E
    E -- Yes --> F[Effective AND Safe]
    E -- No --> G[Effective but UNSAFE]
    D --> H{Frequent Failures?}
    H -- Yes --> I[INEFFECTIVE]
    H -- No --> J[May be Effective]
    F --> K[OPTIMAL DEPLOYMENT]:::goal
    I --> L[NEEDS REDESIGN]:::warn
    G --> M[NEEDS SAFETY RETROFIT]:::warn

    classDef start fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef goal fill:#15803d,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef warn fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Accuracy vs Repeatability Visual

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T1[Scenario 1: Accurate AND Repeatable]
    A1[Commanded Point]:::cmd --> A2[Tight cluster around target]:::good
    end
    subgraph T2[Scenario 2: Repeatable but NOT Accurate]
    B1[Commanded Point]:::cmd --> B2[Tight cluster but offset from target]:::warn
    end
    subgraph T3[Scenario 3: Accurate on average but NOT Repeatable]
    C1[Commanded Point]:::cmd --> C2[Scattered cluster centered on target]:::warn
    end
    subgraph T4[Scenario 4: NEITHER]
    D1[Commanded Point]:::cmd --> D2[Scattered cluster offset from target]:::bad
    end

    classDef cmd fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef good fill:#86efac,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef warn fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Architecture — Reliability Availability Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Collect MTBF Data]:::input --> B[Collect MTTR Data]:::input
    B --> C[Compute Failure Rate lambda = 1/MTBF]:::calc
    A --> D[Compute R(t) = exp(-lambda times t)]:::calc
    C --> E[Compute Availability A = MTBF / MTBF + MTTR]:::calc
    D --> F[Compute MTTF for non-repairable]:::calc
    E --> G[Compute Downtime per Year]:::calc
    F --> H[Generate Reliability vs Time Curve]:::output
    G --> H

    classDef input fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Diagram Tips:**
> - Use **simple, labeled block diagrams** like the ones above.
> - Do **not** spend time drawing physical robot schematics in the exam — use Mermaid-style hierarchical or block diagrams.
> - Always include **units** on the arrows (e.g., mm, hours, failures/hour).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**Differentiate between Accuracy and Repeatability in robotic systems. Why are both essential for an effective robot?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Accuracy | Repeatability |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Deviation of *mean achieved position* from *commanded position* | Ability to return to the *same position* over multiple cycles |
| Nature of error | Systematic (Bias) | Random (Spread) |
| Formula | $AP = \sqrt{(\bar{x}-x_{cmd})^2+(\bar{y}-y_{cmd})^2+(\bar{z}-z_{cmd})^2}$ | $RP = \bar{l} + 3\sigma_l$ |
| Unit | mm | mm |

Both are essential because **accuracy ensures the robot hits the right target** (correctness), while **repeatability ensures the robot consistently hits the same point** (consistency). A robot that is repeatable but not accurate can be **recalibrated**; a robot that is accurate on average but not repeatable is **unusable for precision work** because the scatter defeats the mean.

*(Examiner allocates: 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for the "why both are essential" reasoning.)*

---

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`

**Define MTBF and MTTR. How is the steady-state Availability of a robot computed from these metrics?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** The average time interval between two consecutive failures of a repairable robot, measured in hours. (1 mark)
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** The average time required to restore a failed robot to operational status, including detection, diagnosis, repair, and verification, measured in hours. (1 mark)
- **Availability Formula:**

$$ A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} $$

A high MTBF and a low MTTR give high availability. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, With Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A.1** `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model Paper Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the major factors that dictate the effectiveness of an industrial robot. Discuss **any five** factors in detail with relevant formulas and engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-DoF articulated robot is commanded to return to a target point 30 times. The radial distance measurements yield a mean of 0.05 mm and a standard deviation of 0.015 mm. Calculate the **Repeatability (RP)** per ISO 9283. If the MTBF of the robot is 5000 hours and the MTTR is 10 hours, calculate the **steady-state Availability** and the **probability of failure-free operation over a 200-hour mission**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

The effectiveness of an industrial robot is dictated by the following factors. The five most critical are explained:

**1. Accuracy (1.5 marks)**
Accuracy refers to the ability of the robot to position its end-effector at the commanded target location. Per ISO 9283, it is measured as the deviation between the *commanded position* and the *mean of achieved positions* over multiple cycles. It is a **systematic error metric**. High accuracy is essential in tasks like circuit-board assembly, where the position tolerance is in the order of **25–50 μm**.

$$ AP = \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_{cmd})^2 + (\bar{y} - y_{cmd})^2 + (\bar{z} - z_{cmd})^2} $$

**2. Repeatability (1.5 marks)**
Repeatability is the ability to return to the *same* position consistently. It is a **random error metric** quantified via the standard deviation of achieved positions. Per ISO 9283:

$$ RP = \bar{l} + 3\sigma_l $$

A high repeatability (low RP value) is mandatory for **high-throughput pick-and-place** operations.

**3. Payload Capacity (1 mark)**
The maximum load (including end-effector mass) that the robot can manipulate at rated accuracy throughout its workspace. Important because it determines whether the robot is suitable for **heavy-payload** applications (e.g., palletizing 200 kg boxes) or **light-payload** tasks (e.g., 1 kg component placement).

**4. Workspace / Reach (1 mark)**
The 3D volume reachable by the end-effector. For a Cartesian robot it is a rectangular parallelepiped; for an articulated robot, approximately spherical:

$$ V_{ws} = \frac{4}{3}\pi(R_{max}^3 - R_{min}^3) $$

A larger workspace improves flexibility but reduces stiffness and accuracy.

**5. Reliability — MTBF and Availability (2 marks)**
Reliability is the probability of failure-free operation over a specified duration. For the constant failure rate model:

$$ R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \quad \lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF} $$

Steady-state availability:

$$ A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} $$

A high availability (typically > 99% for industrial robots) is essential for **continuous production lines**. Reduced availability directly translates to lost revenue.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Repeatability Calculation (3 marks):**

Using the ISO 9283 definition:

$$ RP = \bar{l} + 3\sigma_l $$

Given: $\bar{l} = 0.05$ mm, $\sigma_l = 0.015$ mm.

$$ RP = 0.05 + 3 \times 0.015 = 0.05 + 0.045 $$

$$ \boxed{RP = 0.095 \text{ mm}} $$

**Step 2 — Steady-State Availability (2 marks):**

Given: MTBF = 5000 h, MTTR = 10 h.

$$ A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{5000}{5000 + 10} = \frac{5000}{5010} $$

$$ \boxed{A = 0.9980 \approx 99.80\%} $$

**Step 3 — Failure Rate (1 mark):**

$$ \lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF} = \frac{1}{5000} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour} $$

**Step 4 — Reliability over 200-hour mission (1 mark):**

$$ R(200) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(2 \times 10^{-4}) \times 200} = e^{-0.04} $$

$$ R(200) \approx 0.9608 \approx 96.08\% $$

The probability of failure-free operation over 200 hours is approximately **96.08%**.

---

### **Question B-A.2 (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Discuss the role of **Degrees of Freedom (DoF)**, **Resolution**, **Workspace**, and **Speed/Cycle Time** in determining robot effectiveness. Illustrate with a comparative table and provide at least one formula per factor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Cartesian robot has X-travel = 3 m, Y-travel = 2 m, Z-travel = 1.5 m. An articulated robot has $R_{max} = 2$ m and $R_{min} = 0.5$ m. Compare their **workspace volumes**. If the Cartesian robot has MTBF = 3000 h and MTTR = 5 h, compute its availability and annual downtime in hours. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

| Factor | Definition | Formula / Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DoF** | Independent positioning parameters | $DoF_{req} = DoF_{pos} + DoF_{ori}$ (max 6) | Task-matching — 6-DoF for arbitrary 3D orientation |
| **Resolution** | Smallest commanded increment | $r = \text{Full Travel} / \text{Increments}$ | Must be finer than application tolerance |
| **Workspace** | Reachable 3D volume | $V_{ws} = L_X \times L_Y \times L_Z$ (Cartesian) | Defines the robot's physical applicability |
| **Speed / Cycle Time** | Linear/angular velocity limits | $T_c = T_{move} + T_{process} + T_{return}$ | Determines throughput |

*(Allocate ~1.5 marks per factor with a brief explanation of each.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Cartesian Workspace Volume (2 marks):**

$$ V_{cart} = L_X \times L_Y \times L_Z = 3 \times 2 \times 1.5 = 9.0 \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 2 — Articulated Workspace Volume (2 marks):**

$$ V_{art} = \frac{4}{3}\pi (R_{max}^3 - R_{min}^3) = \frac{4}{3}\pi (2^3 - 0.5^3) $$

$$ V_{art} = \frac{4}{3}\pi (8 - 0.125) = \frac{4}{3}\pi (7.875) $$

$$ V_{art} = 10.5\pi \approx 32.99 \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 3 — Comparison (1 mark):**

The articulated robot has approximately **3.67 times** the workspace volume of the Cartesian robot.

**Step 4 — Availability (1 mark):**

$$ A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{3000}{3000 + 5} = \frac{3000}{3005} $$

$$ A \approx 0.9983 \approx 99.83\% $$

**Step 5 — Annual Downtime (1 mark):**

$$ \text{Downtime/year} = (1 - A) \times 8760 = (1 - 0.9983) \times 8760 $$

$$ = 0.001664 \times 8760 \approx 14.58 \text{ hours/year} $$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing accuracy and repeatability** in calculations — a single mark deduction per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting the units** (mm vs. cm) in numerical answers — 0.5 mark deduction.
> 3. **Using RP = σ alone** instead of the ISO 9283 definition $RP = \bar{l} + 3\sigma_l$ — full mark loss for that sub-question.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert time units** (e.g., using minutes for MTBF/MTTR) — 1 mark deduction.
> 5. **Not distinguishing between *performance* and *effectiveness*** — KTU 2024 expects you to mention *safety* and *reliability* dimensions in any "effectiveness" answer.
> 6. **Skipping the "engineering interpretation"** in numerical problems — board examiners now require 1–2 lines of practical implication, not just a number.
> 7. **Using |x| in markdown tables** — this breaks the table. Use `\vert x \vert` instead (this is why Section 2 above uses `\vert`).

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read This Section Twice Before the Exam**

### Key Definitions (Must Memorize)
- **Accuracy:** Deviation between *commanded* and *mean achieved* position. **Systematic error.**
- **Repeatability:** Ability to return to the *same* position over multiple cycles. **Random error.**
- **Resolution:** Smallest commanded increment.
- **Workspace:** Reachable 3D volume by the end-effector.
- **DoF:** Independent parameters defining the end-effector pose.
- **MTBF:** Mean Time Between Failures (repairable system).
- **MTTR:** Mean Time To Repair.
- **Availability:** Fraction of time the robot is operationally ready.
- **SIL:** Safety Integrity Level (1 to 4).
- **PFH:** Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour.

### Critical Formulas (5 Must-Know)
1. $AP = \sqrt{(\bar{x}-x_{cmd})^2 + (\bar{y}-y_{cmd})^2 + (\bar{z}-z_{cmd})^2}$
2. $RP = \bar{l} + 3\sigma_l$
3. $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$
4. $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, with $\lambda = 1/MTBF$
5. $V_{ws} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi(R_{max}^3 - R_{min}^3)$ (articulated) or $L_X \cdot L_Y \cdot L_Z$ (Cartesian)

### Numerical Skill Checklist
- ☑ Calculate repeatability from $\bar{l}$ and $\sigma_l$ (ISO 9283 method).
- ☑ Calculate availability from MTBF/MTTR.
- ☑ Calculate R(t) over a specified mission time.
- ☑ Calculate annual downtime in hours.
- ☑ Calculate workspace volume (Cartesian + Articulated).
- ☑ Interpret numerical results in 1–2 engineering sentences.

### Conceptual Skill Checklist
- ☑ Distinguish accuracy vs. repeatability with examples.
- ☑ Explain why both are needed (correctness + consistency).
- ☑ Discuss why *effectiveness* ≠ *performance* (must include safety + reliability).
- ☑ Map factors to application domains (surgical, automotive, warehouse).
- ☑ Discuss the trade-off between speed and safety in cobots.

### Common Exam Traps
- Accuracy and repeatability are **independent** — always state this explicitly.
- A robot can be **repeatable but not accurate** (calibration issue).
- A robot can be **accurate on average but not repeatable** (noise/jitter issue).
- MTBF ≠ MTTF (MTBF is for *repairable* systems; MTTF is for *non-repairable*).
- Availability is a **steady-state** metric, not an instant metric.
- Safety Integrity Level (SIL) is **not the same as robot generation** or "version number".

### High-Yield One-Liners for the Exam
- *"Repeatability measures the robot's precision; accuracy measures its correctness."*
- *"A high MTBF and low MTTR together produce high availability."*
- *"Resolution finer than tolerance is necessary but not sufficient for accuracy."*
- *"Workspace defines 'where' the robot can act; payload defines 'what' it can act upon."*
- *"A robot's effectiveness is a function of its accuracy, repeatability, speed, and safety."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot-Related Reliability Measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT831 - SAFETY AND RELIABILITY IN ROBOTIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Robot-Related Reliability Measures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot-Related Reliability Measures** are a set of **quantitative probabilistic metrics** used to characterize the probability that a robotic system, sub-system, or component will perform its intended safety function under stated conditions for a specified period of time, without failure.

In the context of KTU's OERUT831 framework (aligned with **ISO 10218-1/-2** for industrial robots and **ISO 13849-1** for safety-related control systems), reliability is *not* just a maintenance metric — it is the **mathematical backbone** that links robot hardware performance to **functional safety**, **Performance Level (PL)** classification, and **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** determination.

The principal measures discussed in Module 2 are:

- **MTTF** – Mean Time To Failure (non-repairable components: bearings, sensors, end-effector grippers)
- **MTBF** – Mean Time Between Failures (repairable systems: robot controller, drive unit)
- **MTTR** – Mean Time To Repair (diagnostics + logistics + actual repair)
- **Failure Rate (λ)** – failures per hour, often expressed in **FIT** (Failures In Time = 10⁻⁹ /h)
- **Reliability Function R(t)** – probability of survival up to time *t*
- **Availability A(t)** – fraction of time the robot is operationally ready
- **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** – fraction of failures detected by automatic diagnostics
- **PFH / PFD** – Probability of Failure per Hour / on Demand (per IEC 61508)
- **B10 / B10d** – Number of cycles until 10% of components have failed (per ISO 13849)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Robot as a Delivery Truck"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine a delivery truck that must deliver parcels **every working day** in a city. To keep the operation running, the manager tracks:
> - **Average km travelled before a breakdown** → that is the **MTBF**.
> - **Average hours the truck sits in the garage being fixed** → that is the **MTTR**.
> - **Probability the truck survives a full 8-hour shift without breaking down** → that is the **Reliability R(t)**.
> - **Fraction of total time the truck is on the road (vs. in the garage)** → that is the **Availability A**.
>
> Substitute the truck for a 6-axis industrial robot arm on a factory line: the *kilometres* become *operating hours*, and the *garage* becomes the *maintenance bay*. **Safety-critical** reliability adds one more layer: *if the truck's brake fails during a downhill run, what is the probability someone gets hurt?* — that probability is the **PFH** (Probability of Failure per Hour) of the safety function.

---

## 1.3 The Reliability Engineering Triangle for Robots

Robot reliability is a **three-cornered balance** between **Hardware Robustness**, **Diagnostic Intelligence**, and **Maintainability**. The product specification defines a **target Performance Level (PL a → PL e)** or **SIL (1 → 4)**, and the engineer must demonstrate, by calculation, that the chosen architecture meets that target — typically through the **PFH value**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For a collaborative robot (cobot) operating in *Power and Force Limiting* (PFL) mode per **ISO/TS 15066**, the safety-rated monitored stop, the force-sensing skin, and the speed-reduction logic are all *individual sub-systems* whose reliabilities **multiply (series combination)** to form the overall robot safety reliability.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Failure Rate (λ) — The Foundation Stone

The **instantaneous failure rate** λ(t) is the conditional probability density that a component fails in the next infinitesimal interval *dt*, given that it has survived until time *t*:

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

where **f(t)** is the probability density of failure and **R(t)** is the reliability function.

### The "Bathtub Curve" Logic

Most robotic components exhibit three behavioural regions over their operational lifetime:

1. **Infant Mortality (Burn-in, t < 100 h):** λ decreases — caused by manufacturing defects, poor solder joints, firmware bugs. *Quality control & burn-in testing reduces this region.*
2. **Useful Life (t ≈ 100 → 50 000 h):** λ is approximately **constant** (the random-failure region). *This is where the exponential model R(t) = e^(–λt) is valid.*
3. **Wear-out (t > 50 000 h):** λ increases — bearings pit, gears wear, electrolytic capacitors dry out. *Preventive replacement triggers the cycle restart.*

> [!NOTE]
> For a robot operating on a 24/7 production line, the controller is in region (2) for years, but the harmonic-drive reducers in each joint move into region (3) after ≈ 20 000 h. This is why **predictive maintenance (vibration analysis on each axis)** is mandatory under **ISO 10218-1:2011, Clause 7.2**.

---

## 2.2 Reliability Function R(t) — Probability of Survival

R(t) is the probability that a robot (or component) performs its function from time 0 to time *t* without failure.

$$
R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)
$$

where **F(t)** is the cumulative distribution function of time-to-failure **T**.

Under the **constant-failure-rate** assumption (region 2 of the bathtub curve), the differential equation **dR(t)/dt = –λ·R(t)** integrates to the **Exponential Reliability Law**:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

This is the single most-used formula in robot reliability engineering because robotic systems are usually specified by the manufacturer in the constant-failure-rate region.

---

## 2.3 Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) and MTBF

**MTTF** is the expected time to first failure of a *non-repairable* component. **MTBF** includes the repair time for *repairable* systems:

$$
MTBF = MTTF + MTTR
$$

For a constant failure rate λ, the mean lifetime is the area under the reliability curve:

$$
MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

For *repairable* robotic systems, the **MTBF** in hours is the inverse of the steady-state failure rate. Standardised per-channel failure rates (per **IEC 61508, Table B.1**) are tabulated in **FIT** units. To convert:

$$
\lambda \text{ (per hour)} = \frac{FIT \times 10^{-9}}{1 \text{ hour}}
$$

---

## 2.4 Availability A — The Operational Uptime Fraction

**Availability** is the steady-state probability that a robot is operational at any randomly chosen instant. For a repairable system that cycles between *Up* (duration MTBF – MTTR) and *Down* (duration MTTR):

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{Up\text{-}time}{Total\ Time}
$$

A robot cell with MTBF = 2 000 h and MTTR = 8 h achieves **A = 2000/2008 ≈ 0.9960** → **"Three-Nines" availability**. Modern automotive welding lines require A ≥ 0.99 (≈ 90 % effective utilisation after all losses).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intrinsic vs. Achieved Availability:** *Intrinsic* A assumes perfect logistics and unlimited spares. *Achieved* A includes Mean Logistic Delay and Mean Administrative Delay. KTU 2024 problems typically work with **Intrinsic A**.

---

## 2.5 Diagnostic Coverage (DC) — The Smart Watchdog

Per **ISO 13849-1, Annex E**, **DC** is the ratio of the *dangerous failure rate* detected by the diagnostic function to the *total dangerous failure rate*:

$$
DC = \frac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}
$$

where:
- **λ_DD** = dangerous failures *detected* by diagnostics (e.g., watchdog timer catches servo overcurrent)
- **λ_DU** = dangerous failures *undetected* (latent fault in a redundant channel that self-test cannot see)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse DC with **Test Coverage** (software testing). DC applies to **automatic, on-line, continuous** diagnostics running during normal robot operation.

---

## 2.6 Probability of Failure per Hour (PFH) — The Safety Number

For **continuous-mode** safety functions (e.g., emergency stop, safe torque off, speed monitoring in a cobot), the relevant KTU / IEC 61508 metric is:

$$
PFH = \lambda_{DU} \times t_{cycle} + \sum \text{(channel CCF contributions)}
$$

A simplified, exam-friendly form for a **single-channel, Category 1/2 architecture** is:

$$
PFH = \lambda_{DU} \times 1\,h
$$

Target ranges per IEC 61508 / ISO 13849:

| Safety Demand | Target PFH (per hour) | Required PL / SIL |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Low | 10⁻⁵ ≤ PFH < 10⁻⁴ | PL c / SIL 1 |
| Medium | 10⁻⁶ ≤ PFH < 10⁻⁵ | PL d / SIL 2 |
| High | 10⁻⁷ ≤ PFH < 10⁻⁶ | PL e / SIL 3 |

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 80 % of Module 2 calculation problems.**

| # | Measure | Formula | Domain / Unit | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Failure Rate (constant) | $\lambda(t) = \lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTTF}$ | failures / hour | Valid only in wear-out-free region |
| 2 | Reliability (Exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless (0→1) | Memoryless property holds |
| 3 | Unreliability F(t) | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | CDF of failure time |
| 4 | MTTF | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | hours | For exponential distribution only |
| 5 | MTBF (repairable) | $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$ | hours | Intrinsic availability input |
| 6 | Availability (steady) | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless | 0 < A < 1 |
| 7 | Series Reliability | $R_{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | dimensionless | Single-point-failure tolerant |
| 8 | Parallel Reliability | $R_{sys} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | dimensionless | "n-out-of-n" hot redundancy |
| 9 | Diagnostic Coverage | $DC = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{DD} + \lambda_{DU}}$ | 0 < DC ≤ 1 | Per ISO 13849-1 Annex E |
| 10 | PFH (single channel) | $PFH = \lambda_{DU} \times 1\,h$ | per hour | Conservative; channel CCF ignored |
| 11 | FIT Conversion | $1\,FIT = 10^{-9}\,/h$ | per hour | Divide channel λ by 10⁹ |
| 12 | B10d → MTTFd | $MTTF_d = \dfrac{B_{10d}}{0.1 \times n_{op}}$ | years | $n_{op}$ = operations per year |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These twelve equations are the arithmetic of every **Safety Integrity Level (SIL) determination** for a robot. A KTU 2024 problem will typically give you λ values in FIT, an operation cycle, and an architecture (1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2), and ask you to compute PFH and recommend a PL/SIL.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of R(t) = e^(–λt) from the Constant Failure Rate

**Premise:** In the *useful life* region of the bathtub curve, the conditional failure rate is constant:

$$
\lambda(t) = \lambda \quad (\text{constant, independent of } t)
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the definition of λ(t):**

$$
\lambda = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables** (R(t) and t on opposite sides):

$$
\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda\, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides from 0 to t**, with the initial condition R(0) = 1 (the component is certainly alive at t = 0):

$$
\int_{0}^{t} \frac{1}{R(\tau)} \cdot \frac{dR}{d\tau}\, d\tau = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} d\tau
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals:**

$$
\ln\bigl(R(t)\bigr) - \ln\bigl(R(0)\bigr) = -\lambda t
$$

**Step 5 — Apply R(0) = 1**, so ln R(0) = 0:

$$
\ln\bigl(R(t)\bigr) = -\lambda t
$$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate both sides** to obtain the **Exponential Reliability Law**:

$$
\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key property unlocked:** The exponential distribution is *memoryless*. P(T > t + s \mid T > t) = e^(–λs) — the robot's residual life is independent of how long it has already been running. This is why MTBF can be *re-set* to the original value after every repair.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of MTTF = 1/λ for the Exponential Distribution

**Definition:** Mean Time To Failure is the expected value of the time-to-failure random variable T:

$$
MTTF = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 1 — Express f(t) in terms of R(t):** The PDF of failure time is the negative derivative of R(t):

$$
f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = \lambda\, e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 2 — Use integration by parts** on the expectation integral. Let u = t, dv = f(t) dt. Then du = dt, v = –e^(–λt):

$$
E[T] = \bigl[-t\, e^{-\lambda t}\bigr]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the boundary term.** As t → ∞, the exponential term t·e^(–λt) → 0 (exponential decay dominates any polynomial). At t = 0, the term is 0:

$$
\bigl[-t\, e^{-\lambda t}\bigr]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - 0 = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the remaining integral:**

$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \left[\frac{-1}{\lambda}\, e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(\frac{-1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude the MTTF identity:**

$$
\boxed{MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}}
$$

**Engineering check:** A servo motor with λ = 5 × 10⁻⁶ /h has MTTF = 1/(5×10⁻⁶) = 200 000 h ≈ **22.8 years of continuous operation** — but in practice we replace the brushes long before that (wear-out region).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Series-System Reliability

**Premise:** A robot safety chain consists of *n* independent sub-systems in **series** (any one failing breaks the safety function). Examples: *sensor → logic solver → contactor → motor driver*.

**Step 1 — For independent events, the joint reliability is the product of individual reliabilities** (probability rule):

$$
R_{sys}(t) = P(T_1 > t \cap T_2 > t \cap \dots \cap T_n > t)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply independence:**

$$
R_{sys}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(T_i > t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)
$$

**Step 3 — For identical sub-systems** with common failure rate λ and exponential life:

$$
R_{sys}(t) = \bigl(e^{-\lambda t}\bigr)^n = e^{-n \lambda t}
$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent failure rate** of the system:

$$
\lambda_{sys} = n \cdot \lambda
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) \quad \text{and} \quad \lambda_{sys} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** Adding sub-systems *in series* always *decreases* reliability. The weakest link dominates — a robot with three 0.99-reliable modules in series has R = 0.99³ ≈ 0.970, not 0.99. This is why **redundancy (parallel paths)** is essential to reach PL d / PL e.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Parallel (Redundant) System Reliability

**Premise:** Two identical channels operate in parallel ("1-out-of-2" or 1oo2 voting). The system survives if **at least one** channel works.

**Step 1 — System fails only if *all* channels fail.** Use the complement:

$$
R_{sys}(t) = 1 - P(\text{all fail}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(1 - R_i(t)\bigr)
$$

**Step 2 — For n = 2 identical channels** with R(t) = e^(–λt):

$$
R_{sys}(t) = 1 - \bigl(1 - e^{-\lambda t}\bigr)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the binomial:**

$$
R_{sys}(t) = 1 - \bigl(1 - 2e^{-\lambda t} + e^{-2\lambda t}\bigr) = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}
$$

**Step 4 — General n-out-of-n parallel form:**

$$
\boxed{R_{sys}(t) = 1 - \bigl(1 - e^{-\lambda t}\bigr)^n}
$$

**Numerical check:** If single-channel reliability is 0.90, then 1oo2 parallel gives:

$$
R_{sys} = 1 - (0.10)^2 = 0.99
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Caveat — Common Cause Failures (CCF):** The 0.99 figure is *theoretical*. Real parallel systems suffer **β-factor CCF** (e.g., both channels fail from the same over-voltage event). The KTU-standard **β = 10 %** correction multiplies the result by (1 – β) = 0.90, giving 0.90 × 0.99 = 0.891 effective reliability.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Steady-State Availability

**Premise:** A repairable robot cycles through **Up-state** of mean duration **MTBF – MTTR** and **Down-state** of mean duration **MTTR**.

**Step 1 — Total cycle length:**

$$
T_{cycle} = (MTBF - MTTR) + MTTR = MTBF
$$

**Step 2 — Fraction of cycle in Up-state** equals steady-state availability:

$$
A = \frac{\text{Up-time}}{\text{Total time}} = \frac{MTBF - MTTR}{MTBF}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to the canonical form:**

$$
\boxed{A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in failure and repair rates** (λ = 1/MTBF, μ = 1/MTTR):

$$
A = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}
$$

**Worked example:** A SCARA robot has λ = 0.0005 /h and μ = 0.125 /h (i.e., MTTR = 8 h):

$$
A = \frac{0.125}{0.0005 + 0.125} = \frac{0.125}{0.1255} = 0.9960
$$

→ **99.60 %** intrinsic availability. The robot is unavailable for **35 hours per year** (8760 × 0.004).

---

## 3.6 Derivation of B10d → MTTFd Conversion (per ISO 13849-1)

**Background:** Manufacturers of electromechanical components (contactors, relays, position switches) publish a **B10d** value — the number of operations at which 10 % of the population has *dangerously* failed. ISO 13849-1 converts this to an **MTTFd** per channel:

$$
MTTF_d = \frac{B_{10d}}{0.1 \times n_{op}}
$$

where **n_op** = average number of operations per year.

**Derivation of the 0.1 factor:** By the definition of B10d, 10 % of components fail after B10d cycles. Assuming a constant hazard (exponential), the failure fraction after N cycles is F = 1 – e^(–λN). Setting F = 0.10 and N = B10d:

$$
1 - e^{-\lambda B_{10d}} = 0.10 \Rightarrow e^{-\lambda B_{10d}} = 0.90
$$

$$
-\lambda B_{10d} = \ln(0.90) \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{-\ln(0.90)}{B_{10d}} = \frac{0.10536}{B_{10d}}
$$

Per ISO 13849, the conversion approximates the constant failure rate and divides by the *average annual operation count*:

$$
\boxed{MTTF_d = \frac{B_{10d}}{0.1 \times n_{op}}}
$$

**Worked example:** A safety contactor has B10d = 1 000 000 cycles. The robot cell performs 1 cycle per minute, 8 h/day, 250 days/year:

$$
n_{op} = 1 \times 60 \times 8 \times 250 = 120\,000 \text{ operations/year}
$$

$$
MTTF_d = \frac{1\,000\,000}{0.1 \times 120\,000} = \frac{1\,000\,000}{12\,000} \approx 83.3\ \text{years}
$$

This **MTTFd ≈ 83 years** places the contactor in the **"High" MTTFd band** of ISO 13849-1, Table 5.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Reliability Measure

> [!NOTE]
> Use this matrix in KTU problems to pick the **correct formula** for the question stem.

| Problem Stem Clue | Measure to Use | Governing Standard |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| "Probability the robot works for *t* hours" | R(t) = e^(–λt) | IEC 61508 |
| "Average hours between failures, repair included" | MTBF = MTTF + MTTR | ISO 13849-1 |
| "Fraction of time robot is on the production line" | A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR) | IEC 61709 |
| "Probability of dangerous undetected failure per hour" | PFH = λ_DU | IEC 61508-1, Table 3 |
| "Number of operations until 10 % dangerous failure" | Convert B10d → MTTFd | ISO 13849-1, C.4 |
| "Two redundant E-Stops, both must fail" | R_p = 1 – (1 – R₁)(1 – R₂) | ISO 13849-1, Annex D |
| "Emergency stop on demand, low demand mode" | PFD_avg | IEC 61508-1, Table 2 |
| "Series: sensor → PLC → contactor → drive" | R_sys = ∏ R_i | Basic probability |

---

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Robot Reliability Measures (Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Robot Reliability Domain] --> nodeBlock1[Component Level]
    nodeStart --> nodeBlock2[System Level]
    nodeStart --> nodeBlock3[Safety Function Level]

    nodeBlock1 --> nodeMTTF[Mean Time To Failure MTTF]
    nodeBlock1 --> nodeLambda[Failure Rate Lambda]
    nodeBlock1 --> nodeB10d[B10d Cycles]
    nodeBlock1 --> nodeFIT[Channel Failure Rate FIT]

    nodeBlock2 --> nodeMTBF[Mean Time Between Failures MTBF]
    nodeBlock2 --> nodeMTTR[Mean Time To Repair MTTR]
    nodeBlock2 --> nodeAvail[Steady State Availability A]
    nodeBlock2 --> nodeRt[Reliability Function R of t]

    nodeBlock3 --> nodeDC[Diagnostic Coverage DC]
    nodeBlock3 --> nodePFH[Probability of Failure per Hour PFH]
    nodeBlock3 --> nodePFD[Probability of Failure on Demand PFD]
    nodeBlock3 --> nodePL[Performance Level PL a to e]
    nodeBlock3 --> nodeSIL[Safety Integrity Level SIL 1 to 4]

    nodeLambda --> nodeRt
    nodeLambda --> nodeMTTF
    nodeMTTF --> nodeMTBF
    nodeMTTR --> nodeMTBF
    nodeMTBF --> nodeAvail
    nodeMTTF --> nodeAvail
    nodeB10d --> nodePL
    nodeDC --> nodePL
    nodeDC --> nodePFH
    nodeLambda --> nodePFH
    nodePL --> nodeSIL
    nodePFH --> nodeSIL
```

---

## 4.2 Reliability Block Diagram of a 6-Axis Industrial Robot Safety Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeUser[Operator Demand] --> nodeEStop[E Stop Button]
    nodeEStop --> nodeSafePLC[Safety PLC Category 3]
    nodeSafePLC --> nodeContactor1[Contactor Channel A]
    nodeSafePLC --> nodeContactor2[Contactor Channel B]
    nodeContactor1 --> nodeServo[Servo Drive STO]
    nodeContactor2 --> nodeServo
    nodeServo --> nodeMotor[Robot Joint Motor]
    nodeMotor --> nodeEnd[Safe Stop Achieved]

    nodeDiag[On Line Diagnostic DC 90 percent] -. monitors .-> nodeSafePLC
    nodeDiag -. monitors .-> nodeContactor1
    nodeDiag -. monitors .-> nodeContactor2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The two contactor channels are in **parallel (1oo2)** — a single contactor can still cut power. The **Diagnostic Module** continuously tests for welded contacts and reports them. Series elements (E-Stop, PLC, Servo) must all work; parallel element (Contactors) can tolerate one failure.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow of Robot Reliability Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeSpec[Step 1 Define Safety Function] --> nodeArch[Step 2 Choose Architecture Category]
    nodeArch --> nodeLambda1[Step 3 Collect Component Lambda in FIT]
    nodeLambda1 --> nodeCalPL[Step 4 Calculate MTTFd per Channel]
    nodeCalPL --> nodeCalDC[Step 5 Calculate Diagnostic Coverage DC]
    nodeCalDC --> nodeCalPFH[Step 6 Calculate PFH for System]
    nodeCalPFH --> nodeCheckPL{Step 7 PFH within Target PL or SIL}
    nodeCheckPL -- Yes --> nodeAccept[Step 8 Accept and Document]
    nodeCheckPL -- No --> nodeAddRed[Step 9 Add Redundancy or Increase DC]
    nodeAddRed --> nodeArch
```

---

## 4.4 Fault Tree for "Robot Fails to Stop on E-Stop Press"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeTop[TOP Robot Does Not Stop on E Stop] --> nodeOr1[OR Gate]
    nodeOr1 --> nodeE1[E Stop Button Mechanically Stuck]
    nodeOr1 --> nodeOr2[OR Gate Wiring or Logic Failure]
    nodeOr1 --> nodeOr3[OR Gate Power Cut Fails]
    nodeOr2 --> nodeE2[Safety PLC Program Fault]
    nodeOr2 --> nodeE3[Signal Wire Broken]
    nodeOr2 --> nodeE4[PLC CPU Watchdog Failure]
    nodeOr3 --> nodeE5[Contactor A Welded Closed]
    nodeOr3 --> nodeE6[Contactor B Welded Closed]
    nodeOr3 --> nodeE7[STO Input of Drive Open Circuit]
    nodeE5 --> nodeAnd1[AND Gate Common Cause Over Voltage]
    nodeE6 --> nodeAnd1
    nodeAnd1 -. beta factor 10 percent .-> nodeE5
    nodeAnd1 -. beta factor 10 percent .-> nodeE6
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the fault tree:** The **TOP event** (robot doesn't stop) occurs if **any** of the three OR-gate branches occur. The **AND-gate** captures **Common Cause Failure (CCF)**: both contactors fail *simultaneously* from one over-voltage event. The dotted **β = 10 %** annotation is the standard CCF discount per **ISO 13849-1, Annex F**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OERUT831]`

**Define the terms MTBF, MTTR, and Availability as applied to industrial robots. Derive the steady-state relationship between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** The average operating time between two consecutive failures of a *repairable* robotic system, including the repair time. (1 mark)
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** The average time required to restore a failed robot to operational status, including diagnosis, parts logistics, and actual repair. (1 mark)
- **Availability (A):** The steady-state probability that the robot is operational at a randomly chosen instant. The canonical relation is **A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)**. A robot with MTBF = 2 000 h and MTTR = 8 h has A = 2000/2008 = 0.9960, i.e., **99.60 % uptime**. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OERUT831]`

**What is Diagnostic Coverage (DC) in a robot safety system? Explain the terms λ_DD and λ_DU with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Diagnostic Coverage (DC):** Per **ISO 13849-1 Annex E**, DC is the ratio of dangerous failures *detected* by the on-line diagnostic function to the *total* dangerous failure rate of a sub-system. Formula: **DC = λ_DD / (λ_DD + λ_DU)**. (1 mark)
- **λ_DD (Detected Dangerous):** Failures that are *caught* by automatic diagnostics, e.g., a self-test detects a stuck relay contact and switches the robot to a safe state. (1 mark)
- **λ_DU (Undetected Dangerous):** Failures that *escape* the diagnostics and remain latent, e.g., a logic solver corruption that the watchdog timer cannot recognise. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, OERUT831 Module 2]`

A 6-axis industrial robot is used in an automotive welding cell. The safety chain consists of:
- **Emergency stop button** with λ = 5 × 10⁻⁷ /h
- **Safety PLC** (Category 3) with λ = 1 × 10⁻⁶ /h
- **Two contactors in parallel** (1oo2), each with λ = 8 × 10⁻⁶ /h
- **Servo drive STO** with λ = 2 × 10⁻⁶ /h
- **Diagnostic coverage** of the PLC = 90 %

The MTTR for the entire cell is 4 hours.

#### (a) Calculate the PFH of the safety chain and recommend the achievable Performance Level. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Combine series elements (E-Stop, PLC, STO)** using series reliability and equivalent failure rate:

$$
\lambda_{series} = \lambda_{EStop} + \lambda_{PLC} + \lambda_{STO}
$$

$$
\lambda_{series} = 5 \times 10^{-7} + 1 \times 10^{-6} + 2 \times 10^{-6} = 3.5 \times 10^{-6}\,/h
$$

**Step 2 — Combine the two parallel contactors.** For identical 1oo2 architecture:

$$
R_{parallel}(t) = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}
$$

At PFH timescale (t = 1 h, very small), expand the Taylor series. The dangerous undetected failure rate of the parallel pair is:

$$
\lambda_{DU,\,parallel} \approx \lambda_1 \cdot \lambda_2 \cdot t \cdot (1 - \beta)
$$

where β = 10 % (CCF factor per ISO 13849-1, Annex F), t = 1 h, λ₁ = λ₂ = 8 × 10⁻⁶ /h:

$$
\lambda_{DU,\,parallel} = (8 \times 10^{-6}) \times (8 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1 \times 0.90
$$

$$
\lambda_{DU,\,parallel} = 5.76 \times 10^{-11}\,/h
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Diagnostic Coverage to the PLC:**

$$
\lambda_{DU,\,PLC} = \lambda_{PLC} \times (1 - DC) = 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.10 = 1 \times 10^{-7}\,/h
$$

**Step 4 — Total PFH (sum all λ_DU contributions):**

$$
PFH = \lambda_{series} + \lambda_{DU,\,PLC} + \lambda_{DU,\,parallel}
$$

$$
PFH = 3.5 \times 10^{-6} + 1 \times 10^{-7} + 5.76 \times 10^{-11}
$$

$$
PFH \approx 3.6 \times 10^{-6}\,/h
$$

**Step 5 — Map to Performance Level (per ISO 13849-1, Table 4):**

| PFH range (per hour) | Performance Level |
|:---:|:---:|
| 10⁻⁵ ≤ PFH < 10⁻⁴ | PL c |
| **10⁻⁶ ≤ PFH < 10⁻⁵** | **PL d** ✅ |
| 10⁻⁷ ≤ PFH < 10⁻⁶ | PL e |

Our PFH = 3.6 × 10⁻⁶ /h falls in the **PL d** band.

**Conclusion:** The robot safety chain achieves **Performance Level d** with Category 3 architecture, suitable for most automotive welding-cell applications. **(7 marks awarded)**

---

#### (b) Calculate the steady-state availability of the cell and the annual downtime in hours. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute total failure rate λ_total of the system** (without redundancy simplification):

$$
\lambda_{total} = \lambda_{EStop} + \lambda_{PLC} + \lambda_{STO} + \lambda_{contactor,\,effective}
$$

For a parallel contactor pair, the *system* failure rate (either contactor works) is reduced. Using the small-λ approximation for the parallel pair:

$$
\lambda_{effective,\,parallel} \approx \lambda^2 \times t = (8 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times 1 = 6.4 \times 10^{-11}\,/h
$$

$$
\lambda_{total} = 5 \times 10^{-7} + 1 \times 10^{-6} + 2 \times 10^{-6} + 6.4 \times 10^{-11}
$$

$$
\lambda_{total} \approx 3.5 \times 10^{-6}\,/h
$$

**Step 2 — Compute MTBF:**

$$
MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda_{total}} = \frac{1}{3.5 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 285\,714\,h
$$

**Step 3 — Apply availability formula with MTTR = 4 h:**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{285\,714}{285\,714 + 4} = \frac{285\,714}{285\,718}
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx 0.999986}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual downtime (8760 h/year):**

$$
T_{down} = 8760 \times (1 - A) = 8760 \times 1.4 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.122\,h/year
$$

**Conclusion:** The robot cell is available **99.9986 %** of the time, with an annual downtime of only **~7.3 minutes**. The cell meets the demanding **99 %+ availability** target of high-volume automotive production. **(7 marks awarded)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, OERUT831 Module 2, Alternative]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the reliability block diagram of a robot safety chain. Compute the system reliability for a 1oo2 architecture of two contactors, each having R = 0.95. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (text-based representation for the exam sheet):**

```
   [E-Stop]──[Safety PLC]──┬──[Contactor A]──┐
                            │                 ├──[Servo STO]──[Robot Motor]
                            └──[Contactor B]──┘
```

The E-Stop, PLC, Servo STO, and Robot Motor are in **series** (any single failure breaks the function). The two contactors are in **parallel** (1oo2 voting).

**Reliability Computation for 1oo2 parallel:**

The system fails only if **both** contactors fail:

$$
P(\text{system fail}) = P(A\text{ fails}) \times P(B\text{ fails}) = (1 - 0.95) \times (1 - 0.95) = 0.05 \times 0.05 = 0.0025
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{1oo2} = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975}
$$

> The 1oo2 architecture raises single-channel reliability from **0.95 → 0.9975**, a 10× improvement. **(7 marks awarded)**

---

**(b) Discuss the Performance Level (PL) classification per ISO 13849-1. Show how a robot safety function achieving PFH = 8 × 10⁻⁷ /h would be classified, and the role of Diagnostic Coverage in this determination. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**ISO 13849-1 PL Classification:** The Performance Level (PL) is a discrete five-level scale (a, b, c, d, e) indicating the reliability of a safety function under **reasonable foreseeable conditions**. The higher the PL, the more reliable the safety function. Required PFH ranges (per hour) are:

| PL | PFH (per hour) | Typical Application |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| a | 10⁻⁵ ≤ PFH < 10⁻⁴ | Low-risk — guarding with fixed fence |
| b | 10⁻⁶ ≤ PFH < 10⁻⁵ | Light guarding |
| c | 10⁻⁶ ≤ PFH < 10⁻⁵ | Interlocked gates |
| d | 10⁻⁷ ≤ PFH < 10⁻⁶ | Cobot speed monitoring, hand-guiding |
| e | 10⁻⁸ ≤ PFH < 10⁻⁷ | Highest — press safety, E-Stop on hazardous machinery |

**Classification of PFH = 8 × 10⁻⁷ /h:**

The value **8 × 10⁻⁷ /h** lies in the interval **10⁻⁷ ≤ PFH < 10⁻⁶**, which corresponds to **Performance Level d (PL d)**. This is the typical rating for **collaborative robot safety functions** such as Power and Force Limiting and Speed-and-Separation Monitoring (SSM) per **ISO/TS 15066**.

**Role of Diagnostic Coverage (DC):** Per ISO 13849-1, DC is a multiplicative factor in the PFH computation. A higher DC **reduces λ_DU** and therefore *shifts the achievable PL upward*. For example:

- **DC = 60 %** (low diagnostics) — typical Category 2 architecture — limits the system to **PL c** at best.
- **DC = 90 %** (medium diagnostics) — Category 3 — enables **PL d**.
- **DC = 99 %** (high diagnostics) — Category 4 with cross-monitoring — enables **PL e**.

**Conclusion:** To achieve **PL d** for the cobot, the engineer must (i) select components with sufficiently low λ, (ii) implement diagnostics with **DC ≥ 90 %**, and (iii) document the PFH calculation per the standard's graph-based method (ISO 13849-1, Annex K). **(7 marks awarded)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 2 (each trap costs 1–3 marks):**
> 1. **Confusing MTBF with MTTF.** MTBF = MTTF + MTTR (for repairable systems). Writing *MTBF = 1/λ* in a repairable-system question is **wrong** — that is MTTF.
> 2. **Forgetting to include β-factor (10 % CCF)** in parallel-redundancy problems. The examiner will deduct 1 mark for an unrealistically high PFH.
> 3. **Using PFD where the question asks for PFH** (and vice versa). *Low-demand* mode → PFD; *continuous* mode → PFH. Robot E-Stop is **continuous mode → PFH**.
> 4. **Mixing units.** λ in /h must be used with time in *h* throughout. Converting 8760 h/year without converting λ loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting the (1 – DC) factor on the PLC.** Always split the channel λ into λ_DD = DC·λ and λ_DU = (1 – DC)·λ.
> 6. **Confusing B10 with B10d.** B10 is the *total* failure count; B10d is the *dangerous* failure count. ISO 13849-1 uses **B10d** only.
> 7. **Writing the availability formula upside down:** A = MTBF/(MTBF+MTTR), **not** A = MTTR/(MTBF+MTTR). The latter is the *unavailability* Q.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Robot-Related Reliability Measures**
> - **Reliability R(t) = e^(–λt)** — Exponential model, valid in the constant-failure-rate (useful-life) region of the bathtub curve.
> - **MTTF = 1/λ** for exponential distribution; **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR** for repairable robotic systems.
> - **Steady-state availability A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR) = μ / (λ + μ).** Watch out for the orientation of the fraction.
> - **Series reliability** — *Weakest link dominates.* Multiply: **R_sys = ∏ Rᵢ.** Adding sub-systems always *decreases* system reliability.
> - **Parallel (1oo2) reliability** — **R_sys = 1 – (1 – R₁)(1 – R₂).** Provides redundancy; tolerates one channel failure.
> - **Diagnostic Coverage DC = λ_DD / (λ_DD + λ_DU).** Higher DC = lower λ_DU = better achievable PL.
> - **PFH = λ_DU × 1 h** for single-channel continuous-mode safety functions (E-Stop, STO, speed monitoring).
> - **Performance Levels** (ISO 13849-1): a, b, c, d, e. **Safety Integrity Levels** (IEC 61508): 1, 2, 3, 4. PL d ↔ SIL 2; PL e ↔ SIL 3.
> - **B10d → MTTFd conversion:** **MTTFd = B10d / (0.1 × n_op)**, where n_op is annual operation count. Result is in *years* per ISO 13849-1.
> - **Common Cause Failure (CCF):** Always apply the **β = 10 %** discount to parallel-redundancy PFH calculations per **Annex F** of ISO 13849-1.
> - **1 FIT = 10⁻⁹ /h** — the standard unit for channel failure rates in functional-safety datasheets.
> - **Collaborative robots (cobots)** typically target **PL d, Category 3, DC ≥ 90 %** for safe speed monitoring and PFL functions per **ISO/TS 15066**.
> - **For exam problems**, identify the question stem first (continuous vs. on-demand, repairable vs. non-repairable, series vs. parallel) **before** picking a formula from the cheat sheet in Section 2.7.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Reliability Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT831 - SAFETY AND RELIABILITY IN ROBOTIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Robot Reliability Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robot Reliability Analysis** is the quantitative engineering discipline that evaluates the probability that a robotic system (including its mechanical structure, actuators, sensors, controllers, and software) will perform its intended function without failure, under specified operating conditions, for a designated period of time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Reliability of a robotic system is the probability that the system, including all its hardware, software, and human–machine interaction sub-systems, will accomplish its designated task under stated conditions within a defined time interval."*

Mathematically, reliability is expressed as a function of time:

$$
R(t) = P(T \geq t)
$$

where $T$ is the **time-to-failure random variable**, and $t$ is the mission time.
