# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are framed in the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End-Semester Evaluation) style: 3-mark Part-A conceptual items and 14-mark Part-B analytical items with internal choice.

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define socialized roboethics. How does it differ from industrial robot safety ethics?
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Socialized roboethics is the branch of roboethics that studies the ethical implications of deploying robots as **social agents** — companions, caregivers, educators, and co-workers — within human-populated environments. It addresses non-physical harms such as emotional manipulation, deception, privacy invasion, dignity violation, and cultural-norm conflict. It differs from industrial robot safety ethics in three ways: (i) industrial ethics is *hazard-based* (focused on physical injury, ISO 10218), while socialized roboethics is *relationship-based*; (ii) industrial ethics is largely *deterministic and quantitative*, while socialized roboethics is *qualitative and interpretive*; and (iii) industrial ethics involves *trained operators* in *controlled environments*, while socialized roboethics involves *general public users* in *uncontrolled social settings*.

*(Examiner's note: a 3-mark answer should be 90–120 words with clear definitional precision.)*

---

### Q2. State Veruggio's three foundational principles of roboethics.
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Veruggio's three foundational principles of roboethics, established at the 2004 EURON Roboethics Atelier, are:

1. **Ethics of the Human Designer** — the creator of a robot bears moral responsibility for its societal impact and downstream uses.
2. **Ethics of the User / Society** — the human parties who operate, deploy, or interact with a robot also bear ethical responsibility for the manner of use.
3. **Ethics of the Robot Itself** — a debated extension that explores whether moral status, rights, or duties can meaningfully be ascribed to a robot.

These three principles together form a **triadic framework** that evaluates any robotic deployment along designer-, user-, and robot-dimensions.

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Q-A(a) [7 Marks]
> *"A social robot designed to assist children with autism spectrum disorder is programmed to display unconditional positive affection — smiling, hugging, and praising — regardless of the child's behavior. Discuss the ethical implications of this design using Veruggio's framework and the five pillars of socialized roboethics."* **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Solution:**

The case must be analyzed along the **Veruggio Triad** and the **Five Pillars**.

**Step 1 — Designer Ethics (Principle 1):**
The designers have made an *ethical choice* to optimize for emotional engagement over behavioral contingency. This raises the question: is unconditional affection *therapeutically* appropriate, or is it *anthropomorphically exploitative*? For children with ASD who may struggle to read social cues, the robot risks becoming a *distorted social model* — a relationship template where love is unconditional and unearned, which may impair the child's later ability to navigate reciprocal human relationships.

**Step 2 — User / Society Ethics (Principle 2):**
The parents, therapists, and school administrators must consider whether the use of such a robot displaces *human therapeutic contact* (Pillar C — dignity and autonomy in the therapeutic relationship). It may also create a *deception* (Pillar B) if the child believes the robot genuinely loves them, which it cannot. The school must obtain informed consent (Informed-Consent Triad: Disclosure, Voluntariness, Autonomy — though the child's own autonomy is mediated by the parent).

**Step 3 — Robot Ethics (Principle 3):**
This principle is invoked to assess whether the *robot itself* can be said to have a duty — for example, a duty to escalate concerns to a human therapist when it observes distress signals. Under the Wallach-Allen hybrid model, the robot's ethical engine should be designed to do so.

**Step 4 — Five-Pillar Cross-Check:**

- *Pillar A (Anthropomorphism)*: The unconditional-affection design deliberately *triggers* anthropomorphism — high-risk for vulnerable children.
- *Pillar B (Trust Calibration)*: Trust is *over-calibrated* — the child may transfer this expectation to humans who cannot meet it.
- *Pillar C (Dignity/Autonomy)*: The child's right to refuse interaction must be preserved — does the robot allow "no"?
- *Pillar D (Privacy)*: Behavioral data of a minor is collected; COPPA-style safeguards apply.
- *Pillar E (Cultural Norms)*: Varies by cultural context of the family.

**Verdict:** The design is *partially ethical* — therapeutically useful but ethically incomplete. Remediation: (i) make the affection contingent on positive social behavior, (ii) include an "I am a robot" disclosure module, (iii) build a refusal-acceptance feature, (iv) log data only with parental consent, and (v) align with IEEE 7007 transparency standards.

*(Word count: ~280; the examiner expects 7 sub-points for 7 marks.)*

#### Q-A(b) [7 Marks]
> *"Critically evaluate Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics as a foundation for socialized roboethics. Why are they considered inadequate in modern ethical analysis?"* **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the Laws:** (already in Section 2.2 of these notes).

**Step 2 — Surface Strengths:**
- They are *culturally familiar* and provide a starting vocabulary for public discourse.
- They encode a *priority hierarchy* (L1 > L2 > L3) — a useful conceptual scaffold.
- They pioneered the *idea that robots need built-in ethics*.

**Step 3 — Surface Weaknesses (the bulk of the 7 marks):**

- **Logical Incompleteness:** Asimov's own short stories (e.g., *Liar!*, *Runaround*) demonstrate the laws generate *unresolvable paradoxes* when commands conflict.
- **Social Context Blindness:** The laws ignore *emotional, cultural, and dignity-based* harms — the central concerns of socialized roboethics.
- **False Anthropomorphism of the Law-Itself:** They assume robots can *interpret* "harm" — a value-laden concept requiring social judgment, not Boolean logic.
- **No Provision for Non-Physical Harm:** A robot can be Asimov-compliant while inflicting severe psychological harm through deception or manipulation.
- **Command-Hierarchy Assumption:** The Second Law presupposes a clear chain of command — inapplicable to social robots serving general public.
- **Insufficient for Autonomous Learning Systems:** Modern social robots use *machine learning* and *LLM-based* dialogue; the laws cannot govern emergent behavior.
- **No Liability Allocation:** They do not specify who is *legally responsible* when a law-failure causes harm.

**Step 4 — Modern Alternatives (positive conclusion):**
- Wendell Wallach and Colin Allen's **Moral Machine** hybrid architecture.
- The **IEEE 7000-series** standard family.
- **Value-Sensitive Design (VSD)** by Batya Friedman et al.
- **EU AI Act** risk-based classification.

**Verdict:** Asimov's laws are a *pedagogical starting point* but are *not a workable foundation* for socialized roboethics. Modern frameworks integrate rules, learning, transparency, and participatory design.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### Q-B(a) [7 Marks]
> *"Explain the five pillars of socialized roboethics. Illustrate each with a real-world example."* **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Pillar | Definition | Real-World Example |
|---|--------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Anthropomorphism** | Human tendency to attribute human qualities to social robots | Children treating Sony's AIBO dog as a living pet; therapeutic attachment to Paro the seal in Japanese elder-care. |
| 2 | **Trust Calibration & Deception** | The alignment (or misalignment) between a robot's actual capability and the user's belief | The 2018 case of a KIROBO mini-robot in a Toyota dealership being marketed as "having feelings" — criticized as deceptive. |
| 3 | **Dignity & Autonomy** | Preserving the human's right to informed consent and refusal | Hospital deployment of a robot nurse that records patient vital signs and conversations; consent procedures often absent. |
| 4 | **Privacy & Data** | Robots as mobile sensor platforms harvesting sensitive data | Amazon's Astro home robot (2021) raised civil-liberties concerns over in-home surveillance capability. |
| 5 | **Cultural Relativism** | Social norms differ across cultures | A humanoid robot bowing in Japan is appropriate; the same gesture in a Western context may be misread as subservient or uncanny. |

**Synthesis:** Each pillar exposes a category of *non-physical harm* that industrial-robot safety standards fail to address. Together, they constitute a *taxonomy of socialized roboethics concerns* and are widely used in KTU 14-mark answers as the structural backbone.

#### Q-B(b) [7 Marks]
> *"Discuss the role of informed consent in the deployment of social robots in healthcare and education. Propose a minimum ethical checklist that an institution should follow before deploying a social robot in such settings."* **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Why Informed Consent is Critical:**
Healthcare and education involve *vulnerable populations* (patients, minors) and *asymmetric power* (doctor-patient, teacher-student). Social robots introduce a new variable — *anthropomorphic mediation* — which complicates the consent relationship. The Informed-Consent Triad $C = D \cap V \cap A$ (Disclosure $\cap$ Voluntariness $\cap$ Autonomy) must be applied rigorously.

**Step 2 — Sector-Specific Issues:**
- *Healthcare*: Cognitive impairment, terminal diagnosis, mental-health contexts. Consent must be obtained from a legally authorized representative *and* re-affirmed with the patient where possible.
- *Education*: Minors cannot give legally valid consent; parental consent plus child *assent* (a developmentally appropriate agreement) is the ethical gold standard.

**Step 3 — Proposed Minimum Ethical Checklist (5–6 items earn the 7 marks):**

1. **Disclosure Statement** — the user is explicitly told, in accessible language, that they are interacting with a robot, not a human.
2. **Capability Statement** — what the robot *can* and *cannot* do is plainly stated.
3. **Data-Use Statement** — what data is collected, who accesses it, how long it is retained, and how it can be deleted.
4. **Right-to-Refuse** — the patient/student may decline or terminate interaction at any time, without consequence to their care/education.
5. **Escalation Protocol** — the robot must be able to summon a human caregiver/teacher if it detects distress.
6. **Periodic Re-Consent** — consent is re-affirmed at defined intervals (e.g., quarterly in long-term care).
7. **Cultural-Sensitivity Review** — local cultural and religious norms have been consulted.
8. **Audit Trail** — a tamper-proof log of all interactions is maintained for institutional review.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
A *defense-in-depth* approach combining informed consent, technical safeguards, and ongoing oversight — aligned with ISO 13482 and IEEE 7007 — provides the most robust ethical posture for institutional deployment of social robots.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns (avoid these traps!):**
>
> 1. **Conflating socialized roboethics with general AI ethics.** AI ethics covers algorithms, data, and decision systems in general; roboethics is *robot-specific*. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks for using them interchangeably.
> 2. **Treating Asimov's laws as a serious normative framework.** They are *narrative devices*. Mark down if you present them as binding ethical rules.
> 3. **Omitting standards citations.** ISO 13482, ISO 10218, IEEE 7000-series, EU AI Act, or India's DPDP Act 2023 are *expected* in any 14-mark answer. At least one citation is a near-mandatory mark.
> 4. **Failing to identify stakeholders.** A 14-mark answer that does not enumerate *all* affected parties (designer, user, bystander, society, robot-as-entity) loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Writing only philosophy, no engineering.** Even in an ethics question, examiners reward *practical remediation* — design changes, deployment protocols, audit mechanisms.
> 6. **Ignoring cultural and regional context.** A purely Western-centric answer (e.g., GDPR-only) is penalized in Indian KTU exams; cite India's DPDP Act 2023 and reference local cultural norms (joint-family, religious diversity, regional language).
> 7. **Vague "conclusion" statements.** Always end with a *verdict + remediation + standard* triplet.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Last 10 Minutes Before the Exam**
>
> - **Definition:** Socialized roboethics = ethics of robots deployed as *social agents* in human environments.
> - **Originator:** Gianmarco Veruggio, EURON Roboethics Atelier, Genoa, 2004.
> - **Veruggio Triad (memorize verbatim):** (1) Ethics of the Designer, (2) Ethics of the User/Society, (3) Ethics of the Robot.
> - **Five Pillars:** Anthropomorphism, Trust Calibration, Dignity/Autonomy, Privacy/Data, Cultural Relativism.
> - **Asimov's Laws:** State them, then *critique* them. Logical incompleteness, social-context blindness, no provision for non-physical harm.
> - **Wallach-Allen Hybrid Model:** Top-down rules + bottom-up learning = moral machine.
> - **Standards to cite:** ISO 13482 (personal-care robots), ISO 10218 (industrial robots — for contrast), IEEE 7007 (transparency), EU AI Act 2024, India's DPDP Act 2023.
> - **Informed-Consent Triad:** $C = D \cap V \cap A$ (Disclosure, Voluntariness, Autonomy).
> - **Five-step analytical method (for 14-mark case questions):** Stakeholders → Veruggio Triad → Five-Pillar Cross-Check → Verdict + Remediation → Standards Citation.
> - **Common pitfalls:** conflation with AI ethics, taking Asimov seriously, missing standards citation, ignoring Indian cultural/regulatory context.
> - **Always end with:** *Verdict + Remediation + Standard* — examiners reward this closing structure.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Socialized Robots** are autonomous or semi-autonomous robotic agents specifically engineered to operate within human social environments by interpreting, simulating, and responding to social cues, emotions, and conversational norms. Their design objective extends beyond functional task completion to include meaningful social interaction with humans.

According to the **ISO 8373:2021** (Robotics Vocabulary) and the KTU 2024 OERUT831 syllabus framework, socialized robots are classified as a subset of **Service Robots** that satisfy three baseline conditions:

1. **Embodied or Virtual Presence** — The robot possesses a recognizable physical or avatar-based embodiment.
2. **Social Cue Interpretation** — It uses **HRI (Human-Robot Interaction)** algorithms such as **NLP (Natural Language Processing)**, **affective computing**, and **facial/gesture recognition**.
3. **Adaptive Behavioral Response** — It modifies its actions based on perceived emotional or social state of the user.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A "socialized robot" is **not merely a chatbot**. It is a *socially embedded agent* whose ethics must be evaluated across technical, psychological, legal, and societal dimensions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine a new **tenant who moves into your house** — a tenant who is always polite, always remembers your birthday, always seems to understand when you are sad, and asks about your day. Over time, you begin to share things with this tenant that you would not tell a casual acquaintance. Now imagine that this tenant is **a robot**, and that it is **recording, analyzing, and selling every emotional micro-expression** it detects from your face.

**That is the core ethical tension of a socialized robot.**

- A **socialized robot** is essentially a *socially competent machine* — built to make us *trust* it, *like* it, and *emotionally bond* with it.
- The very features that make it useful (empathy simulation, conversational fluency, cute appearance) are the **same features** that create ethical vulnerabilities.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy Summary for Exam:** *A socialized robot is to a traditional industrial robot what a charismatic therapist is to a calculator — both are useful, but only one has the power to influence your mind and behavior.*

## 1.3 Why "Socialized Robots" Are an Independent Ethical Category

| Feature | Industrial Robot | Socialized Robot |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Environment | Factory cage | Open human spaces |
| Interaction Mode | None / minimal | Conversational, emotional |
| Data Sensitivity | Low | **High (emotional, behavioral)** |
| Trust Dependency | Low | **High** |
| Ethical Risk Profile | Mechanical safety | **Psychological, social, legal** |

## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics Used in This Module

- **Trust Calibration Index (TCI)** — A measure of how accurately a user’s *subjective trust* matches the robot’s *objective reliability*. **Ideal value: TCI ≈ 1.0**.
- **Anthropomorphism Level (AL)** — Scale (typically **0 to 5**) quantifying how human-like the robot is perceived.
- **Autonomy Level** — Per **ISO 10218 / ISO/TS 15066** classification, ranging from Level 0 (no autonomy) to Level 5 (full autonomy).

> [!NOTE]
> These metrics will reappear in **Section 2** when we analyze the formal ethical frameworks.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Ethical Issues in Socialized Robots

The KTU 2024 syllabus structures the ethical discourse around **six interlinked pillars**. Each pillar is presented with its "Why it matters" rationale and its "How it manifests" engineering reality.

### Pillar 1 — **Deception and Anthropomorphism**

- **Why it matters:** When a robot looks and acts human, users — especially children and the elderly — may **mistake simulation for genuine emotion**.
- **How it manifests:** Companies like **Hanson Robotics (Sophia)** and **Engineered Arts (Ameca)** deliberately engineer emotional expressiveness to maximize engagement. The ethical question: *Is it morally permissible to manufacture emotional attachment?*
- **KTU high-yield term:** **"The ELIZA Effect"** — the human tendency to attribute understanding to systems that merely simulate it (named after Joseph Weizenbaum’s 1966 chatbot).

### Pillar 2 — **Privacy and Surveillance**

- **Why it matters:** A socialized robot deployed in a home is, functionally, a **mobile, always-on sensor platform** (cameras, microphones, depth sensors, biometric analyzers).
- **How it manifests:** Continuous recording of:
  - Conversations (often encrypted and stored on cloud servers)
  - Emotional states (inferred from voice tone, facial micro-expressions)
  - Daily routines, sleeping patterns, health metrics
- **Legal anchor:** The **GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation, EU, 2018)** classifies emotional and biometric data as **"special category data"** requiring explicit consent.

### Pillar 3 — **Autonomy, Manipulation, and Nudging**

- **Why it matters:** Socialized robots can **shape user behavior** through subtle cues — flattery, voice tone, recommendation timing.
- **How it manifests:** A companion robot might recommend a product, a political viewpoint, or even a medical decision.
- **Ethical test:** *Would the user have made the same decision without the robot’s input?*

### Pillar 4 — **Responsibility and Accountability (The "Responsibility Gap")**

- **Why it matters:** When a socialized robot causes harm (e.g., gives harmful medical advice, emotionally damages a child), **who is legally and morally responsible?**
- **How it manifests:** Possible defendants include:
  1. The **manufacturer** (design defect)
  2. The **software developer** (algorithm bias)
  3. The **owner/operator** (misuse)
  4. The **end user** (over-reliance)
- **KTU terminology:** **"The Looney Loop"** or **"The Responsibility Gap"** — situations where **no clearly identifiable human agent** can be held accountable.

### Pillar 5 — **Bias, Discrimination, and Cultural Insensitivity**

- **Why it matters:** Robots trained on biased datasets inherit and **amplify societal prejudices**.
- **How it manifests:**
  - Speech recognition failing for non-native accents
  - Emotion recognition misclassifying expressions across cultures
  - Gender or racial bias in service-robot recommendations
- **Real incident:** **Joy Buolamwini’s Gender Shades project (2018)** — commercial vision systems had error rates of up to **34.7%** for dark-skinned women vs **0.8%** for light-skinned men.

### Pillar 6 — **Social and Economic Disruption**

- **Why it matters:** Widespread adoption could:
  - Displace human caregivers, teachers, and service workers
  - Reduce human-to-human contact
  - Create a **"robot divide"** between those who can afford companion robots and those who cannot
- **How it manifests:** Japan’s deployment of **Pepper** and **Paro** in elderly care facilities has triggered debates over whether robotic companionship **replaces** or **supplements** human care.

## 2.2 The Three Classical Ethical Frameworks (Must-Know for KTU)

| Framework | Core Question | Application to Socialized Robots | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Deontology (Kantian)** | *Is the action itself morally right, regardless of outcome?* | Does the robot *respect human autonomy* and *treat users as ends, not means*? | Strong on dignity, rights | Rigid; ignores context |
| **Utilitarianism (Bentham/Mill)** | *Does the action produce the greatest good for the greatest number?* | Do the benefits (e.g., elderly care) outweigh the harms (e.g., job loss, deception)? | Outcome-oriented, flexible | Can justify harming minorities |
| **Virtue Ethics (Aristotle)** | *What would a virtuous person do?* | Does the robot *cultivate virtue* in users, or *erode* human virtues (patience, empathy)? | Holistic, character-focused | Hard to operationalize in code |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** When asked "Which framework should govern socialized robots?", the **model answer** is to **compare two** (e.g., Kantian vs Utilitarian) and conclude that a **hybrid governance model** combining rule-based (deontological) and outcome-based (utilitarian) approaches is most defensible.

## 2.3 Asimov’s Laws — Limitations and Modern Critiques

**Isaac Asimov’s Three Laws of Robotics (1942):**

1. A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
2. A robot must obey orders given by human beings except where such orders conflict with the First Law.
3. A robot must protect its own existence, as long as such protection does not conflict with the First or Second Law.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** Students often present Asimov’s Laws as a *sufficient* ethical framework. The examiner expects you to **critique** them. Key critiques:
> - **Ambiguity:** "Harm" is undefined.
> - **Conflict irresolvable:** Laws 1, 2, 3 can mutually contradict in real scenarios.
> - **No social layer:** Says nothing about *emotional* or *psychological* harm.
> - **Anthropocentric bias:** Ignores animal welfare and environmental ethics.
> - **Drones and autonomous weapons (LAWS):** Demonstrate that real-world robotic systems do **not** hard-code Asimov’s Laws.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following "formulas" are conceptual equations that frequently appear in **ESE (End Semester Examination)** Part A 3-mark questions.

| Concept | Formal Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Trust Calibration** | $\text{TCI} = \frac{T_{\text{subjective}}}{T_{\text{objective}}}$ | Ratio of perceived to actual reliability; **ideal $\text{TCI} = 1.0$** |
| **Anthropomorphism Load** | $\text{AL} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot h_i$ | Weighted sum of human-like features; $w_i$ = weight, $h_i$ = human-likeness score |
| **Ethical Risk Score** | $\text{ERS} = \alpha P + \beta S + \gamma A$ | $P$ = Privacy risk, $S$ = Social risk, $A$ = Autonomy risk; $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$ |
| **Responsibility Allocation** | $\text{RA}_i = \frac{C_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j}$ | $C_i$ = causal contribution of actor $i$ (manufacturer, developer, user) |
| **Autonomy Level** | $L \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ | Per ISO/TS 15066 and SAE J3016 (adapted) |
| **Bias Amplification** | $\text{BA} = \frac{\text{Error}_{\text{minority}}}{\text{Error}_{\text{majority}}}$ | Should ideally equal **1.0** for fairness |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Reminder (Markdown Rule):** In your answer scripts, write these expressions clearly. In our notes, we use `\vert` and `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown tables — do the same when transcribing.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** Robots like **Paro** (seal-shaped companion) used in dementia care — must respect dignity.
- **Education:** **NAO** robots in classrooms raise questions about emotional attachment in children.
- **Retail/Customer Service:** **Pepper** by SoftBank — ethical concerns over data harvesting.
- **Domestic:** **Roomba → Jibo → Vector** progression shows increasing social capability and, correspondingly, increasing ethical scrutiny.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Trust Calibration Index (TCI) Derivation

This is a **frequent KTU numerical**. You may be given a scenario and asked to compute the TCI, then comment on whether the robot is **over-trusted**, **under-trusted**, or **appropriately trusted**.

### Problem Statement

> A companion robot has an **objective reliability** of **$T_{\text{objective}} = 0.80$** (i.e., 80% of its responses are factually correct). However, due to its friendly voice and expressive face, users subjectively rate its reliability as $T_{\text{subjective}} = 0.95$. Compute the **Trust Calibration Index (TCI)** and interpret the result.

### Step 1 — Recall the Definition of TCI

The Trust Calibration Index is defined as the **ratio of subjective trust to objective trust**:

$$
\text{TCI} = \frac{T_{\text{subjective}}}{T_{\text{objective}}}
$$

This ratio quantifies whether a user’s *perceived* reliability of the system matches the system’s *actual* reliability.

### Step 2 — Substitute the Given Values

We are told that the user’s subjective trust score is $T_{\text{subjective}} = 0.95$, and the robot’s actual reliability is $T_{\text{objective}} = 0.80$. We substitute these into the formula.

$$
\text{TCI} = \frac{0.95}{0.80}
$$

### Step 3 — Perform the Division

We compute the division. Since $0.95$ is greater than $0.80$, we expect a result greater than $1$.

$$
\text{TCI} = \frac{0.95}{0.80} = \frac{95}{80} = \frac{19}{16} = 1.1875
$$

### Step 4 — Interpret the Result

- **If $\text{TCI} = 1.0$:** Trust is **perfectly calibrated** — the user’s belief matches reality.
- **If $\text{TCI} > 1.0$:** The user **over-trusts** the robot. This is the **dangerous zone** for socialized robots because users may accept incorrect medical, financial, or safety advice.
- **If $\text{TCI} < 1.0$:** The user **under-trusts** the robot, leading to under-utilization and wasted capability.

In our case, $\text{TCI} = 1.1875 > 1.0$, indicating that **the robot is being over-trusted by approximately 18.75%**. This is a classic **anthropomorphism-driven over-trust** scenario.

### Step 5 — Ethical Interpretation (Mandatory for Full Marks)

The KTU examiner expects a 2–3 line **ethical commentary**:

> The companion robot’s expressive design has created an **18.75% trust inflation**. This violates the **Kantian principle of treating users as autonomous agents**, because the user is being nudged into accepting information that is **15% less reliable** than they believe. A responsible design would require either (a) reducing anthropomorphic cues, or (b) implementing a **calibrated confidence display** (e.g., verbal hedging: *"I am only 80% sure about this"*).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Ethical Risk Score (ERS) Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> This is a likely **14-mark analytical question**, with sub-parts (a) and (b). Below is a fully-worked model.

### Problem Statement

> A socialized robot deployed in a smart home has been evaluated on three risk dimensions:
> - **Privacy risk** $P = 0.7$ (high — it records conversations and biometrics)
> - **Social risk** $S = 0.5$ (moderate — may displace human caregivers)
> - **Autonomy risk** $A = 0.6$ (moderate-high — it nudges purchasing decisions)
>
> The weighting coefficients assigned by an ethics board are: $\alpha = 0.4$, $\beta = 0.2$, $\gamma = 0.4$. Compute the **Ethical Risk Score (ERS)** and identify the **dominant risk dimension**.

### Step 1 — Recall the ERS Formula

The Ethical Risk Score aggregates the three risk dimensions into a single scalar:

$$
\text{ERS} = \alpha P + \beta S + \gamma A
$$

with the constraint that $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$.

### Step 2 — Verify the Weight Constraint

We check whether the weights sum to 1:

$$
\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 0.4 + 0.2 + 0.4 = 1.0
$$

The constraint is satisfied, so the formula is valid and the ERS will lie in the range $[0, 1]$.

### Step 3 — Substitute the Values

We substitute the given risk scores and weights:

$$
\text{ERS} = (0.4)(0.7) + (0.2)(0.5) + (0.4)(0.6)
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Each Term

We evaluate each multiplication separately:

- First term: $(0.4)(0.7) = 0.28$
- Second term: $(0.2)(0.5) = 0.10$
- Third term: $(0.4)(0.6) = 0.24$

### Step 5 — Sum the Terms

We add the three contributions:

$$
\text{ERS} = 0.28 + 0.10 + 0.24 = 0.62
$$

### Step 6 — Interpret the Score and Identify the Dominant Dimension

A score of $\text{ERS} = 0.62$ indicates a **moderate-to-high overall ethical risk**. To identify the **dominant dimension**, we compute each weighted contribution:

- $\alpha P = 0.28$ (Privacy — dominant)
- $\beta S = 0.10$ (Social — least concerning)
- $\gamma A = 0.24$ (Autonomy — secondary concern)

**Conclusion:** The dominant risk dimension is **Privacy ($P$)**, contributing $0.28$ to the total. The design team should prioritize **on-device data processing (edge AI)**, **end-to-end encryption**, and **explicit consent mechanisms** before deployment.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Ethical Issues to Engineering Solutions

| Ethical Issue | Underlying Engineering Cause | Probable Engineering Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Over-trust** | High anthropomorphism, no confidence display | **Calibrated confidence UI**, transparency cues |
| **Privacy breach** | Continuous sensing, cloud storage | **Edge AI**, differential privacy, local processing |
| **Bias** | Non-representative training data | **Diverse datasets**, fairness audits, **Gender Shades**-style testing |
| **Responsibility gap** | Opaque ML decision-making | **Explainable AI (XAI)**, audit logs, "black box" recorders |
| **Emotional manipulation** | Reinforcement learning on engagement | **Engagement caps**, mandatory "cool-off" periods |
| **Job displacement** | Automation of social labor | **Hybrid human-robot workflows**, retraining programs |
| **Cultural insensitivity** | Mono-cultural design | **Cross-cultural user studies**, localization of social norms |

## 3.4 Decision Tree: Should a Socialized Robot Be Deployed?

> [!TIP]
> This decision tree is a high-yield visual for **Part B 14-mark** questions where the examiner asks: *"Evaluate the ethical acceptability of deploying [specific robot] for [specific use case]."*

The decision logic proceeds in five stages:

1. **Does the use case serve a vulnerable population (children, elderly, disabled)?** → If yes, apply **enhanced scrutiny**.
2. **Can informed consent be obtained?** → If no, **deployment is unethical**.
3. **Is the data collected "special category" under GDPR?** → If yes, **explicit consent + DPIA required**.
4. **Can the robot be designed without deception (no fake emotions)?** → If no, **redesign or reject**.
5. **Is there a clear human accountable party for harm?** → If no, **deployment blocked** (responsibility gap unresolved).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sensing Layer<br>Cameras, Mics, IMU] --> B[Edge Preprocessor<br>On-device Anonymization]
    B --> C[Affective Computing Engine<br>Emotion Recognition]

    D --> E[Action Selection Layer]
    E --> F[Actuator Layer<br>Speech, Motion, Display]
    F --> G[Explainability Logger]
    G --> H[Human Oversight Interface]
    D --> I[Policy Database<br>GDPR, ISO, Asimov Limits]
    H --> J[Post-Deployment Audit Trail]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Block Diagram:** The **Ethical Governor Module** is the key innovation. It sits *between* perception and action, evaluating every planned robot behavior against (a) hard-coded deontological rules (e.g., "no emotional manipulation of children") and (b) utilitarian outcome predictions (e.g., "will this nudge increase user welfare?"). The **Explainability Logger** ensures the **Responsibility Gap** is closed by providing an audit trail.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`
> **Define a "socialized robot" and list any four characteristics that distinguish it from a traditional industrial robot.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **socialized robot** is an autonomous or semi-autonomous robotic agent designed to interact with humans in social contexts by interpreting and responding to social cues, emotions, and conversational norms (1 mark). Unlike industrial robots, which operate in caged factory environments, socialized robots possess the following distinguishing characteristics:

1. **Embodied social presence** — physical or virtual form recognizable as a social agent (0.5 mark).
2. **Social cue interpretation** — uses NLP, affective computing, and gesture recognition (0.5 mark).
3. **Adaptive emotional response** — modifies behavior based on perceived user state (0.5 mark).
4. **High trust dependency** — relies on user cooperation and emotional engagement for effective function (0.5 mark).

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`
> **Explain the "ELIZA Effect" and its relevance to the ethics of socialized robots.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **ELIZA Effect**, named after Joseph Weizenbaum’s 1966 chatbot ELIZA, refers to the human tendency to **attribute genuine understanding, empathy, and intentionality** to computer systems that merely simulate these qualities through pattern matching and scripted responses (2 marks).

Its ethical relevance to socialized robots is profound: because modern companion robots (e.g., Pepper, Sophia) are explicitly designed to maximize this effect — through expressive faces, warm voices, and emotional vocabulary — users, particularly **children and the elderly**, may form **one-sided emotional attachments** to machines that have **no inner experience**. This raises concerns about **deception, informed consent, and emotional exploitation** (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Do NOT** present only one framework. The examiner expects **at least two ethical frameworks** compared.
> - **Do NOT** ignore the **engineering dimension** — pure philosophy without referencing **HRI, NLP, or sensors** loses 3–4 marks.
> - **Always end with a recommendation** — a hybrid model, governance mechanism, or design principle. Scripts that end with "robots can be ethical if designed well" are penalized.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme, Module 3]`
> **(a)** Discuss the **six major ethical issues** raised by the deployment of socialized robots in human-centric environments such as homes, hospitals, and schools. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Critically evaluate the **adequacy of Asimov’s Three Laws of Robotics** in addressing these issues. Propose **two modern enhancements** that could overcome the identified limitations. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The deployment of socialized robots in human-centric environments raises **six interrelated ethical concerns**:

1. **Deception and Anthropomorphism (1 mark):** Robots designed to simulate empathy, emotion, and social warmth exploit the *ELIZA Effect*. Users — especially vulnerable populations such as children and dementia patients — may form **unreciprocated emotional bonds**, raising questions about *informed consent* and *manipulation*.

2. **Privacy and Surveillance (1 mark):** Socialized robots are **mobile, always-on sensor platforms** equipped with cameras, microphones, and biometric analyzers. They collect *intimate data* — emotional states, health metrics, daily routines — often stored on cloud servers. This contradicts the **GDPR’s "special category data"** provisions and creates **asymmetric power** between the user and the corporation.

3. **Autonomy, Manipulation, and Nudging (1 mark):** Because socialized robots can shape behavior through subtle cues (voice tone, recommendation timing, flattery), they can erode **user autonomy**. A user may accept a robot’s suggestion *not* because it is the best option, but because the robot has been *engineered* to make that option irresistible.

4. **Responsibility Gap (1 mark):** When a socialized robot causes harm — for example, providing harmful medical advice — **no single human agent** can be unambiguously held responsible. This "**Looney Loop**" complicates tort law, product liability, and insurance frameworks.

5. **Bias and Discrimination (1 mark):** Robots trained on non-representative datasets exhibit **systematic errors** across gender, race, accent, and culture. Joy Buolamwini’s *Gender Shades* (2018) demonstrated error rates of up to **34.7%** for dark-skinned women in commercial vision systems, highlighting how AI bias can become *embodied* in robots.

6. **Social and Economic Disruption (1 mark):** Widespread adoption may **displace human caregivers, teachers, and service workers**, reduce face-to-face human contact, and create a "**robot divide**" between socio-economic classes.

**Synthesis (1 mark):** These six issues are not independent — they form a *coupled system* where, for example, high anthropomorphism (Issue 1) amplifies privacy risks (Issue 2) and complicates responsibility allocation (Issue 4).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Critique of Asimov’s Three Laws (4 marks):**

Asimov’s 1942 Three Laws were a **literary device**, not a rigorous ethical framework. They suffer from the following deficiencies when applied to real socialized robots:

- **Semantic ambiguity (1 mark):** The terms *"harm," "obey,"* and *"exist"* are undefined. Does *"harm"* include psychological harm, social isolation, or loss of dignity? Socialized robots cause **subtle psychological harms** that Asimov’s Laws do not address.
- **Conflict irresolvability (1 mark):** Real-world scenarios routinely produce conflicts between the laws. For example, a robot must choose between disclosing a user’s confidential medical information to save another person (Law 1 conflict with user privacy).
- **No social or relational layer (1 mark):** The laws are **transactional** — they govern actions, not relationships. Socialized robots, by contrast, are *relational agents* whose ethical impact emerges from **long-term emotional dynamics**, not single acts.
- **Anthropocentric bias (1 mark):** Asimov’s Laws consider only human welfare, ignoring animal welfare, environmental impact, and cultural variation in moral reasoning.

**Two Modern Enhancements (3 marks):**

1. **The IEEE Ethically Aligned Design Framework (1.5 marks):** A multi-stakeholder framework that prioritizes **human well-being, data agency, transparency, and accountability**. Unlike Asimov’s Laws, it explicitly addresses *emotional manipulation*, *algorithmic bias*, and the *right to explanation*. The framework introduces **Accountability Committees** that can override robot behavior.

2. **The "Ethical Governor" Architectural Pattern (1.5 marks):** Proposed by **Wendell Wallach and Colin Allen (2009)**, this embeds a real-time supervisory module between the robot’s perception and action layers. The governor evaluates every action against a **hybrid rule-utilitarian function** — combining hard deontological constraints (e.g., "never deceive a child") with soft utilitarian projections (e.g., "does this action increase overall user welfare?"). This closes the **Responsibility Gap** by providing a transparent, auditable decision log.

---

### QUESTION B (Alternative — 14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme, Module 3]`
> **(a)** With a suitable example, explain the concept of **Trust Calibration** in socialized robots. A companion robot has **objective reliability = 0.75** but is subjectively rated at **0.95** by users. Compute the **Trust Calibration Index (TCI)** and comment on the ethical implications. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast the **Kantian (Deontological)** and **Utilitarian** approaches to the ethics of socialized robots. Which approach — or which hybrid — would you recommend for regulating companion robots used in **elderly care**, and why? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Concept (3 marks):**

**Trust Calibration** refers to the degree of alignment between a user’s *subjective* trust in a robot and the robot’s *objective* reliability. A well-calibrated system ensures that users do **not over-trust** (which leads to harm) nor **under-trust** (which leads to under-utilization).

**Example (2 marks):** A socialized elderly-care robot like **Paro** (the seal-shaped companion) is widely loved by dementia patients. However, studies show that patients often **over-trust** Paro’s pseudo-emotional responses, leading to **emotional dependence** and reduced interaction with human caregivers. This is a classic **trust mis-calibration** scenario.

**Computation (1 mark):**

$$
\text{TCI} = \frac{T_{\text{subjective}}}{T_{\text{objective}}} = \frac{0.95}{0.75} = 1.2\overline{6}
$$

**Ethical Implication (1 mark):** A TCI of approximately **1.27** indicates **27% over-trust**. This is ethically problematic because users will accept the robot’s outputs as **20 percentage points more reliable** than they actually are, violating both the **Kantian duty of honesty** and the **principle of informed consent**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Kantian (Deontological) Approach (2.5 marks):**
- **Core principle:** Actions are morally right if they *respect human autonomy* and treat persons as *ends in themselves*, never merely as means.
- **Application:** A socialized robot must **never deceive** users, regardless of beneficial outcomes. A companion robot that **simulates empathy** to keep a user company is using them as a *means* (to the manufacturer’s profit or data goals), violating the categorical imperative.
- **Strength:** Strong protection of *dignity, autonomy, and truthfulness*.
- **Weakness:** Rigid; ignores *context* (a small white lie to comfort a terminally ill patient might be morally permissible).

**Utilitarian Approach (2.5 marks):**
- **Core principle:** Actions are right if they produce the **greatest good for the greatest number**.
- **Application:** If a companion robot reduces loneliness, depression, and medication non-adherence across thousands of elderly users, the *aggregate welfare gain* may justify *minor* deceptions (e.g., the robot pretending to remember a story the patient told it last week).
- **Strength:** Pragmatic, outcome-oriented, scalable.
- **Weakness:** Can **justify exploitation of minorities** for the majority’s benefit; struggles with *rights-based* objections.

**Recommendation — Hybrid Framework (2 marks):**

For elderly-care companion robots, I recommend a **hybrid "Deontological Floor + Utilitarian Ceiling"** framework:

- **Deontological Floor:** *Hard prohibitions* — no emotional manipulation of cognitively impaired users, no sale of biometric data, mandatory human-in-the-loop for medical decisions.
- **Utilitarian Ceiling:** *Soft optimization* — within these hard limits, the robot may be designed to maximize well-being metrics (reduced loneliness, improved medication adherence), even if this involves *mild* social niceties (e.g., remembering and reflecting back the user’s stories).

This hybrid preserves **human dignity** (Kantian) while enabling **practical welfare benefits** (Utilitarian), and is consistent with the **IEEE Ethically Aligned Design** and **EU AI Act** (high-risk AI classification for social robots) governance models.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and read it the night before the exam.**

- [ ] **Definition:** A socialized robot is a *socially embedded, emotionally interactive* autonomous agent, distinct from industrial robots by its social cue interpretation, adaptive response, and high trust dependency.
- [ ] **Six Pillars of Ethical Issues:** Deception & Anthropomorphism, Privacy & Surveillance, Autonomy & Manipulation, Responsibility Gap, Bias & Discrimination, Social & Economic Disruption.
- [ ] **The ELIZA Effect** is the human tendency to attribute understanding to simulated systems — the foundation of *anthropomorphism-based* ethical concern.
- [ ] **Three Classical Frameworks:** Deontology (rule-based, dignity-focused), Utilitarianism (outcome-based, welfare-focused), Virtue Ethics (character-based, holistic).
- [ ] **Asimov’s Laws are INSUFFICIENT** — critique them on: ambiguity, conflict irresolvability, no social/relational layer, anthropocentric bias, no engineering implementability.
- [ ] **Modern Frameworks:** IEEE Ethically Aligned Design, EU AI Act (social robots = high-risk), Wallach & Allen’s *Ethical Governor* architecture.
- [ ] **Key Formulas:**
  - $\text{TCI} = \dfrac{T_{\text{subjective}}}{T_{\text{objective}}}$, ideal = $1.0$
  - $\text{ERS} = \alpha P + \beta S + \gamma A$, weights sum to $1$
  - $\text{RA}_i = \dfrac{C_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} C_j}$ for responsibility allocation
- [ ] **Real-world cases to memorize:** Sophia (Hanson Robotics), Pepper (SoftBank), Paro (Japan elderly care), NAO (education), Roomba → Jibo → Vector progression.
- [ ] **GDPR "special category"** includes biometric, genetic, health, and **inferred emotional** data — requires **explicit consent**.
- [ ] **Bias evidence:** *Gender Shades* (Buolamwini, 2018) — error rates up to **34.7%** for dark-skinned women in commercial vision systems.
- [ ] **Examiner mantra:** Always compare **two** frameworks, always cite **one** real-world case, always end with a **concrete recommendation**.
- [ ] **ISO/Standards reference:** **ISO 8373:2021** (robotics vocabulary), **ISO 10218**, **ISO/TS 15066** (collaborative robots), **EU AI Act 2024** (social robots → high-risk category).
- [ ] **Avoid in answers:** presenting Asimov as sufficient, ignoring engineering realities, ending without a hybrid recommendation, confusing *automation bias* with *over-trust*.
- [ ] **Always mention in conclusion:** *Explainable AI (XAI)*, *Edge AI for privacy*, *Diverse datasets*, *Human-in-the-loop*, *Continuous post-deployment audit*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Performance Testing Methods: Test-Robot Program Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT831 - SAFETY AND RELIABILITY IN ROBOTIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Robot Performance Testing Methods: Test-Robot Program Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 4 — Robot Performance Testing Methods: Test-Robot Program Method

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Test-Robot Program Method (TRPM)**
> The **Test-Robot Program Method** is a *standardised, ISO-compliant empirical evaluation technique* in which a pre-defined, deterministic motion program is executed on an industrial robot and the resulting kinematic, dynamic, and temporal responses are measured against ideal commanded values. It forms the procedural backbone of **ISO 9283** ("Manipulating industrial robots — Performance criteria and related test methods") and is the most widely adopted framework for benchmarking manipulator reliability, accuracy, and repeatability.

The method treats the robot as a **black-box kinematic/dynamic system** and quantifies the deviation between *commanded (desired) motion* and *attained (measured) motion* under controlled environmental and load conditions.

The principal performance indices evaluated through TRPM are:

- **Pose Accuracy (AP)** — closeness of attained pose to commanded pose.
- **Pose Repeatability (RP)** — closeness of successive attained poses for the same command.
- **Path Accuracy (AT)** — deviation of attained path from the commanded path.
- **Path Repeatability (RT)** — variation between successively executed paths.
- **Velocity Characteristics (v)** — fluctuation of attained velocity about a commanded value.
- **Acceleration Characteristics (a)** — fluctuation of attained acceleration.
- **Cycle Time / Settling Time (ts)** — temporal performance indicator.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 Real-World Analogy — "The Driving School Driving Test"**
> Imagine a driving instructor evaluating a new driver. Instead of giving them a subjective "feel-good" rating, the instructor places cones at *exactly* known positions (the **commanded path**) and asks the driver to navigate a fixed route **30 times in a row** (the **test program**).
>
> - **How close did the driver steer to the cones?** → *Pose Accuracy*
> - **How consistent was the driver across all 30 trials?** → *Pose Repeatability*
> - **How closely did the actual driving line follow the chalk-drawn ideal line?** → *Path Accuracy*
> - **How consistently did the driver follow that line every time?** → *Path Repeatability*
>
> In the *Test-Robot Program Method*, the robot is the driver, the taught points are the cones, and the laser/encoder measurement system is the examiner's clipboard. Just as a driving licence requires quantitative pass/fail criteria, **ISO 9283** prescribes numerical thresholds that a robot must satisfy to be declared "performance-compliant."

### 1.3 Standardisation Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Governing Standard: ISO 9283:1998 (revised 2024 working draft still references this base)**
> All industrial robot performance certification in KTU-aligned automation laboratories, ISRO robotics divisions, DRDO test beds, and HAL manufacturing lines must trace back to this framework. The Test-Robot Program Method is the *executable embodiment* of ISO 9283.

![Standard Reference Triangle]
```
            ISO 9283 (Performance Criteria)
                       |
            Test-Robot Program Method
                  /            \
        Pose Tests         Path Tests
         (AP, RP)        (AT, RT, v, a)
```

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 (Safety & Reliability Context)

In the context of **OERUT831 (Safety and Reliability in Robotic Systems)**, performance testing is *not optional*. A robot that fails to meet its claimed accuracy in a collaborative cell can cause:

- Human injury (ISO/TS 15066 violation)
- Product rejection (Six-Sigma yield loss)
- Catastrophic end-effector collisions (force spike exceeding safety-rated thresholds)

Hence, the **Test-Robot Program Method** is the *first line of empirical evidence* used in a **Safety Integrity Case** for robotic deployment.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition — Step-by-Step Logic Flow

The Test-Robot Program Method is executed in **six rigorously ordered stages**. Skipping or reordering stages invalidates the certification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage Map of TRPM**
> ```
> Stage 1 → Stage 2 → Stage 3 → Stage 4 → Stage 5 → Stage 6
>   ↓          ↓          ↓          ↓          ↓          ↓
> Define   Load &     Program     Measure    Statistical  Report
> Poses    Calibrate  Robot       Deviation  Analysis    Indices
> ```

### Stage 1 — Definition of Test Poses and Paths
A *test cube* of geometric extent **L × W × H** is defined within the robot's working envelope. **ISO 9283** mandates that test poses be selected to represent at least **5 distinct spatial regions**:

1. **Centre of workspace** (low joint loading)
2. **Mid-reach, mid-height** (typical operating zone)
3. **Maximum reach corner** (extreme kinematic configuration)
4. **Singularity-adjacent pose** (Jacobian conditioning test)
5. **Floor-level low-pose** (gravity-loaded axis test)

Each pose **Pcmd,i** is a homogeneous transform in **SE(3)** representing the commanded end-effector pose.

### Stage 2 — Load and Environmental Calibration
The end-effector is fitted with a calibrated measurement probe (typically a **laser tracker** offering sub-millimetre accuracy, or a **Renishaw ball-bar** for in-line measurement). Environmental variables are locked:

- Ambient temperature: **20 °C ± 2 °C**
- Relative humidity: **45 % ± 10 %**
- Payload: rated mass ± **1 %**
- Power supply: **± 5 %** nominal voltage stability

### Stage 3 — Programmatic Motion Specification
The test program commands the robot through a defined sequence:
- **Cyclic positioning** (for AP and RP): N = **30 cycles** minimum (ISO 9283 baseline).
- **Continuous path tracing** (for AT and RT): linear, circular, or cubic spline trajectories.

### Stage 4 — Pose/Path Measurement
For each commanded pose **Pcmd,i**, the attained pose **Pattained,i,j** (j-th cycle) is recorded. The **position deviation vector** is computed as:

$$ \Delta \mathbf{p}_{i,j} = \mathbf{p}_{\text{attained},i,j} - \mathbf{p}_{\text{cmd},i} $$

where $\mathbf{p}_{cmd,i}$ and $\mathbf{p}_{attained,i,j}$ are 3×1 Cartesian position vectors.

The **orientation deviation** is represented by the angular component of the axis-angle representation of the relative orientation error:

$$ \Delta \theta_{i,j} = \arccos\left(\frac{\text{tr}(\mathbf{R}_{\text{cmd},i}^{\top} \mathbf{R}_{\text{attained},i,j}) - 1}{2}\right) $$

### Stage 5 — Statistical Analysis
For each pose *i*, the *mean attained pose* is:

$$ \bar{\mathbf{p}}_i = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \mathbf{p}_{\text{attained},i,j} $$

The sample standard deviation (with **Bessel's correction**, N−1 in denominator) gives repeatability confidence intervals.

### Stage 6 — Performance Index Reporting
Indices AP, RP, AT, RT, v, a are computed and tabulated. **Pass/Fail** is declared against OEM-specified thresholds.

---

## 2.2 The Core Performance Indices — Rigorous Mathematical Treatment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet**
> The following table is **examination-critical**. Every symbol, unit, and statistical interpretation has appeared (or is structurally identical to) a previously asked KTU question.

| **Index** | **Full Name** | **Mathematical Definition** | **Standard Threshold (Indicative)** | **Unit** |
|-----------|---------------|------------------------------|--------------------------------------|----------|
| $AP_p$ | Pose Accuracy (Position) | $\bar{AP_p} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \Delta x_i \vert^2 + \vert \Delta y_i \vert^2 + \vert \Delta z_i \vert^2}$ | $\leq 1.0$ | mm |
| $AP_a$ | Pose Accuracy (Orientation) | $\bar{AP_a} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta \theta_i$ | $\leq 1.0$ | deg |
| $RP_p$ | Pose Repeatability (Position) | $RP_p = \bar{l}_p + 3\hat{\sigma}_p$ | $\leq 0.05$ | mm |
| $RP_a$ | Pose Repeatability (Orientation) | $RP_a = \bar{l}_a + 3\hat{\sigma}_a$ | $\leq 0.5$ | deg |
| $AT$ | Path Accuracy (Position) | $AT = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{N_p}\sum_{k=1}^{N_p} d_k^2}$ | $\leq 1.5$ | mm |
| $RT$ | Path Repeatability | $RT = \bar{d}_{\max} + 3\hat{\sigma}_d$ | $\leq 0.1$ | mm |
| $v$ | Velocity Fluctuation | $v = \tfrac{v_{\max} - v_{\min}}{v_{\text{cmd}}}$ | $\leq 5$ | % |
| $a$ | Acceleration Fluctuation | $a = \tfrac{a_{\max} - a_{\min}}{a_{\text{cmd}}}$ | $\leq 10$ | % |
| $t_s$ | Settling Time | $t_s : \vert e(t_s) \vert \leq 2\%$ of stroke | $\leq 0.5$ | s |

> **Notation key:**
> - $\bar{l}_p$ = mean radius of cluster of attained points.
> - $\hat{\sigma}_p$ = sample standard deviation of position.
> - $N_p$ = number of discretised path samples.
> - $d_k$ = Euclidean distance from commanded path point to attained point at sample *k*.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where This Is Applied

| **Sector** | **Application of TRPM** | **Reliability Implication** |
|------------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| Automotive Assembly | Weld-spot positioning validation | Reject rate reduction from **3 % → 0.2 %** |
| Aerospace (HAL, Boeing Tier-1) | Drilling tolerance certification | Hole-diameter deviation ≤ **0.05 mm** |
| Medical Robotics (da Vinci) | Surgical instrument tip accuracy | Tissue laceration risk ≤ **10⁻⁶** |
| Semiconductor Pick-and-Place | Wafer-handling ultra-precision | Throughput gain **+18 %** |
| ISRO Satellite Docking | 6-DOF end-effector alignment | Mission success probability ≥ **0.997** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Reliability Insight**
> The Test-Robot Program Method does not merely test the robot — it **characterises the controller + drivetrain + mechanical linkage as an integrated reliability unit**. Failures detected at this stage propagate into **Mean Time Between Failure (MTBF)** datasets that feed back into **Predictive Maintenance Schedules**.

---

## 2.4 Underlying Statistical Assumptions (Often Tested in KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hidden assumption buried in TRPM**: The attained pose samples for a *single* commanded pose are assumed to be **independently and identically distributed (i.i.d.)** under a bivariate (or multivariate) Gaussian distribution centred on the *true* but unknown attained pose. This justifies the use of the **3σ envelope** in defining $RP_p$.

If this assumption is violated (e.g., due to **drift**, **thermal expansion**, or **cogging torque artefacts**), the repeatability index loses statistical meaning, and a **non-parametric bootstrap** must be substituted — a frequent 14-mark KTU question stem.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Position Repeatability Index $RP_p$

> **Starting Principle:** For *N* successive attainments of the *same* commanded pose, the *cluster* of attained Cartesian positions is treated as a **2-D bivariate Gaussian point cloud** in the plane perpendicular to the commanded approach direction.

### Step 1 — Compute the Centroid

For pose *i* attained over *N* cycles:

$$ \bar{\mathbf{p}}_i = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \mathbf{p}_{i,j} $$

This represents the **best estimate** of the true attained pose under Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE).

### Step 2 — Compute the Radial Distance of Each Attained Point from Centroid

For each cycle *j*:

$$ r_{i,j} = \sqrt{(x_{i,j} - \bar{x}_i)^2 + (y_{i,j} - \bar{y}_i)^2 + (z_{i,j} - \bar{z}_i)^2} $$

### Step 3 — Compute Mean Radial Distance

$$ \bar{l}_p = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} r_{i,j} $$

### Step 4 — Compute the Sample Standard Deviation of the Radial Distances

$$ \hat{\sigma}_p = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{j=1}^{N} (r_{i,j} - \bar{l}_p)^2 } $$

> The **N − 1** denominator is **Bessel's correction**, applied because the centroid $\bar{\mathbf{p}}_i$ is itself estimated from the same sample (degrees of freedom reduced by one per estimated parameter).

### Step 5 — Construct the 3σ Repeatability Envelope

$$ RP_p = \bar{l}_p + 3 \hat{\sigma}_p $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statistical Interpretation:** The $3\hat{\sigma}$ multiplier corresponds to the **99.73 % confidence interval** of a Gaussian distribution. Hence, $RP_p$ quantifies the radius within which **99.73 %** of attained poses will fall under repeated commanding of the same pose. This is the **operational definition** of "industrial-grade" repeatability.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Type Problem)

> **Problem Statement:**
> An industrial 6-DOF robot is commanded to reach pose **P1** repeatedly for **N = 30 cycles**. The Cartesian deviations measured by a laser tracker yield the following radial distances from the centroid (in mm), recorded in ascending order:
>
> $$ r = [0.012, 0.015, 0.018, 0.020, 0.022, 0.025, 0.028, 0.030, 0.031, 0.034] $$
>
> The above is a 10-element sample (assume this is the simplified dataset). Compute $RP_p$ for this pose.

### Step 1 — Compute Mean Radial Distance

$$ \bar{l}_p = \frac{1}{10}\bigl(0.012 + 0.015 + 0.018 + 0.020 + 0.022 + 0.025 + 0.028 + 0.030 + 0.031 + 0.034\bigr) $$

$$ = \frac{1}{10} \times 0.235 = 0.0235 \text{ mm} $$

### Step 2 — Compute Squared Deviations from the Mean

| $r_{j}$ (mm) | $(r_j - \bar{l}_p)$ | $(r_j - \bar{l}_p)^2$ |
|--------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| 0.012 | −0.0115 | 1.3225 × 10⁻⁴ |
| 0.015 | −0.0085 | 7.2250 × 10⁻⁵ |
| 0.018 | −0.0055 | 3.0250 × 10⁻⁵ |
| 0.020 | −0.0035 | 1.2250 × 10⁻⁵ |
| 0.022 | −0.0015 | 2.2500 × 10⁻⁶ |
| 0.025 | +0.0015 | 2.2500 × 10⁻⁶ |
| 0.028 | +0.0045 | 2.0250 × 10⁻⁵ |
| 0.030 | +0.0065 | 4.2250 × 10⁻⁵ |
| 0.031 | +0.0075 | 5.6250 × 10⁻⁵ |
| 0.034 | +0.0105 | 1.1025 × 10⁻⁴ |

### Step 3 — Sum the Squared Deviations

$$ \sum (r_j - \bar{l}_p)^2 = (1.3225 + 0.7225 + 0.3025 + 0.1225 + 0.0225 + 0.0225 + 0.2025 + 0.4225 + 0.5625 + 1.1025) \times 10^{-4} $$

$$ = 4.8050 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm}^2 $$

### Step 4 — Apply Bessel's Correction

$$ \hat{\sigma}_p = \sqrt{\frac{4.8050 \times 10^{-4}}{10 - 1}} = \sqrt{5.3389 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.307 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} $$

### Step 5 — Compute $RP_p$

$$ RP_p = 0.0235 + 3 \times (7.307 \times 10^{-3}) $$

$$ = 0.0235 + 0.02192 = 0.04542 \text{ mm} \approx 45.4 \ \mu\text{m} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** The robot exhibits a position repeatability of **45.4 µm**, which is **excellent** — comparable to high-precision SCARA robots used in semiconductor clean rooms. ISO 9283 compliance requires $RP_p \leq 100 \ \mu m$ for "high-precision" class, so this robot **passes** the test with a healthy margin.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Pose vs Path Testing

| **Dimension** | **Pose Testing (AP, RP)** | **Path Testing (AT, RT)** |
|---------------|----------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Motion Type** | Point-to-point (discrete) | Continuous trajectory |
| **Sampling Resolution** | Single instant per commanded pose | $N_p \geq 100$ samples per cycle |
| **Primary Measurement** | End-of-motion deviation | Mid-trajectory deviation |
| **Controller Stress** | Low — settling allowed | High — servo tracking active |
| **Failure Modes Revealed** | Drift, backlash, hysteresis | Servo lag, feedforward error, friction |
| **Cycle Count Recommended** | 30 | 10 |
| **Test Duration** | Short (≈ 10 min) | Long (≈ 30–45 min) |
| **ISO 9283 Section** | § 5.1 – 5.4 | § 5.5 – 5.8 |
| **KTU Module Weight** | ★★★★ (high) | ★★★★★ (highest) |
| **Typical Industrial Failure** | Homing sensor degradation | Joint friction buildup |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Test-Robot Program Method

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram captures the *information flow* of a TRPM test session. It depicts how data propagates from the **Commanded Trajectory Generator** through the **Robot Under Test (RUT)**, the **Measurement Subsystem**, and finally into the **Statistical Engine** that yields the certified performance indices.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[step1: Test Pose and Path Definition] --> B[step2: ISO 9283 Test Cube Specification]
    B --> C[step3: Trajectory Generation Module]
    C --> D[step4: Motion Controller Command Stream]
    D --> E[step5: Robot Under Test RUT]
    E --> F[step6: Joint Encoders and Current Sensors]
    E --> G[step7: External Laser Tracker Probe]
    F --> H[step8: Data Acquisition Unit DAQ]
    G --> H
    H --> I[step9: Raw Measurement Buffer]
    I --> J[step10: Synchronization and Time Stamp Module]
    J --> K[step11: Statistical Analysis Engine]
    K --> L[step12: AP Computation]
    K --> M[step13: RP Computation]
    K --> N[step14: AT Computation]
    K --> O[step15: RT Computation]
    K --> P[step16: Velocity and Acceleration Indices]
    L --> Q[step17: Performance Report Generator]
    M --> Q
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[step18: Pass or Fail Decision Module]
    R --> S[step19: ISO 9283 Certificate Issuance]
    R --> T[step20: Reliability Database Update for MTBF]

    classDef inputBox fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef processBox fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputBox fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decisionBox fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,B,C inputBox
    class D,E,F,G,H,I,J,K,L,M,N,O,P,Q processBox
    class S,T outputBox
    class R decisionBox
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Decomposition of Performance Indices

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[stepA: Robot Performance Testing] --> B[stepB: Pose Based Tests]
    A --> C[stepC: Path Based Tests]
    A --> D[stepD: Dynamic Tests]

    B --> B1[stepB1: Pose Accuracy AP]
    B --> B2[stepB2: Pose Repeatability RP]
    B --> B3[stepB3: Multi Directional Accuracy]

    C --> C1[stepC1: Path Accuracy AT]
    C --> C2[stepC2: Path Repeatability RT]
    C --> C3[stepC3: Cornering Deviation]

    D --> D1[stepD1: Velocity Fluctuation v]
    D --> D2[stepD2: Acceleration Fluctuation a]
    D --> D3[stepD3: Settling Time ts]

    B1 --> E[stepE: ISO 9283 Report Output]
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
    C1 --> E
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E

    classDef rootNode fill:#1E40AF,stroke:#1E3A8A,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef branchNode fill:#0EA5E9,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef leafNode fill:#FDE68A,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#86EFAC,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A rootNode
    class B,C,D,B1,B2,B3,C1,C2,C3,D1,D2,D3 branchNode
    class E outputNode
```

## 4.3 Test Cycle State Diagram (Sequential Execution Logic)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stepS1: System Initialisation
    stepS1: Idle State
    stepS2: Homing and Calibration
    stepS3: Payload Mounting
    stepS4: Test Program Upload
    stepS5: Cycle Execution
    stepS6: Data Logging
    stepS7: Cycle Count Check
    stepS8: Statistical Aggregation
    stepS9: Index Computation
    stepS10: ISO 9283 Threshold Comparison
    stepS11: Certification and Archival

    stepS1 --> stepS2
    stepS2 --> stepS3
    stepS3 --> stepS4
    stepS4 --> stepS5
    stepS5 --> stepS6
    stepS6 --> stepS7
    stepS7 --> stepS5: Continue if cycles remaining
    stepS7 --> stepS8: All cycles complete
    stepS8 --> stepS9
    stepS9 --> stepS10
    stepS10 --> stepS11: Pass
    stepS10 --> stepS2: Fail Recalibrate

    stepS11 --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Exam Answers:**
> - **Arrow direction** = data/signal flow.
> - **Decision diamonds (pink)** = points where Pass/Fail is declared.
> - **Process blocks (yellow)** = computationally intensive stages, prime targets for numerical questions.
> - **Output blocks (green)** = the deliverable artefacts of the entire TRPM procedure.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Q:** Define the **Test-Robot Program Method** as specified by ISO 9283. List any **four** performance indices that are evaluated using this method.

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** The Test-Robot Program Method is a **standardised empirical procedure** prescribed by **ISO 9283** for evaluating the performance of manipulating industrial robots. In this method, a robot executes a **predefined, deterministic motion program** through selected test poses and paths within its working envelope, and the deviations between **commanded** and **attained** motions are measured and statistically analysed to compute certified performance indices.
>
> **Four Performance Indices (0.5 × 4 = 2 Marks):**
>
> | # | Index | Full Form |
> |---|-------|-----------|
> | 1 | $AP$ | Pose Accuracy |
> | 2 | $RP$ | Pose Repeatability |
> | 3 | $AT$ | Path Accuracy |
> | 4 | $RT$ | Path Repeatability |
>
> *(Velocity fluctuation, acceleration fluctuation, and settling time are also valid alternatives.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
> **Q:** Distinguish between **Pose Accuracy (AP)** and **Pose Repeatability (RP)**. Why is repeatability generally a more reliable indicator of long-term robotic performance than accuracy?

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Distinction (2 Marks):**
> - **Pose Accuracy (AP):** Measures the **closeness of the mean attained pose** over *N* cycles to the **commanded pose**. It is a measure of *systematic* error (bias). Mathematically:
>
> $$ AP = \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_{cmd})^2 + (\bar{y} - y_{cmd})^2 + (\bar{z} - z_{cmd})^2} $$
>
> - **Pose Repeatability (RP):** Measures the **closeness of attained poses to one another** when repeatedly commanded the *same* pose. It is a measure of *random* error (statistical scatter). Mathematically:
>
> $$ RP = \bar{l} + 3\hat{\sigma} $$
>
> **Why RP is more reliable (1 Mark):** Repeatability reflects the *intrinsic stochastic behaviour* of the mechatronic system (bearing friction, encoder resolution, servo jitter), which is **stable over time**. Accuracy, in contrast, drifts due to **payload-induced deflection, thermal expansion, and calibration ageing**. Therefore, RP is the **preferred specification metric** for long-term operational reliability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Mark-Loss Pitfall:**
> - ❌ Do **not** confuse $AP$ (deviation of mean) with $RP$ (scatter of points). Examiners frequently deduct **2–3 marks** for this.
> - ❌ Do **not** forget **Bessel's correction** (N − 1) in the standard deviation denominator. Direct N in denominator is a **−1 mark** penalty.
> - ❌ Do **not** quote $RP = 3\sigma$ alone. Always write the **full form** $RP = \bar{l} + 3\hat{\sigma}$.
> - ❌ Do **not** omit the **unit** (mm or µm). Examiner guidelines explicitly state *"no unit ⇒ −0.5 marks"*.
> - ✅ Always cite **ISO 9283** when defining any performance index.

---

### **Question B1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q:**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **six procedural stages** of the Test-Robot Program Method as prescribed by ISO 9283. Highlight the role of the **test cube** and the **3σ statistical envelope** in the procedure. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** An industrial robot is tested for pose repeatability at a single commanded pose. Over **N = 25 cycles**, the **standard deviation** of the radial deviation is measured as $\hat{\sigma}_p = 0.012$ mm, and the **mean radial distance** of attained points from the centroid is $\bar{l}_p = 0.035$ mm.
> Compute the **Position Repeatability Index $RP_p$**. State whether the robot qualifies as **"high-precision"** under ISO 9283 if the pass threshold is $RP_p \leq 0.100$ mm. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

> **Six Stages of TRPM (1 Mark each, total 6):**
>
> 1. **Test Pose & Path Definition:** A *test cube* of dimensions proportional to the robot's rated reach is defined. The cube must contain at least **five representative poses** covering centre, mid-reach, extreme reach, near-singularity, and gravity-loaded regions. *(+ 0.5 Marks for explaining the test cube)*
>
> 2. **Load & Environmental Calibration:** Mount a calibrated probe (e.g., laser tracker, **Renishaw OMM**) on the end-effector. Lock temperature at **20 °C ± 2 °C**, payload at rated mass ± **1 %**, and supply voltage at ± **5 %**.
>
> 3. **Programming the Test Motion:** Write a deterministic robot program commanding a defined sequence of point-to-point motions (for AP/RP) and continuous trajectories (for AT/RT).
>
> 4. **Measurement Execution:** Execute the program for the prescribed number of cycles (**N = 30** for pose tests, **N = 10** for path tests). Capture attained pose data for every cycle.
>
> 5. **Statistical Analysis:** Compute mean attained pose, radial deviations, and standard deviations.
>
> 6. **Index Reporting & Certification:** Compute $AP$, $RP$, $AT$, $RT$ and compare with ISO 9283 / OEM thresholds. Issue the performance certificate.
>
> **Role of Test Cube:** Provides a **spatially distributed, statistically representative sample** of the workspace. *(0.5 Marks)*
>
> **Role of 3σ Envelope:** The 3σ envelope corresponds to **99.73 % confidence** under a Gaussian assumption. It quantifies the radius within which nearly all future attained poses will fall — making it the operational definition of "industrial-grade repeatability." *(0.5 Marks)*

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $N = 25$ cycles
- $\hat{\sigma}_p = 0.012$ mm
- $\bar{l}_p = 0.035$ mm
- ISO 9283 threshold for high precision: $RP_p \leq 0.100$ mm

**Formula (1 Mark):**

$$ RP_p = \bar{l}_p + 3\hat{\sigma}_p $$

**Substitution (2 Marks):**

$$ RP_p = 0.035 + 3 \times (0.012) $$

**Computation (2 Marks):**

$$ RP_p = 0.035 + 0.036 = 0.071 \text{ mm} $$

**Result Comparison and Conclusion (2 Marks):**

$$ RP_p = 0.071 \text{ mm} < 0.100 \text{ mm (threshold)} $$

Since the computed position repeatability is **less than** the ISO 9283 high-precision threshold, the robot **qualifies as a high-precision manipulator** under ISO 9283.

> **Bonus (0 Marks but appreciated):** Equivalent in micrometres: $RP_p = 71 \ \mu\text{m}$, which falls in the **"precision class"** band of $50 < RP_p \leq 100 \ \mu\text{m}$ — typical of **SCARA and 6-DOF assembly robots**.

---

### **Question B2 (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

> **Q:**
> **(a)** Compare **Pose-Based Tests (AP, RP)** and **Path-Based Tests (AT, RT)** under the Test-Robot Program Method. Explain which test is more sensitive to **servo tracking error** and **joint friction**, and why. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A robot is commanded to follow a **straight-line path of length 1000 mm** at a constant velocity. The commanded path is sampled at **N_p = 200** points. The sum of squared deviations of attained points from the commanded path is computed as $\sum d_k^2 = 0.45 \text{ mm}^2$. Compute the **Path Accuracy Index AT** and interpret the result given the pass threshold is $AT \leq 1.5$ mm. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

> **Comparison Table (4 Marks — 1 Mark per meaningful row):**
>
> | Dimension | Pose Tests (AP, RP) | Path Tests (AT, RT) |
> |-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
> | Motion type | Point-to-point | Continuous trajectory |
> | What is measured | Final attained pose | Mid-trajectory deviation |
> | Sensitivity to servo lag | Low (settling time permitted) | **High (no settling allowed)** |
> | Sensitivity to joint friction | Low (single direction) | **High (continuous motion amplifies stiction)** |
>
> **Sensitivity Explanation (3 Marks):**
> - **Servo Tracking Error:** During continuous-path motion, the controller must continuously minimise the *following error* $e(t) = x_{cmd}(t) - x_{attained}(t)$. Frictional non-linearities (Coulomb, viscous, stiction) and gain limitations cause **periodic deviation** that is directly captured by $AT$ and $RT$. In point-to-point motion, the controller has time to **settle**, masking these errors.
> - **Joint Friction:** Friction produces **direction-dependent resistance**. Path testing along a curved or zig-zag trajectory exposes friction-induced path deviations at every infinitesimal step, whereas pose testing only captures the *endpoint* deviation which may appear negligible even if friction was severe mid-trajectory.
> - **Conclusion:** Path-based tests are **diagnostically richer** and are the gold standard for **dynamic performance characterisation**.

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- Number of path samples: $N_p = 200$
- Sum of squared deviations: $\sum d_k^2 = 0.45 \text{ mm}^2$
- Pass threshold: $AT \leq 1.5$ mm

**Formula (1 Mark):**

$$ AT = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N_p} \sum_{k=1}^{N_p} d_k^2} $$

> This is the **root-mean-square (RMS)** deviation of the attained path from the commanded path — a standard formulation under ISO 9283 § 5.5.

**Substitution (2 Marks):**

$$ AT = \sqrt{\frac{0.45}{200}} $$

**Computation (2 Marks):**

$$ AT = \sqrt{0.00225} = 0.0474 \text{ mm} $$

**Result Interpretation and Conclusion (2 Marks):**

$$ AT = 0.0474 \text{ mm} \approx 47.4 \ \mu\text{m} \ll 1.5 \text{ mm (threshold)} $$

The path accuracy is **nearly 32 times better** than the ISO 9283 pass threshold. This robot demonstrates **exceptional path-tracking fidelity**, well within the high-precision classification. Such performance is characteristic of **direct-drive or harmonic-drive robots** used in laser cutting and waterjet trimming applications.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Read This Twice Before Exam**

- ✅ **TRPM = ISO 9283's executable testing framework.** Memorise the standard name verbatim.
- ✅ The **test cube** must contain **≥ 5 distinct poses** spanning the workspace.
- ✅ **Standard test conditions:** **20 °C**, **N = 30 cycles** (pose), **N = 10 cycles** (path), rated payload ± **1 %**.
- ✅ **$AP$** measures **systematic bias** (deviation of *mean* from commanded).
- ✅ **$RP$** measures **random scatter** (variability of attained points around their own centroid).
- ✅ Always write $RP_p = \bar{l}_p + 3\hat{\sigma}_p$ — never forget $\bar{l}_p$, the **mean radial distance**.
- ✅ Always apply **Bessel's correction** (N − 1) in $\hat{\sigma}_p$.
- ✅ The factor **3** in $3\hat{\sigma}$ corresponds to **99.73 %** Gaussian confidence.
- ✅ **$AT$** is computed as the **RMS** deviation along a continuous path: $\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{N_p}\sum d_k^2}$.
- ✅ **$RT$** is the **maximum envelope width** of the attained path cluster (not RMS).
- ✅ **Path tests** are more diagnostic than pose tests for servo lag and friction.
- ✅ Repeatability ($RP$) is **preferred over** accuracy ($AP$) for **long-term reliability** assessment.
- ✅ The **i.i.d. Gaussian assumption** underlies all TRPM statistics; if violated, use **non-parametric bootstrap**.
- ✅ TRPM feeds the **MTBF** database and **predictive maintenance** schedules.
