# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Q1.a]**

**Q: Define Robot Reliability Testing. List any four types of reliability tests commonly performed on robotic systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Robot Reliability Testing is the systematic process of subjecting robotic systems and components to controlled operational, environmental, and stress conditions over a defined duration to quantify, verify, and demonstrate their ability to perform intended functions without failure under specified conditions.

**Four types of reliability tests:**
1. **Reliability Demonstration Testing (RDT)** — statistically proves that the robot meets a contractual MTBF claim.
2. **Accelerated Life Testing (ALT)** — compresses time-to-failure using elevated stress (temperature, vibration, load).
3. **Highly Accelerated Life Testing (HALT)** — step-stress method to find design margins and weaknesses.
4. **Environmental Stress Screening (ESS)** — detects manufacturing defects via thermal cycling + vibration.
5. *Bonus:* **Endurance / Life-Cycle Testing** — runs robot through mission profile to failure.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q2.c]**

**Q: Differentiate between MTBF and MTTF with one example each from robotic systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) | MTTF (Mean Time To Failure) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Average operating time between **consecutive failures** of a **repairable** system. | Average time to the **first (and only)** failure of a **non-repairable** component. |
| **Mathematical** | MTBF $= \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | MTTF $= \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ (for Exponential) |
| **System Type** | Whole robot arm, controller, power supply | Bearing, capacitor, encoder, PCB |
| **Example (Robot)** | A 6-axis industrial welding robot in an automotive plant — repairable, so we track MTBF. | A harmonic-drive gear inside the joint — once it fails, it is replaced, so we track MTTF. |
| **Typical KTU Units** | Hours | Hours |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and must contain **internal choice** — students answer EITHER (a) OR (b), each worth 7 marks.

---

### QUESTION A — Choice 1 (14 Marks Total)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Q9, Adapted]**

#### **Q.A (a):** *(7 Marks)*

A 6-axis industrial robot arm is to undergo a **Reliability Demonstration Test** to confirm an MTBF claim of **10,000 hours at 90% confidence**. The test plan requires **no failures** during the test. Determine:
1. The minimum total accumulated test time required.
2. The equivalent test duration if **15 robots** are tested simultaneously.
3. If the customer tolerates **at most 1 failure**, recompute the required total test time.

**Model Solution:**

**(1) For $r = 0$ failures at $C = 90\%$ confidence ($\alpha = 0.10$):**

The lower confidence bound formula:

$$\text{MTBF}_{L} = \frac{2T}{\chi^{2}_{\alpha,\,2r+2}}$$

For $r = 0$: degrees of freedom $\nu = 2(0) + 2 = 2$. Critical value $\chi^{2}_{0.10,\,2} = 4.605$.

Set $\text{MTBF}_{L} = \text{MTBF}_{\text{claim}} = 10000\,\text{h}$:

$$\frac{2T}{4.605} = 10000$$

$$T = \frac{10000 \times 4.605}{2} = 23025\,\text{robot-hours}$$

**(2) For $N = 15$ robots tested simultaneously:**

$$t_{d} = \frac{T}{N} = \frac{23025}{15} = 1535\,\text{h per robot}$$

**(3) For $r = 1$ failure at 90% confidence:**

$\nu = 2(1) + 2 = 4$. Critical value $\chi^{2}_{0.10,\,4} = 7.779$.

$$T = \frac{10000 \times 7.779}{2} = 38895\,\text{robot-hours}$$

$$t_{d} = \frac{38895}{15} = 2593\,\text{h per robot}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Frequent Mistakes:**
> 1. **Wrong chi-squared d.f.**: For $r$ failures, use $\nu = 2r + 2$, **NOT** $2r$ or $r$. Mixing up the formula costs full marks.
> 2. **Unit confusion**: Forgetting to convert total robot-hours into per-unit hours when dividing by sample size.
> 3. **Confidence level inversion**: $90\%$ confidence means $\alpha = 0.10$, **not** $\alpha = 0.90$.

---

#### **Q.A (b):** *(7 Marks)*

**Explain the Weibull distribution and its significance in robot reliability testing. Discuss how the shape parameter $\beta$ influences the failure behavior, and compute the MTTF for a robot joint bearing with $\beta = 2.5$ and characteristic life $\eta = 30{,}000\,\text{h}$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Weibull Distribution Overview:** The Weibull distribution is the most versatile distribution used in reliability testing because it can model all three regions of the bathtub curve through a single shape parameter.

**Reliability Function:**

$$R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$$

**Influence of $\beta$:**

| $\beta$ Range | Failure Behavior | Robot Example |
|---|---|---|
| $\beta < 1$ | Decreasing hazard (Infant Mortality) | Newly manufactured PCB solder defects |
| $\beta = 1$ | Constant hazard (Exponential) | Random electronic noise failures |
| $\beta > 1$ | Increasing hazard (Wear-out) | Mechanical bearing fatigue |

**MTTF Computation:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)$$

With $\beta = 2.5$, $\eta = 30{,}000\,\text{h}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = 30000 \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/2.5) = 30000 \cdot \Gamma(1.4)$$

From gamma function tables: $\Gamma(1.4) \approx 0.8873$.

$$\text{MTTF} = 30000 \times 0.8873 \approx 26{,}620\,\text{h}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Weibull MTTF:**
> 1. **DO NOT** use $\text{MTTF} = \eta$ unless $\beta = 1$. This is the most common error.
> 2. **Always** verify $\Gamma$ values from a table — KTU examiners permit either gamma function table lookup OR recognition of common values like $\Gamma(1.5) = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$.

---

### QUESTION B — Choice 2 (14 Marks Total)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Q10, Adapted]**

#### **Q.B (a):** *(7 Marks)*

A collaborative robot (cobot) manufacturer wants to demonstrate a $B_{10}$ life of **50,000 hours** for its joint bearing using **Weibull analysis**. Test data from accelerated testing yields shape parameter $\beta = 2.0$ and characteristic life $\eta = 120{,}000\,\text{h}$. Determine:
1. The $B_{10}$ life from the Weibull parameters.
2. Comment on whether the cobot meets the $B_{10} = 50{,}000\,\text{h}$ target.
3. The hazard rate at $t = 50{,}000\,\text{h}$.

**Model Solution:**

**(1) $B_{10}$ life formula:**

$$B_{10} = \eta \left[-\ln(1 - 0.10)\right]^{1/\beta}$$

$$B_{10} = 120000 \times \left[-\ln(0.90)\right]^{1/2}$$

$$B_{10} = 120000 \times \left[0.10536\right]^{0.5}$$

$$B_{10} = 120000 \times 0.3246$$

$$B_{10} \approx 38{,}956\,\text{h}$$

**(2) Comparison with target:**

The computed $B_{10} = 38{,}956\,\text{h}$ is **less than** the target $B_{10} = 50{,}000\,\text{h}$.

$$\text{Deficit} = \frac{38956}{50000} \approx 78\%$$

The cobot **does NOT meet** the target. Design improvements are needed — either upgrade bearing material, improve lubrication, or reduce operating stress.

**(3) Hazard rate at $t = 50{,}000\,\text{h}$:**

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$$

$$\lambda(50000) = \frac{2.0}{120000}\left(\frac{50000}{120000}\right)^{2.0 - 1}$$

$$\lambda(50000) = \frac{2.0}{120000} \times (0.4167)^{1}$$

$$\lambda(50000) = 1.667 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.4167 \approx 6.94 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{failures/h}$$

This corresponds to a mean rate of about **1 failure every 144,000 hours** at $t = 50{,}000\,\text{h}$, indicating wear-out is beginning.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. The $B_{10}$ formula **must** be derived, not just quoted — show $\ln(1 - 0.10) = -0.10536$.
> 2. Many students confuse $B_{10}$ with MTTF. $B_{10}$ is the **10% failure point**; MTTF is the **mean** (≈$50\%$ failure point for symmetric distributions, but not for Weibull).
> 3. For $\beta > 1$, hazard rate $\lambda(t)$ **increases with $t$** — do not treat as constant.

---

#### **Q.B (b):** *(7 Marks)*

**Explain Accelerated Life Testing (ALT) using the Arrhenius model. A robot motor's insulation is to be tested. The use-condition temperature is $T_{\text{use}} = 333\,\text{K}$ ($60\,°C$), the stress temperature is $T_{\text{stress}} = 423\,\text{K}$ ($150\,°C$), and the activation energy is $E_a = 0.85\,\text{eV}$. Compute the Acceleration Factor (AF) and determine the equivalent field life if the ALT test is run for $500\,\text{h}$.** ($k_B = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{eV/K}$)

**Model Solution:**

**Arrhenius ALT Theory:** ALT compresses years of real-world use into days of lab time by operating the system at elevated stress (typically temperature). The Arrhenius equation models how chemical / physical degradation rates increase with temperature:

$$k = A\,e^{-E_a/(k_BT)}$$

Failure time is inversely proportional to degradation rate, so:

$$\text{AF} = \frac{t_{\text{use}}}{t_{\text{stress}}} = e^{\frac{E_a}{k_B}\left(\frac{1}{T_{\text{use}}} - \frac{1}{T_{\text{stress}}}\right)}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

$$\text{AF} = \exp\!\left[\frac{0.85}{8.617\times10^{-5}}\left(\frac{1}{333} - \frac{1}{423}\right)\right]$$

Compute the temperature term:

$$\frac{1}{333} - \frac{1}{423} = 0.003003 - 0.002364 = 0.000639\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

Compute the exponent:

$$\frac{0.85}{8.617\times10^{-5}} \times 0.000639 = 9864.5 \times 0.000639 \approx 6.302$$

Apply the exponential:

$$\text{AF} = e^{6.302} \approx 545.7$$

**Equivalent Field Life:**

$$t_{\text{use}} = \text{AF} \times t_{\text{stress}} = 545.7 \times 500 = 272{,}850\,\text{h}$$

Converting to years ($24/7$ operation, $8760\,\text{h/year}$):

$$\frac{272850}{8760} \approx 31.1\,\text{years}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 500-hour ALT test at $150\,°C$ simulates approximately **31 years** of continuous operation at $60\,°C$. This is the foundation of how manufacturers justify 10–20 year warranties from short-duration laboratory tests.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Temperature MUST be in Kelvin**, not Celsius. Common error: $T = 60$ instead of $333$.
> 2. **Exponent sign**: ensure $1/T_{\text{use}} > 1/T_{\text{stress}}$, so the difference is positive.
> 3. **Do not** confuse $E_a$ (activation energy, eV) with the Boltzmann constant's subscript — students sometimes write $E_a/k$ instead of $E_a/k_B$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4: Robot Reliability Testing**

### Core Concepts
- **Reliability Testing** is the empirical verification of robot reliability claims through controlled stress application and statistical analysis.
- **Three Pillars**: Test Planning → Test Execution → Test Analysis.
- Standards involved: **IEC 61508**, **ISO 13849-1**, **ISO 10218-1/2**, **IEC 62061**, **MIL-HDBK-781**.

### Test Type Recall
- **RDT** (Reliability Demonstration Testing) — contractual MTBF proof.
- **ALT** (Accelerated Life Testing) — compressed time-to-failure via elevated stress.
- **HALT** — step-stress to destruction to find design margins.
- **ESS** — defect screening during production.
- **HASS** — production screening derived from HALT profile.
- **Burn-in** — eliminates infant mortality.

### Distribution Mastery
- **Exponential**: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$; MTTF $= 1/\lambda$; useful-life only.
- **Weibull**: $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$; $\beta < 1$ infant, $\beta = 1$ exponential, $\beta > 1$ wear-out; MTTF $= \eta \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$.
- **Lognormal**: for multiplicative degradation (batteries, fatigue crack).
- **Normal**: for symmetric tolerance stack-up.

### Key Metric Differentiations
- **MTBF** (repairable) vs **MTTF** (non-repairable).
- **Availability** $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$.
- **$B_x$ life** = time when $x\%$ of population has failed.

### Quantitative Skills Required for KTU ESE
- Confidence interval using **chi-squared distribution**.
- Sample-size and test-duration calculations.
- Arrhenius acceleration factor.
- Weibull parameter estimation and $B_x$ computation.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
1. Mixing **MTBF** with **MTTF**.
2. Using **MTBF** $= \eta$ for non-Exponential Weibull.
3. Forgetting to multiply test hours by sample size $N$.
4. Wrong chi-squared d.f.: use $\nu = 2r + 2$.
5. Temperatures in Celsius instead of Kelvin for Arrhenius.
6. Treating constant $\lambda$ as a universal assumption — it is **only** valid for useful-life (Exponential) regime.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When asked "why do we test reliability when we can compute it?" the correct answer is: **Prediction models assume failure rates; only testing provides empirical confirmation under real stress combinations, uncovers unknown failure modes, and generates the data needed for statistical confidence statements**. This conceptual question has appeared in KTU ESE every year since 2022.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Testing and Start-Up Safety-Related Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT831 - SAFETY AND RELIABILITY IN ROBOTIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Robot Testing and Start-Up Safety-Related Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Robot Testing and Start-Up Safety-Related Factors

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Robot Testing** is a systematic, multi-stage verification process designed to validate that a robotic system conforms to its design specifications, performance criteria, and applicable safety standards before being placed into service, and at prescribed intervals throughout its operational life. It is broadly classified into **Type Testing (factory-level)**, **Site Acceptance Testing (SAT)**, **Commissioning Tests**, and **Periodic / Recurrent Tests**.

**Start-Up Safety-Related Factors** are the engineered, procedural, and administrative controls applied during the transition of a robotic system from a de-energized, standby, or newly installed state to a fully operational state. These factors ensure that the robot does not pose an unacceptable risk to personnel, equipment, or the environment during the critical first-run phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **ISO 10218-1:2011** and **ISO 10218-2:2011**, start-up procedures are mandatory validation steps that confirm the integrity of every safety-rated function (e.g., emergency stop, speed limiting, protective stops) **before** any production cycle begins.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> - **Commissioning** = Bringing a brand-new or relocated robot into service (one-time).
> - **Start-Up** = Energizing and preparing an existing robot for a shift or new task (routine).
> - Both share the same logic, but commissioning is exhaustive while start-up is confirmatory.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of robot testing and start-up safety like the **pre-flight checklist used by commercial pilots**. A pilot never simply turns the key and takes off — they walk around the aircraft, check every gauge, verify fuel, test controls, confirm instrumentation, and only then receive clearance. The aircraft is also certified at the factory (type testing), accepted at the destination airport (site acceptance), and continuously monitored (periodic tests).

A robot is the same. Before a single motion command is executed in production, the engineer must:
- Verify the mechanical integrity (no loose bolts, cable wear, oil leaks).
- Validate the electrical safety (earthing, insulation, E-stops).
- Confirm software limits (software-stops, workspace limits).
- Test safety-rated functions (muting, interlocks, light curtains).
- Document everything in a **commissioning report**.

Skipping any of these steps is equivalent to a pilot taking off without checking the altimeter — a disaster waiting to happen.

> [!TIP]
> A useful mental model: **Type Test** = "Does the design work?" | **Acceptance Test** = "Did I get what I paid for?" | **Commissioning Test** = "Is it ready for my floor?" | **Periodic Test** = "Is it still safe to run?"

### 1.3 Governing International Standards (Mandatory Reference Set)

| Standard | Scope |
|---|---|
| **ISO 10218-1:2011** | Safety requirements for the industrial robot itself |
| **ISO 10218-2:2011** | Safety requirements for robot systems and integration |
| **ISO/TS 15066:2016** | Collaborative robot safety guidelines |
| **ISO 9283:1998** | Performance criteria and test methods for manipulators |
| **ANSI/RIA R15.06-2012** | North American adoption of ISO 10218 |
| **ISO 13849-1:2015** | Safety-related parts of control systems (PL rating) |
| **IEC 62061:2021** | Functional safety of E/E/PE control systems (SIL) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The benchmark safety metric used by these standards is **PL (Performance Level)** for ISO 13849 and **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** for IEC 62061, both ranging from **a/1** (lowest) to **e/3** (highest). Robot safety functions are typically designed for **PL d** or **PL e**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Phase Robot Testing Hierarchy

A KTU examiner expects students to enumerate and describe these phases in order:

### Phase 1 — Type Testing (Design Validation at OEM)
Performed by the manufacturer in a controlled lab. Validates that the robot meets its published specifications under all rated environmental conditions.
- **Pose accuracy (AP)** and **pose repeatability (RP)** per **ISO 9283**.
- **Path accuracy (AT)** and **path repeatability (RT)**.
- **Static compliance** and **overshoot**.
- **Environmental tests:** thermal, vibration, EMC, IP rating.
- **Endurance / life testing** (continuous duty cycle).

### Phase 2 — Site Acceptance Test (SAT)
Conducted at the end-user facility **before** integration into the production line. Verifies that the robot was not damaged in transit and that the as-delivered unit matches the purchase specification.
- Visual inspection for transit damage.
- Power-up verification (no fault codes on HMI).
- Free-space motion across the full workspace envelope.
- Functional verification of every I/O port.
- Payload test at rated load (typically 100% rated + 110% overload).

### Phase 3 — Commissioning Test
The most exhaustive on-site test, performed **once** after installation and integration. This is where safety-related factors are dominant.
- Risk assessment review (per **ISO 12100**).
- Verification of all safeguarding devices (light curtains, safety mats, interlocks).
- Emergency stop response time measurement.
- Safety-rated soft-axes and software limits verification.
- Stop categories **STOP 0** (immediate uncontrolled) and **STOP 1** (controlled with power removal).
- Reduced speed verification (typically ≤ **250 mm/s** for collaborative modes).
- Documentation in the **Commissioning Report**.

### Phase 4 — Periodic / Recurrent Testing
Performed at defined intervals (often annually or after 2,000 operating hours, whichever is earlier). Confirms that the safety integrity has not degraded.
- Brake wear check.
- Encoder / resolver calibration drift check.
- Insulation resistance (megger) test.
- Earth continuity test.
- Emergency stop circuit continuity test.
- Light curtain / scanner response time re-test.

### Phase 5 — Acceptance Test after Modification or Relocation
Whenever the robot's safety configuration, end-effector, payload, or workspace changes, a partial re-commissioning is mandatory.

## 2.2 Start-Up Safety-Related Factors (The "Pre-Flight" Checklist)

### 2.2.1 Hardware Factors
| Factor | Description |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical integrity** | Bolts torqued, no backlash in gear trains, oil levels, cable chain tension |
| **Electrical isolation** | Main switch lockable in OFF position (LOTO — Lock-Out Tag-Out) |
| **Earthing / Grounding** | Resistance ≤ **0.1 Ω** (per IEC 60204-1) |
| **Emergency stop devices** | At least one readily accessible from every operator station, color **RED on YELLOW** |
| **Enabling device (hold-to-run)** | 3-position, must be released under spring force |
| **Mode selector** | Manual / Automatic / Servo-on modes clearly labelled |

### 2.2.2 Software & Control Factors
| Factor | Description |
|---|---|
| **Workspace limits** | Mechanical end-stops + software limits (redundant) |
| **Speed limits** | Auto-mode full speed; Teach-mode typically ≤ **250 mm/s** |
| **Safety-rated soft axis limits** | Configured per **ISO 13849-1 PL d** |
| **Safe I/O mapping** | Cross-checked against wiring diagram |
| **Fault log review** | No unresolved errors from previous shift |
| **Backup of parameters** | Verified before start-up after maintenance |

### 2.2.3 Procedural & Administrative Factors
| Factor | Description |
|---|---|
| **Operator training records** | Valid certificate per **ISO 10218-2 clause 8.3** |
| **Risk assessment up-to-date** | Re-validated for any process change |
| **PPE availability** | Per the risk assessment |
| **Restricted-access zones** | Marked on floor in **yellow hatching** |
| **Reset / Restart procedure** | Documented and followed |
| **Communication protocol** | Two-way radio, light beacon, or intercom |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | LaTeX | Units | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Safety distance** (perpendicular approach) | $S = K \cdot (T_m + T_s) + C$ | mm | Minimum separation between operator and hazard |
| 2 | **Stopping time** of the system | $T_s = T_r + T_s$ | ms | Total time from stop signal to motion cessation |
| 3 | **Stopping time of the robot** (single axis) | $T_r = \dfrac{J \cdot \omega_s}{T_{brake}}$ | ms | Braking torque applied |
| 4 | **Intrusion distance** (constant C) | $C \geq 0$ (typically 850 mm) | mm | Operator reach into hazard zone |
| 5 | **Approach speed K** | $K = 1600 \text{ mm/s}$ (walk), $2000 \text{ mm/s}$ (reach) | mm/s | Per ISO 13855 |
| 6 | **Pose accuracy (AP)** | $AP = \sqrt{(\bar{x}-x_c)^2 + (\bar{y}-y_c)^2 + (\bar{z}-z_c)^2}$ | mm | ISO 9283 metric |
| 7 | **Pose repeatability (RP)** | $RP = \bar{l} + 3s_l$ | mm | ISO 9283 metric |
| 8 | **Mean reliability (R)** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | Probability of survival to time t |
| 9 | **MTBF** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | hours | Mean operating time between failures |
| 10 | **Probability of dangerous failure per hour (PFHd)** | $\text{PFH}_d \leq 10^{-7} / h$ for PL e | 1/h | Per ISO 13849-1 |
| 11 | **Risk reduction factor (RRF)** | $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{P_{before}}{P_{after}}$ | dimensionless | Effectiveness of safeguard |
| 12 | **Brake deceleration check** | $a = \dfrac{v^2}{2 d_{stop}}$ | m/s² | Verifies stop distance |

> [!NOTE]
> **$K$, $T$, and $C$ are the KTU examiner's favourite three variables** for safety distance problems. Memorize: $S = K(T_m + T_s) + C$, where $K$ = approach speed, $T_m$ = machine stopping time, $T_s$ = safeguard response time, $C$ = intrusion distance.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive assembly lines** use start-up safety protocols to prevent crushing injuries during shift change-over (most common robot injury scenario).
- **Pharmaceutical pick-and-place** cells rely on ISO 9283 repeatability tests to meet FDA serialization traceability (sub-millimetre accuracy).
- **Collaborative robots** in SME workshops require ISO/TS 15066 power-and-force limiting (PFL) validation before any human approach is permitted.
- **Nuclear / hazardous-environment robots** use redundant SIL 3 control to guarantee single-fault tolerance during start-up.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Safety Distance $S = K(T_m + T_s) + C$

This is the **single most important formula** in KTU Module 4. We derive it from first principles using kinematics and ISO 13855.

### Step 1 — Define the hazard scenario
An operator is standing outside a safeguarded zone. The robot's hazard zone is the volume swept by the end-effector. The operator's approach begins at a distance $S$ from the boundary of this zone.

### Step 2 — Identify the two independent approach motions
1. The **operator** moves toward the hazard at approach speed $K$ (mm/s).
2. The **robot**, once commanded to stop, requires a total time $T_s$ (s) to cease motion.

### Step 3 — The "no-touch" condition
The operator must not be able to reach the hazard **before** the robot has finished stopping. The earliest point at which the operator can touch the hazard is when the operator has walked the full distance $S$ at speed $K$:

$$
t_{operator} = \dfrac{S}{K}
$$

### Step 4 — Time available for the robot to stop
The total response time of the safety system is the **machine stopping time** $T_m$ plus the **safeguard response time** $T_s$ (e.g., light curtain's reaction time). For the operator to be safe:

$$
\dfrac{S}{K} \;\geq\; T_m + T_s
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for S and add the intrusion constant

$$
S \;\geq\; K \cdot (T_m + T_s)
$$

But an operator can also *reach* into the hazard from the boundary by a distance $C$ (the **intrusion distance**, typically 850 mm for a standing adult with arms extended). To guarantee safety, $C$ must be added to the right-hand side:

$$
S \;=\; K \cdot (T_m + T_s) + C
$$

### Step 6 — Sanity check with numbers
Assume: $K = 1600$ mm/s, $T_m = 250$ ms $= 0.25$ s, $T_s = 20$ ms $= 0.02$ s, $C = 850$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 1600 \cdot (0.25 + 0.02) + 850 \\
&= 1600 \cdot 0.27 + 850 \\
&= 432 + 850 \\
&= 1282 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the light curtain must be mounted at least **1.282 m** from the hazard boundary.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stopping Time $T_r$ of a Robot Axis

For a single robot axis of moment of inertia $J$ rotating at $\omega_s$ rad/s, when the brake applies torque $T_{brake}$:

### Step 1 — Newton's second law for rotation

$$
J \cdot \alpha \;=\; -T_{brake}
$$

### Step 2 — Constant angular deceleration

$$
\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta \omega}{\Delta t} \;=\; -\dfrac{\omega_s}{T_r}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and solve for $T_r$

$$
\begin{aligned}
J \cdot \left(-\dfrac{\omega_s}{T_r}\right) &= -T_{brake} \\
\dfrac{J \cdot \omega_s}{T_r} &= T_{brake} \\
T_r &= \dfrac{J \cdot \omega_s}{T_{brake}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical example
A 6-DOF robot, axis 3 (shoulder): $J = 0.5$ kg·m², $\omega_s = 2$ rad/s, $T_{brake} = 50$ N·m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_r &= \dfrac{0.5 \cdot 2}{50} \\
&= \dfrac{1.0}{50} \\
&= 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

This $T_r$ is then added to other axes' worst-case stop times to compute the **system stopping time $T_s$** used in the safety-distance formula.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reliability / MTBF for Safety Function Verification

During periodic testing, engineers often verify that a robot's safety function still meets its target **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**.

### Step 1 — Exponential reliability model
For non-repairable components (or safety functions in their useful life period), reliability is:

$$
R(t) \;=\; e^{-\lambda t}
$$

where $\lambda$ is the **failure rate** (failures per hour).

### Step 2 — Mean Time Between Failures
The mean of the exponential distribution gives:

$$
\text{MTBF} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\lambda}
$$

### Step 3 — Probability of failure on demand
For a safety function with periodic test interval $T_i$:

$$
P_{FD} \;\approx\; 1 - R(T_i) \;=\; 1 - e^{-\lambda T_i}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical example
$\lambda = 10^{-6}$ /h, test interval $T_i = 8760$ h (1 year).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{FD} &\approx 1 - e^{-10^{-6} \cdot 8760} \\
&\approx 1 - e^{-0.00876} \\
&\approx 1 - 0.99127 \\
&\approx 0.00873
\end{aligned}
$$

This is well within the SIL 2 / PL d envelope of $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-7}$ per demand.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Test, When?

| Situation | Required Test | Standard Reference | Output Document |
|---|---|---|---|
| New robot out of box | SAT | ISO 10218-2 | Acceptance Certificate |
| First-time installation | Commissioning | ISO 10218-2 | Commissioning Report |
| After end-effector change | Partial Commissioning | ISO 10218-2 | Modification Record |
| Annual safety audit | Periodic Test | ISO 13849-1 | Inspection Log |
| Performance drift suspected | ISO 9283 Pose test | ISO 9283 | Calibration Report |
| Collaborative cell new install | Power & Force Limiting Test | ISO/TS 15066 | Risk Assessment Update |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Robot Commissioning & Start-Up Safety Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Robot Arrives at Site] --> A1{Unboxing and Transit Visual Check}
    A1 -- Damage Found --> A2[Issue Non-Conformance Report and Quarantine]
    A1 -- No Damage --> A3[Mechanical Installation on Pedestal]
    A3 --> A4[Electrical Hookup and Earthing Verification]
    A4 --> A5[Insulation Resistance Megger Test]
    A5 -- Pass --> A6[Safeguarding Devices Installed]
    A5 -- Fail --> A7[Rewire and Retest]
    A6 --> A8[Software Upload and I/O Mapping]
    A8 --> A9[Safety Function Verification Loop]
    A9 --> A10[E Stop Test]
    A10 --> A11[Light Curtain Test]
    A11 --> A12[Safety Mat Test]
    A12 --> A13[Interlock Test]
    A13 --> A14[Software Limit Verification]
    A14 --> A15[Reduced Speed Teach Mode Test]
    A15 --> A16[Free Space Full Envelope Sweep]
    A16 --> A17[Payload Test at 100 percent Rated Load]
    A17 --> A18[ISO 9283 Pose Repeatability Test]
    A18 --> A19[Generate Commissioning Report]
    A19 --> A20[Train Operators and Issue Certificates]
    A20 --> A21[Shift Start Up Checklist Daily]
    A21 --> A22[Ready for Production]
    A7 --> A4
    A2 --> A3
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Start-Up Safety

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Power Supply and Isolation Switch] --> P1[Lock Out Tag Out Verification]
    P1 --> P2[Main Contactor Energized]
    P2 --> P3[Robot Controller Boot]
    P3 --> P4[Safety PLC Initialization]
    P4 --> P5[Safety Configuration Signature Check]
    P5 -- Match Expected --> P6[Servo Power Enable Granted]
    P5 -- Mismatch --> P7[Safe State Hold and Alarm]
    P6 --> P8[Operator HMI Login and Mode Selection]
    P8 --> P9[Manual Reduced Speed Mode]
    P8 --> P10[Automatic Full Speed Mode]
    P9 --> P11[Enabling Device Must be Held]
    P10 --> P12[Safeguard Devices Active and Monitored]
    P11 --> P13[Motion Permitted]
    P12 --> P13
    P13 --> P14[Real Time Health Monitor]
    P14 -- Healthy --> P13
    P14 -- Fault Detected --> P15[Stop Category 0 or 1]
    P15 --> P16[Fault Logged and Ack Required]
    P16 --> P17[Safe Reset Procedure]
    P17 --> P8
```

## 4.3 Risk Graph for Start-Up Hazards (ISO 13849-1 Annex A)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Start Up Hazard Identified] --> R1{Severity S1 or S2}
    R1 -- S1 Light --> R2[F1 Low Exposure Possible]
    R1 -- S2 Serious or Fatal --> R3{Frequency F1 or F2}
    R3 -- F1 Rare --> R4{P2 Avoidance Possible}
    R3 -- F2 Frequent --> R5[Required Performance Level PLe]
    R4 -- Yes --> R6[Required PL a or b]
    R4 -- No --> R7[Required PL d]
    R2 --> R8[Required PL a]
    R5 --> PLE[SIL 3 Redundant Architecture]
    PLE --> P9[Dual Channel Safety Relay and Cross Monitored Contactors]
```

## 4.4 Periodic Test Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0{Time Since Last Test} --> T1[Less than 6 Months]
    T0 --> T2[6 to 12 Months]
    T0 --> T3[Greater than 12 Months]
    T1 --> T4[Visual Inspection and E Stop Press Test]
    T2 --> T5[Full Functional Test plus Brake Wear Check]
    T3 --> T6[Complete Re Commissioning plus Encoder Calibration]
    T4 --> T7[Log Result in Maintenance Book]
    T5 --> T7
    T6 --> T8[Generate New Commissioning Report]
    T8 --> T7
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Type Testing and Site Acceptance Testing (SAT) of an industrial robot. Mention the standard that governs each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Type Testing | Site Acceptance Testing (SAT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | OEM factory / lab | End-user facility |
| **Purpose** | Validate design against spec | Verify delivered unit is undamaged and matches spec |
| **Performed by** | Manufacturer | Integrator / end-user engineer |
| **Standard** | ISO 9283 (performance), manufacturer-specific | ISO 10218-2 (system integration) |
| **Frequency** | Once per design revision | Once per delivery |
| **Includes** | Environmental, EMC, endurance, IP rating | Visual, power-up, free-space motion, payload |

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners award 1 mark each for: location, purpose, and standard — so state them clearly in bullet form.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]**
**List any six start-up safety-related factors that must be verified before energizing a robot system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — list 6 of the following):**
1. Mechanical integrity check (bolt torque, backlash, oil).
2. Lock-Out Tag-Out (LOTO) procedure completed and signed.
3. Earthing resistance ≤ **0.1 Ω** (per IEC 60204-1).
4. Emergency stop devices tested and color-coded.
5. Workspace software limits configured and backed up.
6. Reduced teach-mode speed (≤ **250 mm/s**) verified.
7. Operator training certificates valid.
8. Risk assessment up-to-date for the current process.
9. No unresolved fault codes in controller log.

> [!TIP]
> Always mention the **standard reference** (e.g., "per IEC 60204-1" or "per ISO 10218-2") — KTU examiners award a bonus mark for standard citations.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat flowchart, describe the complete commissioning procedure of an industrial robot as per **ISO 10218-2**. Highlight at least eight mandatory checks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the safety distance formula $S = K(T_m + T_s) + C$ from first principles. A light curtain is to be installed in a robot cell. Given: approach speed $K = 2000$ mm/s, machine stopping time $T_m = 300$ ms, safety device response time $T_s = 25$ ms, intrusion distance $C = 850$ mm. Calculate the minimum safety distance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The flowchart (draw a clean labeled version in the answer script) should include these **eight mandatory checks** (1 mark each for naming + 1 mark for the connected logic flow):

1. **Pre-installation site survey** — pedestal, power, compressed air, floor flatness.
2. **Mechanical installation** — robot mounted per OEM drawing, torque values verified.
3. **Electrical hookup and earthing test** — resistance ≤ 0.1 Ω.
4. **Insulation resistance test** (megger at 500 V DC) — typically ≥ 1 MΩ.
5. **Safeguarding device installation** — light curtains, safety mats, interlocks, scanners.
6. **Software configuration and safety I/O mapping** — cross-checked against wiring diagram.
7. **Safety function verification** — E-stop (STOP 0 and STOP 1), reduced speed, soft limits.
8. **Performance test (ISO 9283)** — pose repeatability RP, pose accuracy AP.
9. **Free-space envelope sweep** at full speed and 100% payload.
10. **Commissioning report** signed and archived; **operator training** completed and certificates issued.

> [!NOTE]
> A 7-mark flowchart question needs the **flow direction correct** (1 mark), **boxes properly labeled** (3 marks), and **at least 8 checks enumerated with standard reference** (3 marks).

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Derivation (5 marks):** Show the steps from Section 3.1 of these notes — define the hazard zone, identify the two independent motions (operator and robot), set the no-touch condition $S/K \geq T_m + T_s$, solve for $S$, and add the intrusion constant $C$.

**Numerical calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= K \cdot (T_m + T_s) + C \\
&= 2000 \text{ mm/s} \cdot (0.300 \text{ s} + 0.025 \text{ s}) + 850 \text{ mm} \\
&= 2000 \cdot 0.325 + 850 \\
&= 650 + 850 \\
&= 1500 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The light curtain must be installed at least **1.5 metres** from the hazard boundary.

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the concept of **Performance Level (PL)** and **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** as per ISO 13849-1 and IEC 62061. State the five PL levels and the probability of dangerous failure per hour for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-DOF industrial robot's axis 2 has moment of inertia $J = 0.8$ kg·m², rotates at $\omega_s = 3$ rad/s, and the brake provides a constant torque $T_{brake} = 60$ N·m. Calculate the **stopping time** of this axis. If the system has a total response time $T_s = 45$ ms and the operator approach speed is $K = 1600$ mm/s with intrusion distance $C = 850$ mm, find the **minimum safety distance**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Concept (3 marks):** PL (per **ISO 13849-1**) categorizes the ability of safety-related parts of control systems to perform a safety function under foreseeable conditions. SIL (per **IEC 62061**) is the analogous metric for electrical/electronic/programmable electronic systems. Both describe the **probability of dangerous failure per hour (PFHd)** but use different architectural models.

**PL Table (4 marks — 1 mark for correct value, 1 mark for unit / description):**

| PL | PFHd (per hour) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **a** | $\geq 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$ | Simple, non-critical interlocks |
| **b** | $\geq 10^{-6}$ to $< 10^{-5}$ | Manual tool change |
| **c** | $\geq 10^{-7}$ to $< 10^{-6}$ | Auxiliary guarding |
| **d** | $\geq 10^{-8}$ to $< 10^{-7}$ | **Robot E-stop, light curtain (typical)** |
| **e** | $< 10^{-8}$ | High-risk, collaborative robot PFL |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For robot safety functions, the KTU expected answer is PL d minimum**, achieved through **Category 3 architecture** (dual-channel with cross-monitoring and single-fault tolerance).

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Stopping time of axis (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_r &= \dfrac{J \cdot \omega_s}{T_{brake}} \\
&= \dfrac{0.8 \cdot 3}{60} \\
&= \dfrac{2.4}{60} \\
&= 0.040 \text{ s} = 40 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Safety distance (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= K \cdot (T_m + T_s) + C
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $T_m$ is the total machine stopping time. For this problem, use $T_m = T_r = 40$ ms $= 0.040$ s (as given by the axis calculation), and $T_s = 45$ ms $= 0.045$ s is the additional safety system response.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 1600 \cdot (0.040 + 0.045) + 850 \\
&= 1600 \cdot 0.085 + 850 \\
&= 136 + 850 \\
&= 986 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Stopping time = **40 ms**, Minimum safety distance = **986 mm** (or approximately **1.0 m**).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert ms → s** before multiplying by $K$ (mm/s) is the #1 cause of full-mark loss. Write units at every step.
> 2. **Confusing $T_m$ and $T_s$**: $T_m$ is the **machine** (robot) stopping time, $T_s$ is the **safety device** (e.g., light curtain) response time. Both are added, not substituted.
> 3. **Dropping the $+ C$ term**: The intrusion distance is a non-negotiable safety margin; omitting it loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not citing the standard** in a Part A question: KTU board examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing standard references in safety topics.
> 5. **Confusing PL and SIL** in a comparison question: PL is for mechanical + electronic control subsystems (ISO 13849); SIL is for pure E/E/PE systems (IEC 62061). They are not interchangeable, though correlated.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Robot testing has 5 phases**: Type Test → SAT → Commissioning → Periodic → Re-Commissioning after modification.
- [ ] **Start-up safety factors** are 3-fold: **Hardware** (mechanical, electrical, E-stop), **Software** (limits, speed, I/O), **Procedural** (training, LOTO, PPE).
- [ ] **Master formula**: $S = K(T_m + T_s) + C$ — KTU's most-tested equation in this module.
- [ ] **Standard values to memorize**: $K = 1600$ mm/s (walk) or $2000$ mm/s (reach); $C = 850$ mm (default); Teach-mode speed ≤ $250$ mm/s; Earthing resistance ≤ $0.1 \text{ }\Omega$.
- [ ] **PL d** is the minimum acceptable Performance Level for robot E-stop circuits.
- [ ] **Stop categories**: STOP 0 (uncontrolled, immediate power removal) vs STOP 1 (controlled stop, then power removal).
- [ ] **Standards to remember**: ISO 10218-1, ISO 10218-2, ISO 13849-1, ISO 9283, ISO/TS 15066, IEC 60204-1.
- [ ] **MTBF** $= 1/\lambda$; **Reliability** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.
- [ ] **LOTO** (Lock-Out Tag-Out) is non-negotiable before any maintenance.
- [ ] **ISO 9283** defines the four key performance metrics: AP, RP, AT, RT.
- [ ] **Collaborative robots** require Power & Force Limiting (PFL) validation per ISO/TS 15066.
- [ ] **A commissioning report is a legal document** — sign, date, and archive it for the lifetime of the robot.
- [ ] **E-stop color code**: RED button on YELLOW background (per ISO 13850).
- [ ] **Reduced speed teach mode** must be enforced by hardware, not just software, to be PL d compliant.
- [ ] **Risk reassessment** is required after **any** of: end-effector change, payload change, workspace change, or near-miss incident.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Mark Maximizer:** On a 14-mark question, always structure your answer as **Definition → Standard → Formula/Method → Worked Solution → Safety/Engineering Insight**. KTU examiners allocate marks roughly as: 3 marks for definition, 3 marks for standard reference, 4 marks for derivation/calculation, 2 marks for safety comment, 2 marks for presentation. Following this template practically guarantees 12+ marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing Equipment and Procedures-Test Reports
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT831 - SAFETY AND RELIABILITY IN ROBOTIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Testing Equipment and Procedures-Test Reports

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Testing Equipment and Procedures** in the context of robotic systems refer to the systematic deployment of specialized instrumentation, fixtures, sensors, and standardized methodologies used to verify that a robot or robotic cell conforms to its specified functional, performance, and safety requirements prior to deployment, during commissioning, and throughout its operational lifecycle.

A **Test Report** is a formal, traceable document that records the objectives, conditions, methodologies, instruments used, observed results, and pass/fail conclusions of a test campaign, providing objective evidence of conformity with applicable standards such as **ISO 10218-1/-2**, **ISO/TS 15066**, **IEC 61508**, and **ISO 13849-1**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, the testing and documentation of robotic systems is a direct contributor to **CO4 (Design and conduct safety tests on robotic systems and interpret results)**. Test reports are not optional artefacts; they are **legal and engineering evidence** of due diligence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a brand-new commercial passenger aircraft. Before it ever carries a paying passenger, it is subjected to a rigorous sequence of tests on the ground, on the runway, and in the air. The pilot does not simply "fly it and see what happens." Instead:

- **Specialized tools** (pitot tubes, load cells, vibration sensors, black boxes) measure every critical parameter.
- **Standardized procedures** (checklists from the aviation regulator) are followed step-by-step.
- A **formal flight test report** is filed with the regulator, signed by the test pilot, the chief engineer, and the quality manager.

A robotic system is no different. A collaborative robot that works alongside humans must be **proven safe** through instruments (laser scanners, force-torque sensors, stop-time meters) and procedures (risk assessment re-validation, validation of safety functions, performance benchmarking). The test report is the **birth certificate of safety** for the robot.

## 1.3 Key Standardised Metrics and Constants

- **ISO 10218-1:2011** — Robots and robotic devices — Safety requirements for industrial robots.
- **ISO/TS 15066:2016** — Collaborative robots — Technical specification (pain thresholds, biomechanical limits).
- **IEC 61508** — Functional safety of electrical/electronic/programmable electronic safety-related systems (**SIL 1 to SIL 4**).
- **ISO 13849-1:2015** — Safety of machinery — Safety-related parts of control systems (**PL a to PL e**).
- **Stop Time** ($T_s$) — Maximum time between trigger of the protective device and cessation of hazardous motion.
- **Stop Distance** ($D_s$) — Distance traveled by the robot after the protective stop is triggered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Test Report:** A structured, signed, and dated engineering document that provides auditable evidence that a robotic system, sub-system, or safety function was tested under defined conditions using calibrated equipment, with results demonstrating compliance (or non-compliance) against pre-specified acceptance criteria.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Testing Equipment

Testing equipment in robotic systems can be broadly classified into the following categories:

### 2.1.1 Performance & Kinematic Test Equipment
- **3D Measurement Systems (CMM, Laser Trackers):** Used to verify repeatability, accuracy, and path following. Typical measurement uncertainty: **±0.05 mm**.
- **Dynamometers and Force-Torque Sensors:** Measure joint torques, payload handling, and clamping forces.
- **Encoders / Resolvers Verification Tools:** Validate positioning and feedback integrity.
- **Accelerometers and IMUs:** Capture dynamic vibration and end-effector oscillation.

### 2.1.2 Safety Test Equipment
- **Safety Laser Scanners (SLS):** Used to verify protective field geometry, response time, and detection capability.
- **Safety Mats and Pressure-Sensitive Devices:** Test floor-level detection zones.
- **Stop-Time Measurement Devices (STMD):** Measure the **stopping time** of the robot under worst-case conditions (max payload, max speed).
- **EMG / Biometric Measurement Systems:** For collaborative robot human-contact studies (per ISO/TS 15066).
- **Pendulum Impact Testing Apparatus:** Simulated mechanical impact tests on robot surfaces.

### 2.1.3 Environmental & EMC Test Equipment
- **Climate Chambers:** Test under temperature (-20 °C to +60 °C typical) and humidity cycles.
- **EMC Test Analyzers (spectrum analyzers, antennas, near-field probes):** Verify compliance with **IEC 61000** series.
- **IP Rating Test Rigs:** Dust and water ingress testing per **IEC 60529** (e.g., **IP54**, **IP65**).

### 2.1.4 Software & Functional Safety Test Tools
- **Logic Analyzers and Protocol Analyzers** (EtherCAT, PROFINET, CIP Safety).
- **FMEA / FTA Software Tools** (e.g., **SISTEMA**, **PAScal**) for calculating **PL (Performance Level)** and **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)**.
- **Fault Injection Test Harnesses** to validate diagnostic coverage (**DC**).

## 2.2 Testing Procedures — The KTU Structured Framework

Testing procedures for robotic systems follow a **V-Model** inspired approach, linking each design phase to a corresponding test phase.

### Step 1: Test Planning
- Define the **test objectives** mapped to system requirements.
- Identify applicable standards.
- Establish **acceptance criteria** quantitatively (e.g., *Stop time ≤ 250 ms*).

### Step 2: Risk Assessment Review
- Re-validate the **risk assessment** performed during design (per **ISO 12100**).
- Identify residual risks that need verification testing.

### Step 3: Test Case Development
- Develop **test cases** with: Test ID, Pre-conditions, Test Steps, Expected Results, Pass/Fail Criteria.
- Use techniques like **equivalence partitioning**, **boundary value analysis**, and **fault injection**.

### Step 4: Test Execution
- Execute using **calibrated equipment** with valid calibration certificates.
- Capture raw data, photographs, oscillograms, and environmental conditions.

### Step 5: Test Report Generation
- Document everything formally (Section 2.3).
- Hand over to the **Notified Body / Certification Authority** if third-party certification is required (e.g., **CE marking**).

### Step 6: Traceability Matrix Update
- Update the **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** to show every requirement has been verified.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Term | Symbol / Variable | Description | Unit | Application in Robotic Testing |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_s = v_{max} \cdot T_s + \dfrac{1}{2} a \cdot T_s^{2}$ | $D_s$ = Stop Distance, $v_{max}$ = max velocity, $a$ = deceleration, $T_s$ = stop time | Minimum safe distance calculation | m (metres) | Safeguarded space layout |
| 2 | $T_{total} = T_r + T_s + T_{inertia}$ | $T_{total}$ = Total response time, $T_r$ = reaction time of safety device, $T_s$ = stop time, $T_{inertia}$ = controller reaction | Determines overall safety response | s (seconds) | Safety laser scanner integration |
| 3 | $PL = f(\text{Category}, MTTF_d, DC_{avg}, \text{CCF})$ | $PL$ = Performance Level, $MTTF_d$ = Mean Time To Dangerous Failure, $DC_{avg}$ = Average Diagnostic Coverage, $CCF$ = Common Cause Failure | Per ISO 13849-1 | Discrete (a–e) | Safety circuit design verification |
| 4 | $PFH_d = \sum \lambda_{Du}$ | $PFH_d$ = Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour, $\lambda_{Du}$ = dangerous undetected failure rate | Per IEC 61508 | $\text{h}^{-1}$ | SIL calculation |
| 5 | $SFF = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{Du}}{\lambda_{total}}$ | $SFF$ = Safe Failure Fraction | Diagnostic + safe failure ratio | Dimensionless (0–1) | Subsystem SIL classification |
| 6 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $R(t)$ = Reliability, $\lambda$ = failure rate, $t$ = mission time | Exponential reliability model | Dimensionless | Predicting failure during life test |
| 7 | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $MTTF$ = Mean Time To Failure | Inverse of failure rate | h (hours) | Reliability verification |
| 8 | $v_{clamp,limit} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot E_{lim}}{m}}$ | $v$ = clamping velocity limit, $E_{lim}$ = biomechanical energy limit, $m$ = effective mass | Quasi-static contact (ISO/TS 15066) | m/s | Collaborative robot clamping test |
| 9 | $F_{static,limit}$ = constant per body region | — | Quasi-static contact pressure limit | N | Force pressure test |
| 10 | $T_{cal}$ ≤ 12 months | $T_{cal}$ = Calibration interval | Maximum period between recalibrations | months | Equipment calibration planning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Notation Convention:** Per the engine protocol, the vertical pipe symbol `$\vert$` is intentionally avoided inside table cells to preserve markdown table integrity. Use `\vert` or `\mid` only in standalone LaTeX blocks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Assembly Lines:** Stop-time and stop-distance tests are mandatory before every new model launch.
- **Medical Robotics (Surgical Robots):** Functional safety per IEC 61508 (SIL 3) and rigorous EMC testing.
- **Collaborative Robots in SME Manufacturing:** ISO/TS 15066 pain threshold testing is critical for human-robot coexistence.
- **AGV / AMR Warehousing:** Verification of safety laser scanner field-of-view, response time, and obstacle detection in cluttered environments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Minimum Safety Distance ($D_s$)

The minimum safety distance is the heart of safeguarding calculations and is derived from fundamental kinematics. The KTU board frequently tests this.

**Problem Context:** A robotic arm moves at a maximum velocity of $v_{max}$. When a safety mat is triggered, the system takes a total reaction time of $T_{total} = T_r + T_s$. What is the minimum distance at which the safety device must be placed from the hazard?

**Step 1 — Identify the kinematic phases.**

When the safety device triggers, the robot does not stop instantaneously. Two motion phases occur:
1. **Phase A — Reaction Phase:** The robot continues at $v_{max}$ for the duration $T_r$ (detector + controller delay).
2. **Phase B — Braking Phase:** The robot decelerates from $v_{max}$ to zero over time $T_s$.

**Step 2 — Compute distance traveled in Phase A.**

Since velocity is constant in this phase:

$$
D_A = v_{max} \cdot T_r
$$

**Step 3 — Compute distance traveled in Phase B (uniform deceleration).**

Average velocity during braking:

$$
v_{avg} = \dfrac{v_{max} + 0}{2} = \dfrac{v_{max}}{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
D_B = v_{avg} \cdot T_s = \dfrac{v_{max} \cdot T_s}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum both phases to obtain total stop distance.**

$$
D_s = D_A + D_B
$$

Substituting:

$$
D_s = v_{max} \cdot T_r + \dfrac{v_{max} \cdot T_s}{2}
$$

Factoring $v_{max}$:

$$
D_s = v_{max} \left( T_r + \dfrac{T_s}{2} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to the ISO 13855 standard form (full $T_s$, not $T_s/2$).**

ISO 13855 uses the conservative worst-case assumption that the robot may still be at $v_{max}$ throughout the entire response window $T_{total}$:

$$
D_s = v_{max} \cdot T_{total} = v_{max} \cdot (T_r + T_s)
$$

For a trapezoidal velocity profile (more realistic for industrial robots with constant acceleration/deceleration), the KTU-preferred form is:

$$
D_s = v_{max} \cdot T_r + \dfrac{v_{max}^{2}}{2 \cdot a}
$$

where $a$ is the maximum deceleration of the robot.

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example.**

Given:
- $v_{max} = 2.0 \text{ m/s}$
- $T_r = 0.020 \text{ s}$ (controller + safety mat reaction)
- $a = 5.0 \text{ m/s}^2$ (robot braking deceleration)

Compute the reaction phase distance:

$$
D_r = v_{max} \cdot T_r = 2.0 \times 0.020 = 0.040 \text{ m}
$$

Compute the braking distance:

$$
D_b = \dfrac{v_{max}^{2}}{2a} = \dfrac{(2.0)^{2}}{2 \times 5.0} = \dfrac{4.0}{10.0} = 0.400 \text{ m}
$$

Total minimum safety distance:

$$
D_s = D_r + D_b = 0.040 + 0.400 = 0.440 \text{ m}
$$

**Result:** The safety mat must be installed at least **440 mm** away from the hazardous zone, plus an additional safety margin per **ISO 13855** (typically 1 m/s × $T_{total}$ as a buffer).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Test Type to Equipment to Standard Mapping

| Test Objective | Standard Reference | Required Equipment | Pass Criterion | Failure Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stop time verification | ISO 10218-1 | Stop-time measurement device, encoder | $T_s \leq$ specified | Hazard zone miscalculation |
| Quasi-static contact force | ISO/TS 15066 | Force-torque sensor, biomechanical gauge | $F \leq F_{limit}$ per body region | Human injury risk |
| Transient contact energy | ISO/TS 15066 | Pendulum, high-speed camera, force sensor | $E \leq E_{limit}$ per body region | Bruising, fracture |
| EMC emissions | IEC 61000-6 / IEC 61000-4 | Spectrum analyzer, antennas, LISN | Below quasi-peak limits | EMI in adjacent equipment |
| Ingress protection | IEC 60529 | Dust chamber, water jet rig | Pass IPXX test (e.g., IP54) | Internal corrosion, shorts |
| Safety function response | ISO 13849-1 | Logic analyzer, STMD | PL achieved verified | Unsafe stop failure |
| Repeatability (pose) | ISO 9283 | CMM / laser tracker | $R \leq$ declared value | Process quality degradation |
| Diagnostic coverage | IEC 61508 | Fault injector, SISTEMA tool | $DC_{avg} \geq$ target | Hidden dangerous failures |
| Cable flexing | IEC 60204-1 | Flexing test rig | Pass $N$ cycles | Wiring failure, fire risk |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Robotic System Test Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Requirements Specification SRS] --> B[Risk Assessment ISO 12100]
    B --> C[Derived Safety Requirements]
    C --> D[Hardware Software Design]
    D --> E[Test Plan Development]
    E --> F[Test Case Library]
    F --> G[Calibration of Test Equipment]
    G --> H[Factory Acceptance Test FAT]
    H --> I[Site Acceptance Test SAT]
    I --> J[Validation of Safety Functions]
    J --> K[Test Report Generation]
    K --> L[Requirements Traceability Matrix RTM]
    L --> M[Certification Authority Notified Body]
    M --> N[CE Marking / Regulatory Sign Off]
    N --> O[Commissioning and Hand Over]
    O --> P[Periodic Re Test in Service]
    P --> Q[Continuous Reliability Monitoring]
    Q --> R[Field Failure Data Analysis]
    R --> S[Design Feedback Loop]
    S --> A
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Stop-Time Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Robot[Robot Arm under test] --> Encoder[Incremental Encoder on Joint]
    Encoder --> DAQ[Data Acquisition System high speed]
    Trigger[Safety Mat or Light Curtain] --> DAQ
    DAQ --> STMD[Stop Time Measurement Device]
    STMD --> Compute[T_s and D_s Calculation Module]
    Compute --> Report[Test Report Generator]
    Report --> Cert[Sign Off by Test Engineer and Safety Officer]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Test Report Document Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[1. Cover Page Project Details] --> R2[2. Document Control Revision History]
    R2 --> R3[3. Scope of Testing]
    R3 --> R4[4. Reference Standards and Documents]
    R4 --> R5[5. Test Equipment List with Calibration IDs]
    R5 --> R6[6. Test Conditions Environment Setup]
    R6 --> R7[7. Test Cases with Pass Fail Status]
    R7 --> R8[8. Raw Data and Observations]
    R8 --> R9[9. Photographs and Oscillograms]
    R9 --> R10[10. Non Conformities and Resolutions]
    R10 --> R11[11. Conclusions and Recommendations]
    R11 --> R12[12. Signatures of Test Team and Approver]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Node Identifier Rule:** All node IDs above use purely alphanumeric labels (e.g., `Robot`, `Encoder`, `STMD`, `R1`–`R12`) prefixed with letters, with raw uppercase text inside quoted labels — strictly avoiding reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style` as standalone node IDs.

## 4.4 Test Equipment Classification Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TE[Testing Equipment in Robotics] --> P1[Performance Equipment]
    TE --> P2[Safety Equipment]
    TE --> P3[Environmental Equipment]
    TE --> P4[Software Tools]
    P1 --> P1a[CMM and Laser Trackers]
    P1 --> P1b[Force Torque Sensors]
    P1 --> P1c[Accelerometers IMU]
    P2 --> P2a[Safety Laser Scanners]
    P2 --> P2b[Stop Time Meters]
    P2 --> P2c[Biometric Measurement Tools]
    P2 --> P2d[Pendulum Impact Rig]
    P3 --> P3a[Climate Chamber]
    P3 --> P3b[EMC Analyzer]
    P3 --> P3c[IP Test Rig]
    P4 --> P4a[SISTEMA PAScal]
    P4 --> P4b[Logic Analyzer]
    P4 --> P4c[Fault Injection Harness]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a Test Report in the context of robotic systems. List any four essential elements that must be present in a valid test report.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Test Report** is a formal engineering document providing objective, traceable evidence that a robotic system, sub-system, or safety function has been tested under defined conditions using calibrated equipment, with results evaluated against pre-specified acceptance criteria.

**Four essential elements:**
1. **Test Equipment List** with valid calibration certificates and traceability IDs.
2. **Test Conditions and Environment** (temperature, humidity, supply voltage).
3. **Test Results** — raw data, observations, oscillograms, and pass/fail status against acceptance criteria.
4. **Signatures** of the test engineer, technical reviewer, and approving authority with date.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation Tip (KTU Valuation):** 1 mark for the definition, ½ mark × 4 = 2 marks for the elements. Always name the standards (ISO 10218, IEC 61508) wherever applicable to earn extra credit.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Briefly explain the function of a Stop-Time Measurement Device (STMD) and state the ISO standard that mandates stop time verification.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Stop-Time Measurement Device (STMD)** is a specialized test instrument used to measure the time elapsed between the actuation of a protective device (e.g., safety mat, light curtain) and the complete cessation of all hazardous robot motion.

**Standard Mandating Verification:** **ISO 10218-1:2011** (Industrial robots — Safety requirements) along with **ISO 13855:2010** (Positioning of safeguards with respect to the approach speeds of parts of the human body).

**Why it matters:** Stop time directly determines the **minimum safety distance** $D_s$ for the safeguarded space. An inaccurate $T_s$ value can lead to a hazardous zone being too small, exposing operators to risk.

> [!TIP]
> Always state the units (e.g., milliseconds) and the acceptance threshold to maximize your marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### (a) Describe in detail the various categories of testing equipment used for verifying safety and performance of robotic systems. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The testing equipment can be systematically classified into four categories:

**(i) Performance and Kinematic Equipment (2 marks)**
- **Coordinate Measuring Machines (CMM)** and **Laser Trackers** — verify pose accuracy and repeatability per **ISO 9283**.
- **Force-Torque Sensors** — measure joint loads and end-effector forces.
- **Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs)** and **accelerometers** — capture dynamic vibrations.
- **Encoders/Resolvers** — verify positional feedback integrity.

**(ii) Safety Functional Equipment (2 marks)**
- **Safety Laser Scanners (SLS)** — validate protective field geometry, response time, and detection capability.
- **Stop-Time Measurement Devices (STMD)** — measure the worst-case stop time $T_s$.
- **Pendulum Impact Test Apparatus** — for collaborative robot surface impact testing.
- **Biometric measurement systems** (EMG, pressure sensors) — for ISO/TS 15066 human-contact studies.

**(iii) Environmental and EMC Equipment (1½ marks)**
- **Climate Chambers** — temperature/humidity endurance testing.
- **EMC Analyzers** (spectrum analyzers, antennas, LISN) — per IEC 61000 series.
- **IP Test Rigs** — dust and water ingress testing per IEC 60529.

**(iv) Software and Functional Safety Tools (1½ marks)**
- **SISTEMA / PAScal** — PL and SIL calculation per ISO 13849-1 and IEC 61508.
- **Logic and Protocol Analyzers** (EtherCAT, CIP Safety, PROFINET) — verify safety bus integrity.
- **Fault Injection Harnesses** — verify diagnostic coverage ($DC_{avg}$).

#### (b) Explain the structure and key sections of a professional Test Report for a robotic system. Why is traceability important? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

A professional Test Report for a robotic system typically contains the following sections (1 mark each, with depth explanations):

| Section | Content |
|---|---|
| **Cover Page** | Project name, document ID, revision, date, classification. |
| **Document Control** | Revision history, distribution list. |
| **Scope of Testing** | What was tested, what was excluded. |
| **Reference Standards** | ISO 10218, ISO 13849, IEC 61508, etc. |
| **Test Equipment List** | Make, model, serial number, calibration ID, calibration validity date. |
| **Test Conditions** | Environmental, electrical, mechanical setup. |
| **Test Cases & Results** | Test ID, procedure, expected vs. actual, pass/fail. |
| **Raw Data & Evidence** | Oscillograms, photos, data logs. |
| **Non-Conformities** | Deviations, root cause, corrective action. |
| **Conclusion & Sign-off** | Overall verdict, signatures, date. |

**Why Traceability is Important (1 mark):**
- Enables **audit** by regulatory bodies and certification authorities.
- Provides **legal evidence** in case of accidents or litigation.
- Supports **continuous improvement** by linking test results to design changes.
- Required for **CE marking**, **NRTL certification** (UL, TÜV), and ISO 9001 quality management.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Part B 14-mark Answers:**
> - Do **NOT** write only bullet points without explanation. KTU 2024 valuation keys demand **one or two lines of justification per bullet**.
> - For numerical problems, always show **formula → substitution → result → unit**.
> - **Common trap:** Students often confuse **Performance Level (PL)** with **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**. PL is per **ISO 13849-1** (a to e); SIL is per **IEC 61508** (1 to 4). Use the correct standard for the correct context.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, describe the test setup for measuring the stop time of a robotic arm. Derive the expression for minimum safety distance. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.2** of these notes. Key elements:
- **Robot under test** with **incremental encoder** mounted on a critical joint.
- **Triggering device** (safety mat, light curtain, or emergency stop button).
- **Data Acquisition (DAQ)** system synchronized to both encoder and trigger.
- **STMD** computes $T_s$ from trigger edge to encoder velocity-zero.
- **Computation module** for $D_s$.

**Derivation of Minimum Safety Distance (4 marks):**

The total stop time is the sum of:
$$
T_{total} = T_r + T_s
$$

where $T_r$ is the safety device + controller reaction time, and $T_s$ is the mechanical braking time.

During the reaction phase, the robot travels:
$$
D_1 = v_{max} \cdot T_r
$$

During the braking phase (assuming uniform deceleration $a$):
$$
D_2 = \dfrac{v_{max}^{2}}{2a}
$$

Total minimum safety distance:
$$
D_s = D_1 + D_2 = v_{max} \cdot T_r + \dfrac{v_{max}^{2}}{2a}
$$

**Numerical Substitution Example (must include):**
- $v_{max} = 1.5$ m/s, $T_r = 0.025$ s, $a = 4$ m/s²
- $D_1 = 1.5 \times 0.025 = 0.0375$ m
- $D_2 = (1.5)² / (2 \times 4) = 2.25 / 8 = 0.28125$ m
- $D_s = 0.31875$ m ≈ **318.75 mm**

#### (b) Discuss the test procedures for verifying collaborative robot safety per ISO/TS 15066. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Test Procedures (7 marks distributed as below):**

**(i) Quasi-Static Contact Test (2 marks)**
- A force-measuring probe is pressed against the robot surface at a controlled rate (≤ 5 mm/s).
- The clamping force must not exceed the **biomechanical limit** for the relevant body region (e.g., 140 N for the hand, per ISO/TS 15066 Table A.1).
- Equipment: Force-torque sensor, calibrated probe, fixture.

**(ii) Transient Contact Test (2 marks)**
- A pendulum impactor is released against the moving robot surface.
- The transient contact energy must remain below the limit:
  $$
  E_{lim} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \leq E_{max,body}
  $$
- Equipment: Pendulum rig, high-speed camera, accelerometer.

**(iii) Stop-Time and Stop-Distance Verification (1½ marks)**
- Performed at maximum payload, maximum speed, worst-case extension.
- Acceptance: $T_s$ must allow $D_s$ to be kept within the safeguarded space.

**(iv) Power and Force Limiting (PFL) Verification (1½ marks)**
- Verify that on contact, the robot reduces force/velocity within prescribed limits.
- Use Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS) and Speed-and-Separation Monitoring (SSM) tests.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Question B (a):**
> Many students lose 2–3 marks by:
> - Not drawing a **labeled block diagram** with arrows (KTU awards 3 marks purely for the diagram).
> - Forgetting to mention **encoder mounting** and **DAQ synchronization**.
> - Omitting the **numerical example** — KTU always rewards a worked-out numerical in 14-mark questions.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Test Report** is the **legal and engineering evidence** of conformity; it is non-negotiable for certification.
- ✅ Always reference **calibrated equipment** with valid calibration certificates — uncalibrated test data is invalid.
- ✅ **Stop Time ($T_s$)** is the foundation of all safeguarding distance calculations: $D_s = v_{max} T_r + \dfrac{v_{max}^{2}}{2a}$.
- ✅ Standards triad to remember: **ISO 10218 (Industrial Robots)**, **ISO 13849 (PL)**, **IEC 61508 (SIL)**.
- ✅ **ISO/TS 15066** governs collaborative robots — quasi-static and transient contact tests are mandatory.
- ✅ Test report sections: **Cover → Scope → Standards → Equipment → Conditions → Results → Evidence → Non-Conformities → Conclusion → Sign-off**.
- ✅ Traceability Matrix (RTM) links every **requirement → test case → test result** — it is the auditor's first checkpoint.
- ✅ **EMC testing** (IEC 61000 series) and **IP rating testing** (IEC 60529) are environmental compliance essentials.
- ✅ Functional safety verification demands **fault injection** to validate **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)**.
- ✅ The **V-Model** principle applies — every design phase has a corresponding test phase.
- ✅ KTU 2024 Scheme mandates alignment to **NEP 2020** outcomes; emphasize **CO4** (testing and interpreting safety results) in your answers.
- ✅ For numerical problems, follow the strict pattern: **Given → Formula → Substitution → Result → Unit → Interpretation**.
